SALT LAKE CITY,	-	OCTOBER 1, 1886.


OCTOBER CONFERNECE.
	The Fifty-sixth Semi-Annual Conference of the
Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints con-
vened Wednesday, October 6th, in the new Stake
House at Coalville, Apostle Franklin D. Richards
presiding. Of the authorities of the Church there
were present, Patriarch John Smith, and four of the
Quorum of the Apostles. The services were of
an exalted character throughout, and the Holy
Spirit seemed to be poured out in rich abundance
upon those who took part in the services.  An
Epistle from the First Presidency was read during
the Conference by Elder H. M. Wells. The
Epistle is published in full in this number of the
paper. It is encouraging to this people, who are
so cruelly deprived of the counsel and instruction
of the President of the Church and his Counsel-
ors, to have from time to time official Epistles
containing definite advice, and to know that they
are still watched over and guided under the in-


spiration of the Almighty by His servants, who
hold the keys to administer unto the people of
His Church here upon the earth. It will be a 
great comfort and blessing, not only to those who
are here in Zion, but to thousands afar off, to read
the words of divine inspiration, though they have
not the privilege of hearing the voices of those to
whom they were wont to listen in times that are
past—the days of peace and prosperity.
	The Conference was numerously attended, and
discourses touching upon all general topics, and
exhorting to lives of purity and holiness, were de-
livered by members of the Quorum of the Apos-
tles and other leading men; of the Apostles, who
addressed the people, there were F. D. Richards,
John Henry Smith, Heber J. Grant and John W.
Taylor. Several Presidents of Stakes represented
their fields of labor, and altogether the meetings
were interesting, and no doubt the good teaching
will have a beneficial effect in its results upon the
the Saints.
	It is certainly a time of solemnity and supplica-
tion,  and there is the greatest need of the Holy
Spirit in the hearts and homes of this people.
Great events are close at hand; of this fact there
can be no doubt in the minds of any who are
watching the signs of the times.


SISTER KING’S FUNERAL.

	The funeral obsequies over the remains of Sis-
ter Hannah T. King took place on Tuesday, the
28th ult.,in the Thirteenth Ward Assembly Rooms,
Bishop Millen Atwood presiding. Besides the 
Bishop and his Counselor, Nelson Empey, there
were present on the stand, President Horace S.
Eldredge, Bishops John Tingey and Orson F.
Whitney, Elders Charles W. Stayner, George
Goddard, Samuel Neslen, George B. Wallace,
Joseph W. Johnson and Robert F. Neslen
	The large gathering of the friends of the de-
ceased who were present, showed the great esteem
in which she was held in the community.
	The services commenced by the choir singing:
			"Unveil thy bosom, faithful tomb."
The singers were Mrs. Agnes Olsen Thomas, 
Miss Louie Wells, Mathoni Pratt and J. D.
Spencer, accompanied on the organ by C. S.
Burton.
	Elder Charles W, Stayner then offered prayer.
	The Choir sang:
			"Rest for the weary soul."
		ELDER GEORGE B. WALLACE
Addressed the congregation. He eulogized Sister
King as a faithful and noble minded member of
the Church; related some of his early experiences
as a missionary in the part of England where she
resided; and described the pleasant home and
surroundings which she forsook to gather with
the Saints in this land. He said that he had
known many noble women, but he never met but
one Hannah T. King. His remarks were very
consolatory to the bereaved, and brought many
kind reminiscences of Sister King's early days
to the minds of the numerous friends present.
	The following remarks were then made by 
		BISHOP O. F. WHITNEY:
“It has fallen to my lot, by selection and ap-
pointment, to be one of the speakers at the funeral
of Sister King. There may be many here who 
have known her longer, and known her better
than I have, but there are none, I am certain, who 
respected her more, or valued her friendship more
highly. Brother Wallace has dwelt more particu-
larly upon the incidents of her life, the temporal
phase of her career, and portrayed the sacrifices
she made for the Gospel's sake. I desire, also, 
to add my tribute to her memory, as the Holy
Spirit shall give me utterance.
	It is good to hear the life story of such a woman
as Sister King. Possessed of a refined soul, a
cultivated mind, and surrounded by the comforts

[column 2]
and luxuries of life; with friends and associates
who loved to minister to her wants; an intimate
friend of one of England's poetesses, the cele-
brated Eliza Cook, and numbering among her ac-
quaintances many in the upper walks of society
in her native England; yet, when the Gospel
found her she did not hesitate to relinquish all
and follow her Lord and Master through the
world. She had a noble spirit, and this, without
doubt, was the noblest act of her life. I am told
 that, to this day, it is a matter of astonishment to
her friends in England that she should ever have
done as she did, forsaken all, and united herself
with so despised a people as the Latter-day Saints.
With my knowledge of her character, and the
loftiness of her soul, it would have been to me a
matter of astonishment had she done otherwise.
	She was naturally a proud woman, but her pride,
which was doubtless the result of conscious recti-
tude, respectability, and the traditionary aristo-
cratic sentiment of her native land, was not of
that pernicious kind which prompts so many to
reject the Gospel because of its unpopularity and
its humble origin, and trample on the prec-
ious pearl whose value they do not compre-
hend. She was not ashamed to stoop, for she
stooped to conquer; she saw the jewel glittering
in the dust at her feet, and was not ashamed to 
pick it up, and wear it. The worldly-minded
do not understand the secret motive of such
an action. And here is the real reason for as-
tonishment. They over-look the prize; their
gaze is riveted upon the sky, or on the distant
horizon, and they ignore and trample on the
treasure shining below; like the proud and haughty
Pharisees, who were looking for the Messiah, but
expected Him as a mighty conqueror who would
throw off the Roman yoke and restore at that 
time the temporal power to Israel; they were not
prepared to receive the humble Nazarene, the
man of sorrows, acquainted with grief, and so
rejected their Redeemer and spiritual deliverer, to
their condemnation.
	Thus is the pride of the world ever tested by 
the Truth. Our tests are many and varied; the
trials being suited to our different natures and
calculated to bear upon our weakest parts. To
the young man who came to the Savior and asked
what good thing he should do to obtain eternal
life, the answer was—‘keep the commandments,’
which he claimed to have done from his youth up;
and then, to sell all he had and give to the poor,
and follow the Lord of life and glory; a King in
heaven, a peasant on earth, who was poorer than
the fowls of the air or the beasts of the field, hav
ing not 'where to lay his head.' A similar test
came to our beloved Sister, Hannah T. King, but 
unlike the young man who 'went away sorrowful'
because ‘he had great possessions,’ she made the
sacrifice and laid hold upon eternal life, the great-
est gift of God, in preference to the good things
of this earth. It is not the possession of wealth,
but the love of it, that is sinful. The worship of
any idol we are called to sacrifice, makes it hard
to enter the kingdom of heaven.
	I am told that her father, who was a gentleman,
and a fine specimen of physical manhood, when
he found that he could not dissuade her from her
purpose, sat down and gradually died of a broken
heart. ‘How cruel,'—some might say,—‘surely it
cannot be a message from God that would have
such results.' Indeed! Why, then, did the Son of
God say: 'Think not I come to bring peace, but
a sword?' Why did the Prophet say, in reference
to the gathering of Israel in the last days: 'I
will take you one of a city, and two of a famly,
and I will bring you to Zion?' The word of the 
Lord had gone forth, and she could not restrain
herself. She was a child of Israel, a daughter of
Abraham; her veins tingled with magic blood;
her heart responded to the call and she listened to
the voice of the good Shepherd. The blood of
Israel was in her veins—that blood which God


[Column 1]
has scattered over the earth and with it ‘sprinkled
many nations’—and when He lifts the gates and
bids it return, it as naturally seeks its Source and
Reservoir, as the raindrops that fall upon the hills
trickle back to the ocean whence they came.
This was why Sister King became a “Mormon,”
as it is called. She had the nobility, the great-
ness of soul, to sacrifice the earthly for the heaven-
ly, the temporal for the spiritual.
	But, did not her father object, and did not
Christ say, with Moses, ‘Honor thy father and
thy mother?’ Yes; and she did honor her Father
and her Mother—in heaven. What are the claims
of earthly parents upon us, compared to those of
our heavenly Parents? Besides, it is an individual
responsibility—this question of the soul’s salva-
tion, and our noble sister, no less than her father,
had a soul to save and an individual work of
salvation to perform. How could she have done
otherwise, and been true to her conscience—the
voice of God in the human heart? Furthermore,
it will yet be seen that she truly honored, even her
earthly father, in disobeying his behests. By the
course she took she became a savior to her father’s
house—one of ‘the salt of the earth.’ How could
she ever have, ministered for their salvation in the
Temple of God, had she not taken the step she
did. We must first save ourselves before we at-
tempt to save others; we must plant our own feet
upon the rock, ere we reach out to rescue others
from the floods and quicksands.
	The test is ever between the temporal and the
spiritual. In one scale is placed father, mother,
wife, children, home, country and possessions; in
the other the unspeakable riches of eternal life.
He or she who refuses to make the sacrifice, even
if, like Abraham, they are required to offer an
only son, is unworthy of the kingdom of God.
They who forsake all and follow the Master are
promised an hundred-fold in this life and in the
world to come life everlasting.	
	But enough of sacrifices. What sacrifice can
we make that is at all commensurate with the
Great Sacrifice made for us? How our little trials
pale into insignificance beside those of our blessed
Redeemer, and what mention are they worthy of
in the light of the glories and rewards to come?
The real sacrifice required of us is that of a
broken heart and a contrite spirit. God cares
not for gold and silver, nor is the smell of the
blood of goats and bulls pleasing unto Him. It
is a spiritual sacrifice that He demands, and when
that is made the temporal will follow easily. The
battle is won when the spirit has humbled itself
and been purified; the flesh must succumb as a
matter of course. It is the acceptable offering of
a broken heart and a contrite spirit, faith and hu-
mility, that will redeem Zion and work out for her
children a full and complete salvation in the pres-
ence of our Father and God.
	I might enlarge, if time would permit, upon the
glories of the future. I might dilate upon the
celestial, the terrestrial, and the telestial kingdoms,
and depict the great reward which our beloved
sister has attained, through her humility and in
tegrity in embracing the Gospel and enduring faith-
ful unto the end. I might show also, in the same
connection, the grandeur of this religion, stigma-
tized by the world as “Mormonism,” which pro-
vides for the salvation of all men according to
their merits, even of those who curse it and trample
on its advocates; and possibly let more light upon
the mystery—although it is no mystery to Latter-
day Saints—why a noble spirit, a large soul, with
lofty aims and broad sympathies, should be at-
tracted naturally to a saving plan so glorious, far-
reaching and sublime as “Mormonism.” But it
would not be necessary. Like cleaves to like; af-
finities will coalesce. The principle is an eternal 
one.
	May the God of mercy bless all who mourn,
and comfort the hearts of the relatives and friends 
of Sister King, and move upon them to emulate her

[Column 2]
virtues, to follow in her footsteps as she followed 
Christ's, and, becoming like Him, be worthy to 
meet Him, and mingle eternally with the righteous 
who have gone before. Amen." 
	Bishop Atwood gave some final instructions in 
regard to the casket and the remains, and 
	The choir sang: 
			"Nearer, my God, to Thee."
	Elder Joseph W. Johnson, who baptized Sister 
King into the Church, pronounced the benedic-
tion.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿A PRAYER.

Mighty God of earth and ocean,
	Hear us from Thy blest abode;
While we in faith, in deep devotion,
	Praise our great and gracious God;
Help us humbly, we implore Thee,
	Countless are Thy mercies here;
Mighty Prince of peace and glory!
	Bless our Prophet and our Seer.

We our shepherd are bewailing, 
	In his exile from the flock,
Let thy power, oh, God! prevailing,
	Plant him still on safety's rock;
Who for freedom now would barter
	Purest gold, will still remain:
Oh! to see our living martyr,
	And to hear his voice again.

Every gift and every blessing 
	Comes, O Lord, from Thee above;
Life and liberty possessing,
	We the righteous giver own;
And with earnest supplications,
	Now we bow in humble prayer;
Great and mighty God of nations, 
	Shield our Prophet and our Seer.

Let Thine angels hover near him,
	Fill him with prophetic fire;
Send Thy minstrels forth to cheer him
	With the touch of seraph's lyre; 
Bless his bed and soothe his pillow,
	Charge his sentinels sincere;
Toss'd on persecution’s billow,
	Guard our Prophet and our Seer.

While Thy humble interceders
	Praise Thy name for mercies past, 
Grant to our beloved leaders
	Thy protection to the last. 
Let Thy Zion, now before Thee, 
	In her destined freedom stand;
Lord of liberty and glory,
	Sweep the tyrants from our land. 

Break their chain from Zion’s noble, 
	Save Thy servant from their scorn,
Bare Thine arm in days of trouble,
	Bid Thy children cease to mourn;
Then to Thee, with warm devotion,
	Shall our hearts and voices sing;
Honor be to Zion's chieftain,
	Glory be to Zion's King.

							J. PATON.


[Column 1]

OLYMPIA MORATA.

A LEARNED ITALIAN LADY OE THE
SIXTEENTH CENTURY.

	A noteworthy instance of a woman who
combined the rarest intellectual gifts with
every domestic virtue, was the subject of this
brief notice, Olympia Morata, whose short and
brilliant career extended over the period be-
tween 1526 and 1555. She was born in Fer-
rara in the halcyon days, of the Renaissance,
when that city was the resort of all the great-
est scholars and writers of the age; for the
Este family were, after the Medici, the most
enlightened and munificent patrons of art and
learning in Italy.
	The enthusiasm for the revival of the Greek
culture was inimical to clerical instruction,and

[Column 2]

the education of youth, hitherto in the hands
of ecclesiastics, now passed from their control
to lay teachers. In a burst of Liberalism
consequent on this emancipation from priestly
control, men began to extend the benefits of
their learning to their daughters as well as to
their sons, and the girls studied under the
same tutors as their brothers. There was
nothing remarkable then, in the learned Pro-
fessor Morata's imparting to his daughter all
the knowledge of which he was master, but
there was something very remarkable in the
avidity with which the child drank it all in.
Olympia was lovely as well as gifted, and the
elderly gentlemen, mostly famous scholars,
who frequented her father's house, were en-
chanted with the precocious little beauty "The
language of the Muses was her earliest, mother
tongue," said one of them in describing her.
When Olympia was seven years old, her
father had to remove with his wife and five
children to Venice, or wherever he could get
employment, for he was not permitted to dwell
in Ferrara having fallen under the displeasure
of the Duke. In those glorious days when art
and learning were nursed and fostered in the
bosom of royalty, the cultivators and dispensers
of them had much to bear, and the position of
the proudest genius amongst them was but a
gilded slavery. The Dukes of Ferrara were
perhaps, the most liberal of these princely
patrons, who loved to shine with the borrowed
light of genius attracted to their courts.
	After six years of exile, the banished pro-
fessor was permitted to return to his native
city, and was re-instated in royal favor. He
was appointed tutor to the Duke's young
brothers, and also gave lessons to the sovereign's
own little daughter, Anna, a child of eight
years old. Morata was a poor scholar without
other patrimony than his brains, and it cost
him not a little effort to educate his peerless
daughter as he wished, for he had to work for
money, and she had to attend to household
affairs. With her passionate love of books
this was a trial, and we can imagine with what
joy the parents and the girl received an invita-
tion from the Duchess to reside in the palace
and share the instruction of the little Princess
Anna. Olympia was now past thirteen, and 
so advanced in her education that she excited
the wonder of everybody. The Duchess
thought her companionship would be very
beneficial to the child, and she was received
with the greatest kindness and consideration.
Instead of the luxury, learning was now the
business of her life.  In a Greek poem, trans-
lated by Mr. Trollope, she says:

"And I, though woman, womanly gear have left,
Distaff and thread, and workbasket and weft!
The Muses haunts, Parnassus' flowery hill,
These have been all my joys, these shall be still."

	The Duke Hercules II., had married Renèe,
a daughter of France. She was a woman of
unattractive appearance, but of great intel-
lectual power and a taste for theology, which
got her into trouble. Her mental qualities
were inherited by two of her daughters, who
were counted among the greatest scholars of
their time, Lucretia, Duchess of Urbino, and
Leonora, the object of Tasso's most unhappy
love. Her children were still in their infancy
when she became imbued with the doctrines of
a new faith just then preached by Calvin in
Geneva.
	Among the learned men admitted to her
private intercourse was a foreign traveller, 
called M. Charles d'Espeville. The Duke had
secret information from the papal spies, who
tormented him continually on the subject of
his wife's orthodoxy, that this gentleman was
no other than the arch-heretic, John Calvin
himself; and he entered the apartment of the
Duchess with the police, who seized the
traveller and another, to carry them off to
prison; but Renèe planned their escape on the


[Column 1]

﻿road. Henceforth she had to suffer persecution
for her opinions. At one time she was de- 
prived of her children and shut up in her
apartments, but she made her submission and
was pardoned for a time.
	We may be sure that a court presided over by
this lady could never become frivolous, and her
young protegee studied constantly and assidu-
ously.  But all the same she was in much
danger of being spoiled—now in the full
bloom of her beautiful girlhood, flattered, ad-
mired, adored by poets, scholars, soldiers,
princes, by every man who came within reach
of her fascination. Her marvelous accomplish-
ments stirred even the old dons into extrava-
gant admiration. "We used to hear her de-
claiming in Latin, improvising in Greek, ex-
plaining the paradoxes of the greatest orators,
and answering all the questions addressed to
her," writes one enthusiastic admirer. She
was just sixteen when she delivered lectures on
these abstruse themes. But this was not such
an extraordinary spectacle in the Italy of the
6th century as it would be now.
	It must not be imagined that Olympia sought
only the approbation of men. She formed
tender friendships with the noblest and best
young persons of her own sex-friendships
which were tried in adversity and lasted for
life. Her fellow pupil, the Princess Anna, was
one of these friends, also Princess Lavinia
Della Rovere. Another close companion of
her youth was Francesca Bucyronia, whose lot
resembled that of Olympia, as she, too, became
converted to the reformed religion, married a
German, and went with him to his own coun-
try.
	Olympia as long as she remained in the
Ducal palace continued to be like a pagan
goddess, receiving men's homage as her due,
but caring for no one in particular; still un-
touched by the deep, religious sentiment which
was making itself felt in the innermost circle
of the Duchess's court. She wrote thus to a
friend afterwards:
				
	"Had I remained longer at court it would have been all
over with me and my salvation; for never while I remained
there could I attain the knowledge of anything lofty or
divine, or read the books of either Testament."

	Olympia was called home to attend her
father's sick bed, and her family just then 
seem to have fallen under the suspicion of
heresy, for they were left to struggle with sick-
ness and poverty without aid from the sove-
reigns; and after the death of the professor his
daughter was not received back into the
palace.
	The Duke was under the guidance of his
clerical advisers, who threatened papal en-
croachments on his possessions, and the
Duchess was simply afraid to show any favor
to a family suspected of a change of faith, be-
ing herself under constant surveillance. Olym-
pia, in this season of adversity, which must
have presented a sad contrast to the happy,
joyous life she had left, behaved like a brave
devoted daughter and sister, and returned to
the distaff and the needle, the brewing and
baking which she thought she had abandoned 
for ever. She became strongly impressed by
the doctrines of the reformers, and from this
time henceforth she was a profoundly religious
woman. While Olympia was still at the court,
she and other liberal minded young persons
had felt their interest excited in the fate of a 
young man called Fannio, then in prison by
order of the Inquisition, which had condemned
him as a heretic. The entreaties of his young
wife had induced him to recant to save himself
from the stake, but he repented of his recanta-
tion, and now declared his faith openly. Dur-
ing his prolonged imprisonment, Olympia and
some other friends visited him, and no doubt
the converse of a man about to die the death
of a martyr was such as to make a lasting im-
pression on her mind. There was another in-

[Column 2]

fluence at work, which had its effect in develop-
ing the gay thoughtless girl into a serious,
noble woman. When she was in the zenith of
her glory, a young German, Grünthler,
studying at the university of Ferrara, was
captivated by her charms, and ventured to 
make known his feelings to her. He was cold-
ly rejected, but this did not prevent the disin-
terested lover from renewing his suit when the 
lady of his choice was sharing her poor family's
misery and disgrace.  He had now his degree
of doctor of medicine, and was acknowledged
to be very clever and well-informed. Olympia's
eyes were opened to his worth, and she returned
his love with a passionate fervor, as is proved
by her letters to him after their marriage,
when he was obliged to go to Germany to seek
a home for his bride. When at last he found
a temporary resting place, he returned for her,
and took with him also his wife's little brother
Emilio.
	After some months uncertainty the Grünth-
lers settled down in the native town of the
doctor, where they enjoyed freedom to worship
God in their own way, and led a calm, happy,
peaceful life, the lady attending to her domes-
tic affairs, as she had but one maid servant,and
teaching her little brother and the child of her
friend, Fraucesa, who had been sent to her for 
instruction. In the evenings, when the doc-
tor's professional duties were over, two or three
friends would drop in for a little music,in which
Grünthler was skilled, when they often sang
hymns translated or composed in Greek by
Olympia.
	The civil religious wars that then raged in
Germany caused Schweinfurth to undergo a
cruel siege of fourteen months; a famine fever
broke out in the town, and poor Grünthler,
who worked like a hero among the sick, at
last fell ill with the terrible malady. Olympia
watched by him day and night, praying in-
tensely for his recovery, and he was spared to
her for a time, but only to suffer more terrible
misfortunes. The town was sacked, and the
Grünthlers, treated barbarously by the soldiers,
escaped barely with their lives, and suffered
such hardships before reaching a place of
safety that Olympia's health was seriously im-
paired. The good Protestant, Count Erbach.
took in the poor starving fugitives, and his
wife nursed Olympia through a fever. After
this Dr. Grunthler was invited to a post in
Heidelburg, and Olympia was offered the
Greek chair in the University, which she de-
clined. Then the Elector wished her to take 
some high office in the train of the Electress,
but Olympia could not be tempted from the
domestic hearth ever again. Her health
gradually declined; she pined away and died
at last, expressing her joy at being called to a 
happier world. Her noble husband did not
long survive her, he fell the victim of another
pestilence, and the little brother died a few
days after him. So this devoted family were
not long separated. They parted in deep sorrow
but awoke to a deeper joy.
		G. S. GODKIN.
	Author of the Life of Victor Emanuel
Lung' Arno Guicuardini, Florence. 
—English Woman's Review.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT!


[Column 1]

THE HOUSE AT MIDNIGHT.

A LAY SERMON THAT IS WELL WORTH
READING AT ANY TIME.

	How quiet the house is at midnight! The
people who talk and laugh and sing in it every
day are asleep, and the people who fell asleep
in it long ago come back into it. Every house
has these two classes of tenants. Do we love
best those with whom we can laugh and talk
and sing, or the dear, silent ones who come so
noiselessly to our side, and whisper to us in

[Column 2]

faint, far-away whispers that have no sound, so
that we hear only their very stillness.
	I am not tired, but my pen is weary. It 
falls from my fingers, and I raise my head. I
start to leave the table, and my eyes fall upon
a little book lying on the floor. It is a little 
"First Reader." He left it there this after-
noon. I remember just how I was impatient
because he could not read the simple little
lesson—such an easy lesson—and I told him it
was a waste of my time to teach him, and
pushed him away from me. I remember now
I see the flush come into the little tired face,
the brave, cheerful look in his eyes—his
mother's brave, patient, cheeriness, struggling
with his disappointment and pain. I see him
lie down on the floor, and the little face bent
over the troublesome little lesson, such a sim-
ple, easy lesson, any baby might read it. Then, 
after a little struggle alone, it has to be given
up, and the baffled little soldier with one more
appealing look toward me for re-inforcements,
sighs and goes away from the lesson he cannot 
read to the play that comforts him. And
there lies the little book,  just as he left it. Ah,
me, I could kneel down and kiss it now as
though it were alive and loving.
	Why, what was my time worth to me to-day? 	
What was there in the book I wanted to read
one-half so precious to me as one cooing word 
from the prattling lips that quivered when I
turned away. I hate the book I read. I will 
never look at it again. Were it the last book
in the world I think I would burn it. All its
gracious words are lies. I say to you, though
all men praise the book, and though an hour
ago I thought it excellent, I say to you that
there is poison in its hateful pages. Why, what
can I learn from books that baby lips cannot
teach me?  Do you know I want to go to the
door of his room and listen; the house is so still
maybe he is not breathing. Why, if between
my book and my boy I choose my book, why
should not God leave me with my books? My
hateful books.
	But I was not harsh. I was only a little
impatient. Because, you see, his lesson was so
easy, so simple. Ah, me, there were two of us
trying to read this afternoon. They were two
easy, simple lessons. Mine was such a very
simple, easy, pleasant, loving one to learn. Just
a line, just a little throb of patience, of gentle-
ness, of  love that would have made my own
heart glow and laugh and sing. The letters
were so large and plain, the words so easy and
the sentences so short. I did not read one
line aright. See, here is my copy now, all
blurred and blistered with tears and the heart-
ache; all marred and misspelled and blotted. I 
am ashamed to show it to the Master, and yet
I know he will be patient with me; I know
how loving and gentle he will be  Why, how
patiently and lovingly all these years he has
been teaching me this simple lesson I failed
upon to-day. But when my little pupil stum-
bled on a single word—is my time, then, so
much more precious than the Master's that I
cannot teach the little lesson more than once?
	Ah, friend, we do waste time when we plait
scourges for ourselves. These hurrying days,
these busy, anxious, shrewd, ambitious times,
of ours are wasted when they take our hearts
away from patient gentleness, and give us fame
for love, and gold for kisses. Some day, then,
when, our hungry souls will seek for bread, our
selfish god will give us a stone. Life is a sim-
ple, easy lesson; such as any child may read.
You cannot find its solution in the ponderous
tomes of the old fathers, the philosophers, the 
investigators, the theorists. It is not on your
bookshelves. But in the warmest corner of
the most unfettered heart it glows in letters
that the blind may read; a sweet, plain, simple,
easy, loving lesson. And when you have
learned it, brother of mine, the world will be
better and happier.—Robert J. Burdette.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


CLEANSING FIRES.

Let thy gold be cast in the furnace;
	Thy red gold precious and bright;
Do not fear the hungry fire,
	With its caverns of hungry light:
And thy gold shall return more precious,
	Free from every spot and stain:
For gold must be tried by fire,
	And a heart must be tried by pain!

In the cruel fire of sorrow
	Cast thy heart, do not faint or wail;
Let thy hand be firm and steady.
	Do not let thy spirit quail;
But wait till the trial is o'er,
	And take thy heart again;
For as gold is tried by fire,
	So a heart must be tried by pain!

I shall know by the gleam and glitter
	Of the golden chain you wear,
By your heart's calm strength in loving,
	Of the fire they have had to bear.
Beat on, true heart, for ever,
	Shine bright, strong golden chain;
And bless the cleansing fire,
	And furnace of living pain!

		SELECTED.


[Column 1]

"WHEN WE WERE GIRLS."

	How often women of mature years make
this expression to each other, when some little
incident of the past is awakened, or when
meeting with each other after a prolonged ab-
sence; how natural to say, "When we were
girls." And now I am going to give you an
incident which actually transpired, not "when
we were girls," but when we met again after
a separation of forty years.	
	Two girls who had been playmates in child-
hood and intimate companions at school, who
shared the same tasks, and whose lives seemed
likely to run on in the same channel, year
after year, and whose hopes and ambitions
were similar, and whose tastes and inclinations
were very like, and who had pledged the
school-girl eternal friendship for one another,
while yet young, drifted apart through religious
tendencies and belief. Still loving tenderly,
without any unpleasantness, each went her
own way. One to the "far west." or what
seemed so in those days, and the other re-
mained behind, clinging to the old Puritanical
notions of religion. Each had her own pecu-
liar life history; both were characteristic of
the girls; and indeed just what might have
been expected from their temperament and
training and education. Interwoven in both
their lives were the love of poetry and the
beautiful in nature, and this endowment de-
scended to their children, for though they were
New England girls they were not old maids.
	On a cold, wintry day, when snow lay deep,
and one would scarcely expect a visitor from a
distance, a lady alighted from a carriage at
the door of a rather old-fashioned house in a
large town in the Old Bay State, and after
ringing the bell, inquired for Mrs. Dr. ——.
"O, yes, she lives here." After having dismissed
the coachman, the lady is admitted into the
parlor, and in a moment more Mrs. Dr. ——
enters, and the two schoolgirls, playmates and
companions meet after forty years' separation.
It was a strange-meeting—two who had passed
through so many experiences since they last
met, and the change of face and figure that
motherhood and time bring with them—and
neither would have known the other, only that
the traveler had been told by the servant that
she whom she sought was the mistress of the
mansion. Here stood the two little women,
grown old and grey; they gazed at each other
for one moment only, and then, the Doctor's
wife, grasping the hand of her visitor, said:
'I know you are not my cousin Irene, for she

[Column 2]

is dead, and the dead come not back again, but
you are one of my old time friends, and I
ought to know you. Yes, yes, now you speak,"
for the visitor had, in her excitement, ex-
claimed,  "We knew each other well, when we
were girls." "O, it is, it is Emmie, can it be
possible?" and the two were, by this time, en-
folded in each other's arms in a loving and ten-
der embrace. "After all these long years you
have come back to us, to your old home and
friends in New England," and by this time
the two were seated on the sofa by a warm,
glowing fire, and the visitor had thrown off
her wraps, and the two friends sat, as it were,
entranced. I cannot tell you all that trans-
pired in that short interview, but I can give
you some idea of its effect; the Dr's wife every
now and then saying, "It seems as though you
had come from another world," and then again
she would say, "Of all the people in the
world, now that my mother and her sister,
Aunt Ursula, are dead, I would rather see
you than any one." Frequently she would say,
in beginning a new sentence, "When we were
girls, you know."
	They talked on and on, and many of the re-
markable events in each one's life were re-
counted briefly, but in truth the visitor had
most to tell, for her history was richer far, be-
cause of the countries she had seen and the
advancement she had made in active work for
her sex and for humanity, as well as because
the religion she had embraced, and that took
her from home and friends, had so engrafted
itself into her soul as to broaden and expand
all her ideas, thoughts and energies.
	"How has it fared with you all these years,
and what can you tell me of our school friends?
Where are the boys who stood in the front
rank of scholarship, of whom we were all so
proud?" Questions like these from the visitor
were answered one after the other in quick
succession. Information of half a hundred
old schoolmates was briefly given, and memory
recalled many a scene and transaction in the
past, and in fancy they wandered over the
same hills again, gathering berries, or rustlsd
among the fallen, frosty leaves under the
chestnut trees, picking up the brown nuts, their
fingers pricked and bleeding from the sharp
burrs.
	What a picture these two women made as
they sat there on that wintry day. They had
been so far apart. Many thousand miles had
lain between them, and in such different
spheres were their lives cast, that not a letter
had found its way from one to the other. Both
had been engrossed with matronly cares and
duties as well, and yet each acknowledged to
the other, now that they had met again, how
often and how tenderly they had thought of
the time "when we were girls."
	They met upon the same familiar ground of
intimacy as in the former days; no icy barrier
kept them apart even after forty years; they
did not question each other as to rank or
wealth; the old feeling of girl-love came back
instinctively, and their hearts rushed towards
each other, removing all embarrassment.
	The past came back to them in all its rich-
ness and glory, and they saw not the luxuriant
furnishings of the handsome parlor in which
they were seated, nor the fast falling snow
without, but the old village home and all its
familiar haunts; and they reminded each other
of this or that, until a description of the whole
place and its surroundings had been graphic-
ally given. They knew exactly how many
peacocks used to sit perched on the rail fence
under the locust walk, as they went by to
school; every phase of that happy school-life
it would seem, was recalled and retouched by
the vigorous imagination of the two who sat,
as 'twere, entranced with the "visions of the
past."

[Column 3]

"But thank'd be memory—her sweet power can bring
Back to my heart its early joys again; 	
Her magic power revives the frozen spring
Of youth and hope, and re-unites the chain
	Of sacred sympathies."

	Again and again bright tear-drops fell, or a
great sob choked them, as they spoke of some
dear departed one, with whom they had been
closely associated.
	There is something particularly sad and sol-
emn in going back over old places and home-
steads after the absence of years and finding the
great changes that time has wrought, but when
one meets a genuine friend, who has held you
in her heart of hearts without questioning, it
is a proof of constancy, of which we might
all be justly proud it is so uncommon.
Such a friendship is indeed golden. Such
meetings are like a foretaste of futurity. They
afford us a dim glimpse of that which may be
when we shall reach "that other shore," where
our loved ones are waiting. Then shall we re-
count the dangers we have passed, the ship-
wrecks we have encountered here below; but
these two women studiously avoided all these,
and were careful to keep back all the dark
days, all the fiery ordeals, and yet if you had
seen them as they sat and held that sweet con-
verse together, you would have known by the
marked lines in their countenances, and the
subdued and softened light that shone in their
deep-set eyes, that the discipline of sorrow had
left its sure impress. How like and unlike
those two women were, and yet how well they
blended.
	Some day I will tell you more, for there is
much more to tell, but well might we say with
Byron of such an one,

	"thou art the friend
To whom the shadows of long years extend."
		AUNT EM.


R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS.

JUAB STAKE. 

	The quarterly conference of the Relief So-
ciety convened in the Nephi meeting house,
September 17th, 1886. On the stand were
Prest. Mary Pitchforth and Counselors, Amy
L. Bigler and Maranda Bryan, Sister M. W.
Snow of Salt Lake City, Prest. Mary A. Hyde
of the Relief Society of Sanpete Stake, and
representatives of the different branches of
Juab Stake; of the brethren, Prest. Charles
Sperry, Bishop Udall, Patriarch J. S. Bigler
and others.
	After the usual opening exercises, Prest. M.
A. Hyde addressed the sisters on the importance
of educating their children in the principles
of their religion, and providing them with
good books, and making home pleasant and
happy, for society is changing, and we must
keep our young folks from the allurements of
the evil one. A recitation, "The hand that
rocks the cradle," etc., by Mrs. M. E. Teas-
dale.
	Sister M. W. Snow referred to her long ex-
perience with the Relief Society; said she had
been a member of the R. S. organized by the
Prophet Joseph in Nauvoo, and had witnessed
with pleasure the good which had been accom-
plished by the sisters of Zion since that time.
Spoke of the great responsibility resting upon
the teachers, and exhorted them to be humble
and prayerful, that they might be guided by
the Spirit of God in all their visitations among
the Saints.
	Bishop David Udall said, "We are highly
favored in having the privilege of meeting in
quarterly conference, to transact business for
the Stake, and receive counsel from those who
are inspired of God to teach us of the plan of
salvation."


The choir sang, "Praise God, from whom all 
blessings flow," etc. Benediction by Bro. Geo.
Kendall.
	Afternoon session: Amy L. Bigler, Maranda
Byran and Jane Picton each exhorted the
sisters to faithfully perform their duties in the
Relief Society, and watch over their children
and keep them from the snares of the wicked.
A song, "Where are the friends of my youth,"
by Mrs. Elizabeth Grace, followed by remarks
from Sisters M. A. Hyde and M. W. Snow, on
revelation and the principle of celestial mar-
riage. A song, "Who's on the Lord's side,
who," by Mrs. Sarah A Andrews.  
	Prest. Mary Pitchforth felt to praise God
for the manifestations of His Holy Spirit dur-
ing our conference, and desired the sisters to
treasure up the good counsel which had been
given; and carry it out in their lives.  Recita-
tion, "The mountain boy's prayer," by Bro. L.
A. Bailey.
	Prest. Rebecca Udall, Counselors Sarah
A. Andrews and Ann Midgley each bore a
strong testimony to the truth of the Gospel,
and desired to prove faithful to every duty re-
quired of them. A song, "Stick to your
mother, Tom," by Mrs. L. Pratt.
	Prest. Charles Sperry desired to encourage
all Latter-day Saints to live their religion,
keep the commandments of God and prepare
themselvss for every event of His providence.
A reading, "What's the matter?" by Mrs. S.
A. Andrews. 
	Sisters Snow and Hyde, during the confer-
ence, each bore a strong testimony in regard to
the principle of plural marriage; they desired
to impress upon the minds of the rising genera-
tion, that Joseph Smith did practice plural
marriage, and that he sealed them to their
husbands, performing the ceremony in the
presence of their husband's first wives. Said
although the sons of the Prophet deny that
their father taught or practiced celestial or
plural marriage, they were living witnesses
that he did.
	Conference adjourned for three months.
Singing, "Awake, ye Saints of God, awake."
Benediction by G. Kendall.
		MARY PITCHFORTH PREST.
		M. E. BUCHHOLT, Sec.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	Two of the thrones of the last Napoleon are
for sale in France at one hundred dollars
each.
	It will cost $4,000 and take 6,000 books of
gold leaf to gild the great dome at Notre Dame
university, Indiana. The work is now in pro-
gress.
	The Vatican library contains 24,000 manu-
scripts, of which 2,164 are oriental, 3,052
Greek, and 17,069 Latin. A complete cata
logue is now being prepared.
	The President and Mrs. Cleveland have re-
turned to Washington from their summer so-
journ in the Adirondack wilderness. Social
life at the national capital is beginning to re-
sume its wonted autumnal activity.
	The Princess Maria Josepha, niece of King
Albert Frederick of Saxony, was married
October 3rd, to Arch Duke Otto, nephew of
Francis Joseph of Austria. The Princess re-
nounced for herself and her descendents all
right of succession to the throne of Saxony.
	A telegram from the City of Mexico, Oct.
4th, states that a curious phenomenon has oc-
curred at Chimapla in that state. Within four
days tremendous subterraeann reports were
heard, though at the time the meteorological
conditions were perfect. The people in the town
were filled with alarm, and an investigation


was made, by which it was discovered that a
high hill in the vicinity had been completely
divided into two parts by some powerful
force.
	Captain Uriel Crocker, of Boston, celebrated
his 90th birthday at his country place in Co-
hassett, on the 13th of September last. He
was prominently connected with the raising of
the Bunker Hill Monument, and has been a
director of the Old Colony Railroad ever since
its organization in 1835. His business partner,
who sat by his side during the day, Osmyn
Brewster, is eleven months and eleven days
younger. Their's was the old firm of Crocker
and Brewster, Publishers and Booksellers on
Washington Street.
	It is said that Queen Victoria can wash and
dress a baby with any woman, and can general-
ly diagnose the trouble when a child cries.
The Queen's skill in baby nursing was acquired
from the famous Mrs. Lilley, who attended her
majesty at the birth of the nine royal children.
This excellent person was a great favorite with
the Queen, who honored her with her confi-
dence upon the most delicate matters. When
Mrs. Lilley died, in her ninety-second year,
her majesty was unaffectedly grieved, feeling,
that she had lost a true friend. The old nurse
to the last proved worthy of the confidence of
her mistress.


MISCELLANEOUS.

	They are never alone that are accompanied
by noble thoughts.
	It is really of little consequence who we are
—it matters more what we are.
	Determine a person's worth by his actions,
not by what people say about him.
	Ideas are the seeds of thought, but they do
not produce flowers unless the soil where they
are sown is fertile.—Lady Btessingion.
	A good wife must be grave abroad, wise at 
home, patient to suffer, constant to love, friend-
ly to all, and provident for her household.—
Theophrastus.
	In every relation of life we must bear and
forbear; we must not expect perfection, and
each person should carry the cloak of charity
for the other—Rev. M. Huggins.


NOTES ABOUT WOMEN.

	Indian belles of Alaska wear a thick coat-
ing of oil and soot on their faces, when not in
full toilet. This is said to preserve the com-
plexion, which, after a thorough scrubbing,
looks as fair and smooth as a good article of
soft soap.
	Celebrations of one hundredth birthdays are
common occurrences nowadays. That of Mrs.
Mary H. Gilbert is the latest. She was born
in Gloucester, Mass., Sept. 8, 1786, and has
lived there since. At her birthday celebration,
she repeated a poem learned when five years
old.
	It is now reported that Miss Rose Elizabeth
Cleveland does not intend to make her home
in Chicago, and that she will go to housekeep-
ing in "The Weeds"—her home at Holland
Patent, N. Y. In this connection the Utica
(N. Y.) papers publish the following: "To
my friends and neighbors who have this day,
by their unmeasured kindness and marvelous
energy, saved to me my old home, I wish to
make some expression of my sense of obliga-
tion. I beg them to believe that no words can
ever express the gratitude I shall forever feel.
		ROSE ELIZABETH CLEVELAND.


"My dear," said an anxious wife to her hus-
band, who is running for office, "we must econo-
mize in every possible way."
	"I do economize," he replied.
	"Yes," she said, "you spend ten or fifteen
dollars a day in treating a lot of bar-room
loafers to beer and whisky just to get them to
vote for you. Do you call that economy?"
	"Certainly; that's political economy."—
Life.
