EDITORIAL NOTES.

	THE several wards of this city, and the country
wards, as far as we have learned, have been
specially mindful of the poor, the aged and the
sick during the Christmas season.

	THE pictures of Lucy Smith, the Prophet
Joseph’s mother, ordered from New York, have,
for some unknown reason, been delayed, but will,
no doubt, soon arrive, and at our earliest possible
convenience will be distributed to the subscribers
of the WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

	THE theatrical entertainment given free by the
“Old Folk’s Committee,” in the Salt Lake Theatre
Christmas week, was a most enjoyable affair,
and well patronized by the aged, the widows
and orphans, and it was indeed a pleasing and
agreeable sight. The real enjoyment depicted on
the countenances of the assembled multitude must
have been highly gratifying to those who had
taken so much pains to interest and amuse those
who have more of the sober realities and fewer
enjoyments than those in more prosperous cir-
cumstances, or who are not so far advanced in
years.


﻿A TRIBUTE OF LOVE.

EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	If you will accept them I would like to write
you a few lines concerning my dear mother, Sister
Ann Jenkins, who passed suddenly and peacefully
away on the evening of the 13th of Nov., 1886.
I desired to do so sooner, but sickness and other
causes have prevented.
	She was a faithful mother in Israel, and as such
I think worthy of mention in our EXPONENT
which she highly valued and diligently read and
from whose columns she derived much pleasure.
She, like many other faithful women in Zion, passed
through many trials and scenes of suffering, which
are known to none but God and their own hearts.
She was well known as an exceeedingly patient


woman, duty was always a first consideration with
her, and one of her earliest teachings which I can
remember was, “It is better to suffer wrong than
to do wrong.” She accepted plural marriage
early in her life with submission and thankful-
ness, and taught her children with earnestness to
respect and revere that sacred principle. I never
heard one doubting word pass her lips in regard
to the truth of this work to which she was devoted.
She was a careful economical and industrious
house-keeper, and although her children have
been for some time all married, yet her fingers
were ever busy knitting and stitching for her
grandchildren (thirteen of them) whom she dearly
and impartially loved, indeed on the day she died
she took care of the youngest of them for two
hours and she was stitching on a little dress about
twenty minutes before she expired. Her hus-
band was sitting by her; she had lain aside her
work and was reading the evening paper when
she fell forward at his feet and showed no sign of
life after. Her face wore a pleasant peaceful ex-
pression. Her life was one of unassuming merit
and fruitful with quiet acts of kindness. Although
we miss her gentle presence, her words of loving
advice and her sweet and blessed influence, yet
we are reconciled and would not recall her, for we
know all is well with her and she is free from the
suffering and cares which we are left to contend
with. In her life, we her children, have a noble ex-
ample to emulate. 
					MARY ANN LAMBERT.


NEW HALL OPENED.

	The upper hall of the Eighteenth Ward Inde-
pendent school house, the new and handsome
building which has been in course of erection for
the past two years, was opened Dec. 27 with
a social party. A happy and congenial multitude
thronged the interior, most of them being mem-
bers of the ward. Among the guests were Apos-
tle F, D. Richards and President Angus M. Can-
non, both of whom, during a lull in the dancing,
made some excellent and timely remarks. The
people of the ward were warmly praised for the
energy and taste displayed in the erection of so
beautiful and convenient a hall, designed for lec-
tures and entertainments, etc., in connection with
the principal object of the building, viz: a Latter-
day Saint school. The past with its primitive
scenes and crudities, was contrasted with the ad-
vanced educational facilities of the present, and
the changes in Society and in the country general-
ly since the early days of Utah were dwelt upon.
The young were counseled to avoid excess of gai-
ety and the pursuit of pleasure, and indulge in
dancing and recreation moderately, not only out
of deference to the trials their parents and friends
were undergoing at the present, but for their own
souls’ good, and to turn their thoughts to re-
ligion and the soberer things of life, principally,
using parties and kindred entertainments as a
means of rest and recreation, which was their
proper utility and significance.
	Bishop Whitney added a few words to what had
been said, speaking in praise of the building com-
mittee, the donors to the school fund, and refering
to the objects in view in the completion of the new
hall and building.  Dancing was then resumed
and kept up to a reasonable hour, when the assem-
bly dispersed. A vocal quartette was finely ren-
dered during the evening by Miss Deanie Caine,
Mrs.Vilate C. Young, Messrs. H. G. Whitney
and J. D. Spencer, accompanied on the organ by
C. S. Burton. Toronto and Thomassen’s 18th
Ward band, supplied the ball music in excellent
style, and Thomas Goodman was prompter.
Everything passed off happily and the party was
voted unanimously a very pleasant affair.—Deseret
News.


SEQUEL TO MY LAST.

	I have given my views very freely on celes- 
tial marriage, substantiating it by Bible proof,
quoting Scripture from the old and new Testa-
ment. But the enquirer often asks the ques-
tion, “How can you women whose husbands
have other wives endure it? I should think
you would quarrel and be jealous of your hus-
bands.”
I will answer these questions by giving my
own experience. When I first began keeping
house after marriage, there were two of us,
and we agreed to have family prayer, each one
taking her part; we also asked a blessing upon
our food. This was in the absence of our hus-
band. We prayed night and morning vocally
and in secret. We prayed for each other, for
our husband, our children, brothers and sisters
in the Church, and for all mankind. We asked
our Heavenly Father to preserve us through
the day, to bless our labors, to preserve us
from sickness, give us our daily bread, and
enlighten our minds by His Holy Spirit, and
to give us grace to enable us to overcome all
evil thoughts, jealousies, hatred and malice.
We are all weak of ourselves, but through
the Savior's redeeming grace, and through
charity, that covers the imperfections of others,
we are able to endure, to discipline ourselves,
and live in peace in the marriage relation. We
 each had a child; the sister wife a daughter,
and I a son. We had a double cradle made
to hold the two children, one lay at the head
and one at the foot. One of us would take
our sewing and care for the children, while the
other would do the housework, alternating the
work according as we saw fit. This made the
work light and easy for both of us. We lived
together until we had three children each.
	We were good and kind mothers to one
another’s children; we could go out and enjoy
ourselves and leave our children in the other’s
care. Our children grew up with the same
love for each other as though there were but
one mother. We studied each other’s comfort
and happiness, and few, indeed, were the hard
words between us, and our children never
quarreled; they were united, and they have
ever loved each other. All who became mem-
bers of the family observed the above rules.
As soon as our children could utter a prayer,
when old enough, they took their part in the
family devotion, both the boys and the girls.
We also taught our children the gospel of our
Lord and Savior according to St. Matthew, St.
Mark, St. Luke and St. John, and we teach
them to contend earnestly for the faith once
delivered to the Saints; not many faiths, many
times delivered, but the one.
	But I forget myself; I must not let my mind
run into doctrine in this article; I undertook
to tell you my experience in the Patriarchal
family relation; and when I tell mine, I tell
the experience of all true Latter-day Saints.
Yet it is all doctrine to me. What pertains to
this life affects the hereafter.
	When the family became large, each wife
had a home provided for herself and children.
I have a comfortable and happy home. I am
the mother of five children and twenty-two
grandchildren, and I rejoice in all the revealed
principles of life and salvation.
	So you see, dear reader, that the wives that
“choose the better part,” like Mary of old,
have that peace of mind that the world can
neither give, nor take away. They have no
desire to quarrel; they have confidence in their
husbands, and the husbands are true to their
wives, and their wives to them, in the marriage
relation. If they are not true to their covenants
that they make in the Lord, they forfeit their
wives and their standing in the Church. Those
who are cut off from the Church for ungodly
conduct are employed by officials to do wicked
and vile acts, and they have been known to


shoot down innocent men, and thus add another
sin to their doom; a sin that cannot be forgiven
in this world nor the world to come. Why?
because God had commanded him and he
obeyed. The man that was killed, or the man
that is deprived of his liberty for marrying
more wives than one, is only responsible to his
God, and not to his fellow man nor to his ex
post facto laws. The Latter-day Saints are on
a higher plane; they do not descend to the low
and grovelling passions that rule and govern
so many who wear the human form.
	If all are true to their God, then all work
together in unity, peace and love. But the
world sees not the true status of this peculiar
people. The scum will always rise to the top,
and this is always seen, and is not fit for use in
the kingdom of God; only fit to bring offences,
"But woe unto him by whom offences come,"
sayeth sacred writ. I have been a close ob-
server of these matters. They who oppose
this work, which is of God, are like the troubled
waters, casting up mire and dirt. Let the op-
poser take warning and cease.
			MARY ANN MERRILL PRATT.
	Salt Lake City, Dec. 26, 1886.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A REPORTS.
		TOOELE STAKE CONFERENCE.
	The quarterly conference of the R. S., Y. L.
M. I. A. and P. A. Associations of the Tooele
Stake was held in the Grantsville meeting
house, Dec. 11th and 12th, 1886, commencing
Saturday at 10 a.m., Mrs. Mary A, Hunter
presiding. After the opening exercises of sing-
ing and prayer, Sisters Minerva W. Snow and
Elizabeth Howard, who were here from Salt
Lake City, addressed the meeting.
	Sister Howard was the first speaker, and
gave very good instructions to the sisters, both
old and young, in her usual good way, and in
that good spirit that comes from every true
Latter-day Saint.		
Sister Minerva Snow spoke to the sisters in a
kind and motherly way, that will encourage
all who desire to do the will of God and keep
His commandments, and Sister Ann Tate gave
a verbal report of the Tooele Relief Society,
as Prest. Delamare was not in attendance. The
choir sang, and meeting was dismissed by Bro.
Wm. Rydalch.
	In the afternoon Primary Conference con-
vened, at which there was a large attendance,
Sister Sarah Hale presiding. The children
gave, in a very creditable manner, songs, recita-
tions and essays, and Sisters Minerva W. Snow
and Elizabeth Howard instructed the children
to obey their parents, and not forget their
prayers, and they would grow up to be good
men and women in the kingdom of God.
	On Saturday Evening the Young Ladies
held their Conference. After the usual opening
exercises, reading of reports, etc., Sister Tate
reported the Tooele Y. L. M. I. A. in not so
good a condition as it should be, but they had
started to meet again.
	Miss Melinda McBride gave a report of the
Grantsville Y. L. M. I. A. It was in a good
condition. Met in Conjoint session once in two
weeks, and had very interesting meetings. 
Sister Howard and M. W. Snow again ad-
dressed the meeting, and encouraged the young
people to do right and live up to their privil-
eges.
Bro. Wm. Collet endorsed the remarks of
the sisters, and said he had been very much
interested in the good instructions, and desired
to do the will of God at all times.
	Singing by the choir. Benediction by Bro.
Alma Hale.
	On Sunday morning the sisters from the city
attended Sunday School.  At the close of the
exercises, the sisters spoke in a very able man-
ner on the principles of the Gospel; encouraged
the children in attending Sunday School, and


to keep the Sabbath day holy. The sisters
also met with the meeting in the afternoon, and 
gave much good instruction to all present.
	ANN TATE, Cor. Sec. of Stake.


SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

SYNOPSIS OF MINUTES.

	Regular meeting held in the Fourteenth
Ward Assembly Rooms, Saturday, Oct. 2nd,
1886, Prest. M. I. Horne presiding. After the
usual exercises, minutes of the last meeting,
held in August (conference intervening) were
read and accepted; also minutes of the P. A.
of the 5th, 20th and 8th wards; also minutes of
the Y. L. M. I. A. of the 8th and Mill Creek
Wards.
	Prest. Horne then addressed the meeting for
a short time in her usual motherly and instruc-
tive way, after which Sister Eliza R. Snow
Smith occupied a short time. Said Latter-day
Saints ought never to look sad; that God would
overrule everything that is not for our good,
for He loves every one of us. Spoke of the
weaknesses we all have to overcome in our-
selves, and the progress we must make; that
there was no standing still in this kingdom,
but going onward; God will have a tried peo-
ple, and those that are of pure minds and con-
trite hearts. Sister Eliza made mention of
the family of Bro. Jacob Hamblin, who died
lately, and thought it would be well for the
sisters to give a trifle to help them.
	Coun. M. W. Snow spoke of the pleasant
time she had had recently visiting in the set-
tlements; thought the people were improving,
and rejoiced that she was a Latter-day Saint,
and associated with this people. Gave a few
items concerning the family of Bro. Jacob
Hamblin, and ended by praying that we might
all fill our missions. 
	Sister E. S. Taylor spoke of the necessity of
meeting together and getting comforted and
strengthened; that the Lord knows how to
mete out our trials, and will give us strength
according to our day. Exhorted all to set a
good example.
	Sister Emma Terry of Draper, Sister Julia
Pack, Sister M. Brown and Sister Nellie Cole-
brook of this city, each occupied a few minutes,
speaking upon interesting subjects to the edifica-
tion of all present.
	Meeting adjourned for two weeks with sing-
ing, and benediction by Sister M. E. Kim-
ball.


[Column 1]

VARIOUS QUALIFICATIONS. 	

	Once in a while, even if very busy, random
suggestions will shoot across one's mind, that
are, perhaps, worthy of note.
	It has occurred to me many times how gener-
ally the plain qualifications of every day use-
fulness are neglected in the education of young
persons; mothers doing themselves many ordin-
ary duties because they can perform them bet-
ter and quicker than their daughters, and can-
not spare time to instruct them in these mat-
ters. I have seen a delicate lady, the mother
of eleven children, beautifully darning the
stockings for her grown up daughters, while
they were practising their music lessons. I
wanted to express myself, but had to "hold in,"
as I was not on sufficiently intimate terms to
take such a liberty.
	I often ask myself, "Why should a girl
marry until she knows something of house-
keeping? I know of young wives who have
wasted enough bread in their first year of
housekeeping to have fed them another year;
bread that was quietly conferred to the pail at
the back door for a neighbor's pig. Why
should a girl hire out and receive good wages,
when she is unqualified to cook a plain meal
There are laboring men who work hard, and

[Column 2]

furnish abundance for the pantry, yet never
see a well kept table. They will sit down op-
posite a pretty face, with carefully prepared
banged hair, and eat burned up meat, soggy
potatoes and yellow biscuit. I know of one
good natured man, who hands his beloved sixty
dollars a month to provide for the house. He
sees no end of flounces and embroideries, but
is ashamed to open his dinner pail before his
fellow workmen. I regard him as one of the
martyrs, who live unpraised, and will bear up-
on his headstone no record of his cross and
heroism.
	There seems, after all, to be in this earthly
economy a beautiful adaptation of natures,
whereby the inefficient wife is provided for by a
diligent and indulgent husband, and again,
where an economical, industrious and managing 
housewife is the prop, inspiration and savings
bank of the meagre earnings of an occasional
income through an indifferent husband. If
such couples were otherwise mated, it looks as
though one pair might go clear to the foot of
prosperity's hill while the other was resting on
the summit.
	Some years ago I heard President George Q.
Cannon say in public, that in choosing a wife
he would rather take one who could make good
bread and could not play the piano, than to
take a good pianist who could not make good
bread.
	At the time I heard this declaration I had
 no experience in eating poor bread, and sup-
posed that a good quality of that article was
an easy enough production, requiring no special
attention, and commanding no special praise,
and felt it almost an affront to my sex that we
must all be expected to qualify ourselves in
cooking especially as I had a particular aver-
sion to kitchen work. Of course I had forgot-
ten an ignominious experiment of mine some
years before, which brought about my dismissal
from the pantry, and remanded me to the sew-
ing room. I have since learned the real merit
of President Cannon's remarks, and know that
that brief portion of his sermon, if cultivated
by every household, where needed, would be-
come a universal blessing. I recall a visit
once made to a lady who was a frequent guest
of my mother's; one whom we thought much
of. She was nearly all the time in delicate
health and in a state of despondency. Her
case seemed very sorrowful to me, for I was
much attached to her personal and mental at-
tractions. One day I visited her, and her help
being absent, she cooked the dinner—one to be
remembered. As she took the bread from the
oven, and turned it out of the baking pan to
cool for dinner, I remarked: "It is not quite
done through, is it?" and she replied: "No; I
always bake it underdone, good bread gets
eaten up so quick." The dinner began with
an Irish stew, and her husband, after tasting
his, soliloquized, drolly: "Two pounds of beef,
ten potatoes, six onions and—how much Scotch
—Adeline?" I never wondered afterward at
her poor health, and remember making a rush
for the pantry when I got home. Imagine the
effect of such a cook upon the health of an in-
valid.
	It has come to my notice upon several occas-
ions how needful it is for hired nurses to be
able to prepare tastefully and economically the
food for the sick room. Very often the hus-
band is a poor man, and the matter is of great-
consequence to him that the few delicacies he
can afford be used to the best possible advan-
tage. The success of the physician, too, de-
pends much upon the cook. I very much fear
that when the doctor and cook are in opposite
scales, the doctor's chances are with the patients
—downwards. I do pity the doctor, when his
or her reputation hangs as much, perhaps, upon
the cook's performances as upon his or her own
ability and anxious endeavors.
		AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

LITTLE HOUSEKEEPERS.

	In the large cities of the Union benevolent
ladies have established what are called "kitchen
gardens." That means a school where little
girls are taught to keep house. They are the
daughters of the very poor people, mostly
foreigners. Their mothers from over the sea
had frequently not any decent houses even to
live in, and so they knew nothing about our
kind of neat Yankee housekeeping.
	The ladies gather up the dirty, ragged,
hungry little girls from the streets and tene-
ment rooms where they live so wretchedly.
They are first bathed, scrubbed from head to
foot, combed, and then given some food. After
that they come every day to the housekeeping
school. They are given luncheon. The change
that takes place in them is wonderful. They
become neat, orderly and ladylike. They soon
begin to try to tidy up their wretched homes,
and in many instances whole families become
happier and more prosperous, just because the
little daughter goes to the kitchen garden.
	At the close of their school for the summer,
the girls have an exhibition showing what they
have learned. They have miniature tables,
tiny dishes and little beds and mattresses—all
the furniture and belongings of a house, and
with these they practice till they are first-class
workers and housekeepers.
	When they have finished the course of in-
struction, the children are given diplomas, and
are regular graduates. It is a very useful sort
of learning they have, too. A class graduated
in Chicago not long ago, whose exhibition the
newspaper men went to see. The class con-
sisted of twenty-four little girls, ranging in
age from 12 to 15, and representing almost
every European country. Their long white
pinafores, fluted muslin caps, and prim little
ties gave them an appearance that was very re-
freshing. They entered the room in single
file to a military march, carrying miniature
tables and boxes. In the latter were linen,
cutlery and small table furniture. Brought to
a halt, the teacher put the little housewives
through a catechism like this:
	"How often should a room be swept?"
	"Thoroughly once a week," answered a wee
bit of a Swede with a voice bigger than her-
self.
	"What kind of a broom is best for the cor-
ners and edges of a room?"
	"Whisk broom," sang out a maid with yellow
pig-tails, who seemed to be having her throat
sawed by the well laundried tie under her
chin.
	"Why is a dust rag better than a feather
duster?"
	"Because the feathers scatter the dust instead
of gathering it."
	"After this part of the programme the girls
took out their little table cloths, the piano
struck up a series of popular tunes, and the
board was spread beautifully.
	Before the beds were made the class had an
oral recitation, one question being: "What
three things are necessary for nice bed mak-
ing?" The answer being: "Level mattress,
smooth clothes and square corners. Then the
mattresses were whipped into shape, the pillows
knocked into form, and in an incredibly short
time every bed was flat and smooth, and the
children retired under a murmur of applause.
On their return they carried trays, with which
they went through a series of pretty maneu-
vers. Dishes were washed, and after the
place was "tidied up," brooms were used for
an exercise in calisthenics. The visitors ap-
plauded the neat and pretty girls and were de-
lighted.
	It takes two years to learn all the things in
the course. Cooking and laundry work are
part of it, and the girls learn to bake bread

[Column 2]

and cake, and iron muslin and laces that are
as beautiful as the prettiest picture.
	As soon as they get their diplomas there are
always ladies waiting to hire them to work.
Their teacher keeps a list of these, and they
go directly from the kitchen garden into good
places. They know all the branches of house-
keeping, so they can be cooks, laundress, cham-
bermaids, nurses, or dining room girls. They
also learn plain sewing. They get better
wages than ordinary houseworking girls can,
because they know their business so perfectly.
But that is always the way. They keep house
so nicely that it is like poetry to look at them
and their homes.
	No girl can get a diploma unless she under-
stands reading and writing and arithmetic. It
would be a good plan for rich girls to attend
such schools, too.—Ex.


[Column 1]

CANDIES AND CREAMS.

	A cream made of confectioner's sugar is the
basis of uncooked candies. Take a good sized
bowl, and break into it the white of one, two
or more eggs, and add to it an exactly equal
quantity of cold water Then stir in confec-
tioner's sugar slowly until you have it stiff
enough to be moulded into shape by the fin-
gers. Flavor to taste with any essence liked
best. Vanilla is always safe. Mould the
paste into balls about the size of a large mar-
ble, or lozenge-shapes, and lay upon a platter
or waxed paper to dry.
	Flavor a part of the French cream made
with peppermint or winter-green essence to
taste, mould into lozenges, set to dry, and you
will have three varieties of candy finished.
Mould some of the cream flavored with vanilla
into balls about the size of a twenty-cent piece,
have ready some English walnuts, cracked
so as to divide nicely in halves, and press half
of the nut on each side of the ball of cream.
Set to dry, and they will be ready to eat in a
few hours.
	Chocolate creams are a favorite kind of
candy. To make, mould small bits of cone
shape, and set to dry. Melt some baker's
chocolate in a bowl set over a boiling tea-ket-
tle. When the creams are dry, take them one
by one on a hair pin or fork, and roll in the
melted chocolate until thoroughly coated. Set
aside to dry. Should the chocolate cool be-
fore you have finished, place over the tea-ket-
tle again, or better still, stand it while using in
a pan of hot water.
	Another delicious variety may be made by
working into the cream the juice and grated
rind of an orange. Should the juice make the
French cream too thin, add more sugar before
moulding into shape. Make into balls the size
of a hickory nut. Lemon creams may be made
in the same way, by substituting lemon juice
and rind for orange.
	Another kind of sweet is easily made by re-
moving the seeds from the nicest and freshest
of dates, and inserting in place of the seed a
long narrow roll of the French cream, and
closing the date over it. Fig creams may be
made in the same way, or by cutting the figs
into four or five length-wise strips, and insert-
ing them in a piece of the cream moulded in
an oblong shape, and then cut open and closed
over the fig.
	Most melting candies may also be made by
using fruit jellies and jam (especially currant
and plum jelly and berry jam) or pine-apple
preserves. Cut the fruit into small squares or
oblongs, and inclose it in a coating of the
cream; or make squares of the cream, and
press a small square of the jelly against it be-
fore drying. A tempting candy is made by
making the cream into cakes about half-an-
inch thick (which may be done with a rolling-
pin on a marble slab, or making it into a flat
ball, laying it on a platter, and patting it un-

[Column 2]

til of the right thickness. Make each bar
about three inches long, and spread with a
layer of jelly or jam; place on this another
layer of the cream, then a layer of the filling
and then one of the cream. Press well to-
gether, and set to dry. If desired, this candy
may be cut into squares. In either case trim
the edges smoothly with a sharp knife, so that
they may not have a ragged look.
	In making cocoanut creams use, if possible,
fresh cocoanut; if not, the best brand of desic-
cated. If fresh cocoanut is used, prepare it the
day before using. While mixing the French
cream, and before making it thick with the
sugar, stir in the cocoanut, then add more sugar,
and mould until the cream is stiff enough to
make into balls or cakes. If you like, some of
the creams may be colored pink, and then
rolled in grated cocoanut before set to dry.
These are very pretty-looking candies. Grated
maple sugar added to the French cream, and
moulded into squares, also make a toothsome
candy.
	A nut candy, which is a great favorite with
most children, is made by chopping several
kinds of nuts, say almonds, walnuts, and but-
ternuts, fine. Make a soft cream, and stir in
the nuts. Then add enough sugar to mould
into shape. Make into large squares or bars,
and wrap in waxed paper to keep fresh. Fruit
candy, which is not as rich as the nut candy,
is made in the same way. Currants, raisins
seeded figs, and a very small quantity of citron,
chopped together. make a very nice fruit mix-
ture to put with the French cream. Make in-
to balls; or use first a bar of the cream, then a
layer of the fruit, as in the jelly candies. If
you wish to color any of your candy brown,add
melted chocolate.
	Of boiled candies, perhaps none is liked so
well as molasses candy and molasses taffy. If
the following recipe is followed exactly, success
is promised every time. Two cups of sugar,
four cups molasses. Use the same sized cup to
measure both molasses and sugar. Two small
table-spoonfuls of butter, two table-spoonfuls
of glycerine. Put into a kettle, and boil fast
twenty-five minutes. Take care not to scorch.
At the end of the time for boiling, put a few
drops in cold water. If it hardens and is brit-
tle, remove from the stove. Have ready be-
fore the candy stops boiling pans thoroughly
buttered, and just before pouring into the pans
stir in one tea spoonful of cream of tartar.
After putting into pans, pour a few drops of
lemon or vanilla on top. When cool enough,
pull until white, and make into sticks. Half
this quantity will make enough for a small
family.
	Taffy is made by boiling hard one cup of
molasses, one cup of sugar, and a small table-
spoonful of butter. After boiling twenty
minutes, test by dropping a little in cold water.
If it is brittle, pour into buttered pans, and
mark into squares with the back of a case
knife.
	To make peanut candy, first prepare the
nuts by taking off the shells and the brown
skin that covers them. Then boil two cups of
molasses, one of brown sugar, a piece of butter
the size of a small egg, and a table spoonful of
vinegar. Boil until nearly brittle, place the
peanuts in a buttered pan, pour the candy over
them, and cut into squares or bars.
	Butter-scotch, the most powerful rival of
taffy, is made by using a coffee cup of brown
sugar, half a cup of water, and a desert-spoon-
ful of vinegar; also a piece of butter the size of
a hickory nut. Boil twenty minutes. Pour
into buttered pans, and cut into narrow strips,
	Chocolate caramels are as popular with
grown up children as taffy and butter-scotch
with little ones. To make them, use a cup of
molasses, a cup of brown sugar, a cup of milk,
and a small table-spoonful of butter. Add to
the ingredients a table spoonful of glycerine,
as in molasses candy. Let the mixture boil


fast for twenty minutes, and when nearly done
add a cup of grated chocolate. When nearly
brittle pour into buttered pans; crease in squares
with the back of a knife when nearly cold.
Flavor, if desired, in the same way as molasses
candy. If the flavor is put into the candy
while cooking, it is lost before the candy is
cold.
	A cooked French-cream candy, which is very
nice and more economical when eggs are scarce
than the uncooked cream, may be made by
using two cups of granulated sugar, half a cup
of hot water. Put the sugar and water into
a good tin basin, and let it boil ten minutes.
Then test by dropping some from the spoon.
If it draws into threads, take the pan from the
stove, and try rolling a spoonful into a ball. If
it is creamy and moulds easily, pour into a
bowl and flavor. If it will not cream when
first taken from the stove, boil two or three
minutes longer. After it is poured into the
bowl, beat rapidly with a large spoon until it
all creams. This cream may be rolled or cut
with a knife into any shape desired, and be
used as the foundation of many kinds of can-
dies, in the same way as the uncooked cream.
If in working the cream gets too cold, set the
bowl in a pan of hot water.—Mrs. Henry
Brown in Cosmopolitan.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


DANCING IN THE WHITE HOUSE.

	Since Mrs. Cleveland has returned home it
has turned out that she went to New York, to
shop and buy dresses. The report is that the
new dresses have been ordered without train,
or dancing length, as the dressmakers say; and,
what is more startling yet, it is said that Mrs.
Cleveland intends giving one or two dancing
parties in the White house as soon as the offi-
cial mourning for Arthur is over. Mrs. Cleve-
land is passionately fond of young folks' society.
She loves to dance, and enjoys all the pleasures
of young debutantes. She is not much more
than a debutante herself, because she went to
Europe just after leaving school, and came
back to America to be married. She has had
no chance to go into society, and she intends to
do so this winter, and her friends think she is
right.
	When the subject of dancing parties was
broached to the President he would not listen
to it. Then Mrs. Cleveland got out some old
books showing pictures of dancing in the exe-
cutive mansion during former Presidents' terms,
and finally she mentioned that a stately minuet
or square dance would be no harm, and finally
she carried her point with the President, as she
always does. Naturally, there is much flutter
in society over the rumor that there will be
dancing parties at the white house, and many
say they will not believe it until they receive
invitations.—Ex.


[Column 1]

OBITUARIES.

	DIED, at Kaysville, Nov. 8th, 1886, Alice Ellison, wife
of John Ellison, of consumption. Deceased was born at
Waddington, Yorkshire, England, Nov. 25th, 1820. Was
baptized in July; 1838, by H. C. Kimball. Emigrated to
Nauvoo in the spring of 1841; shared in the persecutions in
common with the Saints; crossed the plains in 1852; settled
in Kaysville, Davis Co., Utah, 1853. She was the mother
of eleven children, seven of whom still live to mourn her
loss. She suffered much pain during her last sickness; she
was confined most of the time to her room nine weeks pre-
vious to her death. Her family were kind and loving, and
rendered her every assistance in the hour of affliction.
Sister Ellison was President of the Teachers in the Relief
Society from their organization; was always at the meet-
ings whenever her health would permit. She was truly an
example to her sisters with whom she met from time to
time. She often bore a faithful testimony to this great
work, and the Prophet Joseph Smith as being a man of

[Column 2]

God. Her faith in the Gospel was firm and unshaken up
to the last time she ever spoke to the sisters: she was full
of the Spirit of God, and bore a faithful testimony, and
said she knew that this work in which we were engaged
was the work of God, and that the Saints, if they proved
faithful, would overcome the world. The same day, in
private conversation, she said she felt she had not done her
duty in bearing her testimony to the truth of celestial
marriage. She said she knew that principle was as just
and true as any other principle which had been revealed 
from heaven, "and it cannot be doubted." She felt glad
that she had lived in that principle for a number of years.
She has gone to a higher sphere, and to mingle with those
that have gone before, yet her memory will still live in the
hearts of the sisters of the Relief Society. She leaves a
husband, seven children, twenty-eight grandchildren and
nine great-grandchildren to mourn her loss. Rest in
peace, dear sister, until we meet again, when our parting
will be no more.
		SARAH B. LAYTON.

	At a recent meeting of the Relief Society of American
Fork, a committee was appointed to draft Resolutions of
Respect and condolence to the memory of our dear friend
and sister, Alice Greenwood, First Counselor to the Presi-
dent of the Relief Society, who died at this place Nov.
13th, 1886, after a lingering illness of nearly two years,
which she bore with great faith and fortitude, manifesting
throughout a strong reliance on the promises of the Lord
made to the faithful; her mind was clear to the end, and
her faith in the Gospel bright and unwavering, and when
her vitality was exhausted, she sweetly fell asleep without
a struggle, thus fulfilling the promise, that to the righteous
death is sweet. Sister Greenwood has been identified with
the Relief Society since its organization, and has been a
great comfort and support to the President in her capacity
of Counselor. She was among the early Saints who were
compelled to flee from Nauvoo, having at that time three
small children. She endured many privations incident to
the journey. She came to Salt Lake City in the second
company of 1847, resided four years in the city, and then
removed with her family to American Fork, which she
assisted in founding, and has been affectionately associated
with the interests of that place up to the time of her
demise. She was the mother of fourteen children, twelve
of whom she saw honorably married, one having died in
infancy, and one while on a mission to help the emigration
across the plains.
	The funeral was held on the 15th inst., at the meeting
house, when, notwithstanding the extreme severity of the
weather, there was a large attendance. The body was
borne to the meeting house by her seven sons and five son-
in-laws, followed by the family, and accompanied by the
Relief Society in procession, and numerous friends. Words
of comfort and consolation were given to the mourners by
the brethren who took part in the services.
	The following resolutions were unanimously adopted by
the Relief Society:
	Whereas, it has pleased our Heavenly Father to call to a
higher sphere our beloved sister, Alice Greenwood, there-
fore, be it 
	Resolved, That we, the Relief Society of American Fork,
do sincerely mourn the loss of our beloved sister, and feel
to tender our sympathy and condolence to the husband
and numerous relatives who are called upon to lay down
an affectionate wife and mother, and with them we ac-
knowledge the hand of an Almighty God in our bereave-
ment; and;
	Resolved, That we do fully appreciate her services, and
her untiring devotion as a co-laborer, as worthy of respect
and emulation; and,
	Resolved, That we recognize in the death of our sister
the loss of a faithful and efficient officer of our Society; but
we feel comforted in the assurance that she enjoys the rest
of the faithful, and we look forward to the time when the
grave will give up its dead, and a reunion take place with
those we love, when earth will be purified and become the
glorious home of the redeemed.
	Resolved, That a copy of these resolutions be placed
upon our records, that one be tendered the bereaved family,
and one sent to the EXPONENT for publication.
		CHARLOTTE SHELLEY,
		ELLEN D. CLARKE,
		JEMIMA DURRANT,
		EMMA FEATHERSTONE, Committee.

	Another soul has gone to rest,
		Another faithful spirit flown;

[Column 3]

	Gone now to dwell among the blest,
		Where there's no grief nor sorrow known.

	Not alone a lasting memory
		Will her faithful deeds procure,
	But in God's celestial mansion,
		Eternal recompense is sure.

	Though hard it seems to part with friends,
		Yet we've the sweet assurance given,
	That ties so dear we form on earth,
		Will reunite in heaven.

			M. E. S. ABEL.
American Fork.

[Column 1]

A CHRISTMAS POEM.

THAT SAD YET BEAUTIFUL STORY.

Christmas dawns again in gladness,
	Quick the years of earth roll round;
Banished now the thoughts of sadness,
	Happy thoughts to-day resound!
		Bless the day, the happy day!
			Christ's sweet birth forever may
		Brighten hearts of chastened hoping 
			Into love and joy, we pray!
 
Ne'er forget we in our joyance
	Them whom persecution doom
Unto suffering for obeyance
	To their God, in a living tomb.
		Brethren. sisters, let us pray
			That our faithful brethren may 
		Drink the cup of brightest gladness,
			By God's Spirit all this day.

'Tis night. Judea's throngs have gone to rest;
	A wandering pair for shelter seek in vain;
The crowded inn holds not another guest,
	And deafened ears are turned to cries of pain.

My Father! must I die?" the Virgin cries,
	While tears of anguish mark her fair young cheek;
'None careth for mine heavy agonies;
	Oh!give me aid—the humblest roof I seek!"

"None careth for thee, dear one? O, my love,
	My Virgin Bride, mine Angel, say not so!
For aid I've sought in vain; but, as a dove,
	Shall Peace soon bless thee; seeking it I go."

And faithful Joseph soon returns to say:
	"No other shelter than a stable's roof 
Can here be found, this night; content thee, pray, 
	And from this one, dear heart, stand not aloof,

"For cold the winds do pierce thy gentle form,
	And thy dear head is bowed in sickening pain;
Come, loved one, shelter from the raging storm
	At least 'twill give—and from the threatening rain."

She leans upon his arm, and sweetly tells,
	In looks, her gratitude for even this;
And from her heart a prayer of pure love swells,
	As on her cheek she feels a husband's kiss.

And through the weary, wakeful, lengthened night,
	Our Savior's mother strives with nature's chain,
As only mothers know; that heavy fight
	For life, and its belov'd reward through pain.

But all hours pass, the blackest and the bright;
	And those hours passed for her, and morning found
A nestling babe, that, born with dawning light,
	Caused heav'n and earth with brightest praise to sound.

And who but mothers know the happy thought
	Of her, the favored of all women blest?
Nay, mothers, even we, who have not brought
	The world a Savior, know not how she pressed

Her darling in her arms. The gentle one,
	Whose innocence was proof 'gainst all earth's wiles—
Oh, none can tell how gazed she on that son,
	As, charmed, she lingered on his baby smiles.

And never mourned her tender heart, for ruth
	Her little boy did never stray from right;
Truth, justice, righteoueness attend His youth,
	And these, with knowledge, make His manhood bright.

And ever and anon the tempter's darts
	Are hurled with vengeance at that head in vain;
And well He knows the griefs of human hearts,
	And well He feels life's keenest, heaviest pain.

[Column 2]

But God-like strength dwells in that holy breast;
	No wiles succeed, all righteous laws He gives!
Succeeding ages by His laws are blest,
	In Him alone a world's salvation lives.

Alas! for thee, Judea! Can thy ears
	Still close to moaning cries of pain and grief?
Thy hardened heart resisteth still those tears
	Of God-like manhood, pleading heav'n's relief!

Beersheba to Dan unite to call
	Upon their heads the curse of holy blood—
Alas! and still they clamor for the fall,
	Upon their children, heaven's vengeful flood.

Ascending lone the mount, the Suff'rer cries,
	"If it be possible, my Father great,
Then let this cup pass by!" His heart replies
	Quick to the prayer, "Thou hast not long to wait!

"Thy Father's glories soon shall shine around Thee;
	Bear up, my Son, for yet a little while;
Amid temptations, faithful have I found thee,
	Be faithful still and win Thy Father's smile.

"For myriads watch Thee now, mine own belov'd,
	Their fate eternal upon thee is laid;
Then stand thou firm, nor from Thy path be mov'd,
	A few short hours—the sacrifice is made!"

"Thy will be done, not mine! Forgive the weakness,
	O Father kind and wise, that in my grief
Petitioned Thee, in lowly, earnest meekness
	To send; e'en now, Thine aid for my relief.

"I go, I go, to meet a doom of suffering,
	Oh, well I know mine hour hath come to die!
Great God! receive in heaven my lonely offering—
	To save mankind, my Father, here am I.

"I have not sinned; my heart is pure; the wicked
	'Gainst me find no complaint. Alone I tread
The wine-press. O, my Father! by Thy Spirit
	Pour forth Thy strength upon my suffering head!"

The hour had come; the dreadful, awful time;
	So great that darkness covers all the earth!
Yet who can read the solemn depths sublime,
	That burst from dying lips of suffering Worth:

"And am I thus forsaken, O, my God!"
	While torment holds that pure and youthful form.
"And so, 'tis finished!" As that high head bowed,
	And death released Him from earth's blasting storm!

He dies, and we are saved! Ah, who can tell
	The gratitude we owe to that pure life!
And pray we e'er that we may serve Him well,
	And that, untarnished, we may win earth's strife.

For through that Savior glorious things are giv'n,
	To him that overcometh in the strife—
Pure rest eternal in God's highest heaven,
	And chastest joys amid eternal life!

		RUBY LAMONT.


WHAT TO DO WITH YOUR
EXPONENT.

	Sisters, save your EXPONENT. Do not waste
or destroy them. After you have read them
you can put them to much valuable service by
sending them to your friends in the east, or
elsewhere, or if you happen to have no relatives
or friends with whom you correspond, procure
from some one of our missionaries abroad the
name of an inquiring friend, and furnish her
with this beam of light from Utah. You have
no idea what an amount of good you can do
in this way until you have once tried it.
	A missionary brother writes, "Send me all


your EXPONENTS, they are better than tracts,
Many times when I call upon a family, and
am refused the privilege of bearing my testi-
mony, I say, "Would you like a paper published
by the sisters of our Church?" They say, "Oh,
yes; by all means give me one. I did'nt know
your women knew anything of literature. Why
I thought your women knew nothing but
drudgery. I thought the Mormon women had
no voice, were not allowed to speak," etc.
	Printed matter is now carried very cheaply
through the mails, and when at the cost of so
very few cents we can do so much towards en-
lightening the world as to our real condition,
by sending our EXPONENT abroad, it does not
pay to either sell them for wrapping paper, as
many do, nor to destroy them. If we are live
Latter-day Saints we will be on the alert for
every opportunity to promote the Latter-day
work, and will not overlook these, what many
may call small matters, but which, when looked
upon in the right light, are very great ones. I
consider that if we can say or do anything to
dispel one erroneous idea entertained against
us as a people, we have done a great thing.
	We, the women of Latter-day Israel, are the
promoters and sustainers of the great law or-
dained of God for the saving of the women of
this nation, as well as those of other nations,
and we should not be slow in our endeavors to
publish this fact to their ears. The ignorance
that prevails among the women in the east
with regard to the women of Utah, and the
erroneous ideas they have of our real condition,
is astonishing, and it is our duty to dispel these
false ideas as far as it lies in our power to do
so.
	The EXPONENT is not the voice of its editor
and the writers of its columns alone, but it is
the voice of every woman who professes to be
a Latter-day Saint, and it is the duty of every
one of these to help send this voice abroad,
and there is no sister so poor or so illiterate as
to be unable to do her part. We see from the
quotation made from the missionary's letter
that the Exponent can find its way into many
homes where our Elders are not admitted.
This fact ought to stimulate us to follow the ad-
vice I have given above, and let this paper
accomplish all the good that it can.
	Perhaps I have said enough, at least I hope
I have said enough on this subject, that at least
some sister may act upon the advice I have
given with regard to the disposition of this
valuable paper.
	Mc.


A WOMAN’S QUESTIONING.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	Don’t you think we have fallen on very
curious times? I want to know if this is the
land of the free and the home of the brave, or
is it only a boast? Is this the land where all
are declared to be free to worship God accord-
ing to the dictates of their consciences? Also,
where no laws should be enacted that should
hinder a man (or a woman I suppose) from
seeking happiness as best suited them, provid-
ing they did not tread on any body elses toes?
Is this the country that has invited immigra-
tion to help develop its vast resources, and de-
clared all men equal before the law, or have I
had a beautiful dream that these things were so,
only to awake and find it only a dream, a
phantom, a shadow, without substance?


A few days since, a celebrated prosecuting
(persecuting) attorney declared in open court
that the Mormons must be convicted whether
they could obtain proof of guilt or not. Phew!
is this justice? And recollect, cohabitation is 
only a misdemeanor, while Hopt, the murderer,
is still alive, and, judging from appearances,
will be pardoned when it can be done on the
quiet. But then, he is not a Mormon; oh, no!
Then we have that noble, brave deputy marshal,
Thompson, that hides in houses and shoots
men down (in the back, remember) as they pass
along the streets, and who has a jury ready at
his command, and more than willing to do his
will, for so much per diem. This is called
manslaughter, and he is admitted to bail; we
may look for a verdict of justifiable homidice
or some such trash; but then the murdered man
is a Mormon, that makes all the difference; had
it been the other way the Mormon would have
had to die, but they will make their verdict to
please the howling wolves that would like to
devour us body and soul, if they could. The
fable of the wolf and the lamb was never more
verified than in the present crusade.
	When Senator Logan was here with the G.
A. R., he said in his speech, if we did not like
the treatment we are getting we might emi-
grate. Kind, very kind! but he has gone first.
I should not be surprised if more of the wolves
were stopped in their mad career. Has the 
whole nation got hydrophobia?
	Those that live longest will see most. If we
are only true we shall come out of the furnace
purified of all the dross.
							MARY.
	Grantsville, Jan. 10, 1887.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]		
	SHALL WE BE LIKE THEM?

	BY ELDER H. E. BOWRING.

	"Why can't they be like the rest of us?"
This remark was made by Grover Cleveland,
President of the United States of America, in
reference to the Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints in Utah, and was no doubt
elicited by the thought that if that people
could be induced to give up their faith in the
revelations of the Almighty (which the Presi-
dent and the majority of mankind do not
recognize), it would save the government and
himself much trouble and expense, and the
people vexatious prosecutions, imprisonment
and much suffering. There is no doubt that
the President was honest in the thought that it
would be for the benefit of those alluded to;
but let us consider the question, and see what
we would be like. Would it benefit us to be
like their corrupt politicians, pettifogging law-
yers, and time-serving priests? Shall we con-
form to the practices and vices that appear in-
separable with the boasted civilization of the
present age, and be like "the rest of them" in
their licentiousness, debauchery and drunken-
ness; their swindlings, robberies and defalca-
tions? Shall we adopt their dreadful practices
of infanticide, fœticide and abortion; their
assignations, seductions and adulteries; their
murders, suicides and pollutions? Shall we
copy them in their man-made systems of reli-
gion, having a form of godliness but denying
the power thereof; ever learning, but never
able to come to a knowledge of the truth?
Shall we heap to ourselves teachers having
itching ears, and turn the truth into fables?
Shall we, like them, transgress the laws, change
the ordinances, and break the everlasting
covenant? Shall we imitate them in what is
admitted by themselves to be a social evil, and
have our beautiful cities infested with thousands
of prostitutes, bringing disease, death and per-
dition to our once happy homes, corrupting our
chaste, virtuous and lovely sons and daughters,
and instead of a comparative heaven, bring a
very pandemonium of evils into our midst?

[Column 2]
Shall we repudiate our wives whom we have
covenanted to love and cherish not only for
time, but for eternity? Shall we turn them
out, and trust them to the tender mercies of a
cold-hearted and cruel world, and have them
branded with the name of harlot, and consign
them to a life of shame? Shall we allow our
much loved and cherished sons and daughters
to be branded with the name of bastard? those
whom we have reared in happy homes, taught
to be strictly chaste and virtuous, bringing
them up in the fear and admonition of the
Lord, teaching them to be sober, industrious,
honest, God-fearing and law-abiding? Shall
we disobey the commands of God, and ally
ourselves with the world, the flesh and the
devil? Our answer is No, no, no! a thousand
times No! Now in the celestial order of mar-
riage, to which, evidently, the President was
alluding, who is injured? Who complains?
Who murmurs? Have the parties who of
their own free will and choice have entered
into the covenant of eternal companionship,
asked for protection from the courts or the
federal government? Have they complained
of cruelty, desertion or neglect, unhappiness,
misery or want? Are not our wives and child-
ren well fed, housed and clothed, and sur-
rounded with the necessaries, comforts and
many of the luxuries of life? Will not our
people compare favorably with those of any
State or Territory in the Union? Let our
wives and children answer, for they are direct-
ly interested. Let the disinterested traveler,
merchant and statesman, who has had the
opportunity of visiting our happy homes, our
comfortable firesides, our thrifty farms, factories
and workshops, tell of our brotherly love, our
hospitality, our love of hearth and home, our
affection for our wives and children, and our reve-
rence for our country, our religion and our
God. Thousands could testify of the truth of
these things if they had the moral courage to
proclaim themselves friends to an unpopular
religion and people. The writer of this article
has seen in the past four months in England
more contentions, jealousies, evil speaking,
angry passions, unkindness, envy, malice and
uncharitableness in the monogamic relation,
than he has in all his thirty years' experience
among the despised, but happy Latter-day
Saints in Utah. Who, then, shall we imitate?
Shall we not strive to be like the ancient
worthies, who, through faith, subdued kingdoms,
wrought righteousness, obtained promises,
stopped the mouths of lions, quenched the
violence of fire, escaped the edge of the sword,
waxed valiant in fight, and put to flight the
armies of the aliens? Like them, and like
those noble Saints of latter days, when persecu-
ted, imprisoned or tormented, we will not ac-
cept deliverance at the expense of our honor
and integrity to our covenants, knowing that
we will obtain a better resurrection and an ex-
altation in the kingdom of our God. We will
rather follow in the footsteps of Abraham,
Isaac and Jacob, of Moses, Elkanah and
Hannah, of ancient apostles and prophets,
also those of our own day. We will imitate
the example of those noble women of Utah,
who recently met in convention at the Salt
Lake City Theatre, where thousands of our
wives and mothers, sisters and daughters,
young and old, protested against the prosecu-
tions and persecutions of their husbands and
fathers for their faith in, and obedience to the
commandments of the Almighty. Their resolu-
tions and protestations were echoed and en-
dorsed by their sisters in every city, town and
hamlet of the Latter-day Saints. In the
words of our immortal poetess, "Our hearts are
fixed; we know in whom we trust" We will
follow their noble examples; our faith is as firm
as the everlasting hills, as unchangable as the
immutable laws of Jehovah. Through obedi-
ence to the everlasting Gospel we have obtained

[Column 3]
a perfect knowledge that the Almighty lives;
that through the atonement of our Lord and
Savior Jesus Christ we can obtain salvation by
obeying the laws and ordinances of His king-
dom; that He does and always will reveal His
mind and will to His servants the prophets,
when He has a Church upon the earth; that
He has given us laws to obey in the age in
which we live, and revealed them to his apos-
tles and prophets; that the angel has come to
the earth who was spoken of by John the
Revelator (Rev. xiv, 6), and delivered the
everlasting Gospel to the Prophet Joseph Smith,
who came forth with the same promise the
Savior gave in His day when upon the earth,
that if any man would do the will of his
Father, he should know of the doctrine,
whether it was of God or not, and that the
signs should follow and be enjoyed by those
who believed and obeyed the message of life
and salvation, with all the gifts and blessings
that were enjoyed by the ancient Saints.
	The Elders of the Church have gone forth
proclaiming the message of life and salvation
to the inhabitants of the earth, warning them
of the judgments that will be poured out upon
those who will not know God nor obey the
Gospel of His Son Jesus Christ. After their
testimony the prophet declared they should
have the testimony of plagues, pestilence and
famine, earthquakes, bloodshed, secret combina-
tions, wars and rumors of wars. Desolation
and death should stalk through the land, until
the inhabitants of the earth should be destroyed
and but few men left. Knowing these things,
who shall separate us from the love of Christ?
Shall tribulation or distress, or persecution  or
famine, or nakedness, or peril, or sword? "For
I am persuaded, that neither death, nor life,
nor angels, nor principalities, nor powers, nor
things present, nor things to come, nor height,
nor depths, nor any other creature shall be
able to separate us from the love of God which
is in Christ Jesus our Lord."—Millennial
Star.

		NOTES AND NEWS.

	Tennyson's new book of poems contains the
three act play "The Promise of May." which
was produced in London some years ago. The
greatest interest in the volume centers in
"Locksley Hall," in which the poet reviews
the life of mankind during the last sixty years
and comes to the conclusion that its boasted
progress is of doubtful credit to the world in
general and to England in particular. A
cynical vein of denunciation of democratic
opinion and aspirations runs throughout the
poem, in marked contrast with the spirit of
the "Locksley Hall" of the poet's youth.
	John G. Whittier, the venerable Quaker
poet, celebrated his seventy-ninth birthday
December 17th, at Oak Knoll, Danvers, Mass.,
where he received the congratulations of hosts of
friends. The day was observed in Amesbury
by some of the schools in reading and reciting
selections from his works and also in a like
manner by some of the literary societies. Mr.
Whittier was the recipient of a large number
of callers. The poet is in good health, but
passes most of his time indoors.—Ex.


Mrs. Dinah Mulock Craik, the English
novelist, is now an elderly woman, given to
wearing caps over her snow-white hair. She
lives in the country, and occasionally goes up
to London to visit with literary people.
	The first statue of a woman, not of royal
birth, set up in England, was recently erected
in memory of Miss Dorothy Wyndham Patti-
son, better known as "Sister Dora," the famous
hospital nurse."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


STAND SIDE BY SIDE.

If God should judge us as we judge one another,
	How hard our lot would be;
If He shows no more mercy than we show to our brother,
	How much of hope have we?
If He should criticise our most earnest labors,
	With just as cool regard
As we oft criticise our nearest neighbors,
	Oh! where is our reward?

All things that can be shaken, will be shaken,
	So says the word of God;
And should we feel that we are almost forsaken,
	"Passing under the rod,"
How much it would increase our heaviest trial,
	And every pain renew,
If loved ones pass us by with cold denial,
	And friends should prove untrue.

When wicked men are seeking to decoy us,
	To gain their selfish ends,
By strange devices and inhuman laws destroy us,
	'Tis then we need our friends;
When even the grand old Constitution trembles
	Beneath their wicked sway,
And every human prop beneath us crumbles,
	O, Saints, be true, I pray!

When all the world rejoices in our sorrow,
	And all our hopes deride,
Aud speak of harder times for us to-morrow,
	We should stand side by side.
When our best men are led as to the slaughter,
	Confined in loathsome dens,
Or else renounce their wives, their sons and daughters—
	How can we falter then?

Our Lord has given us one great, gracious promise,
	That, though we stand alone,
His grace and strength will be sufficient for us,
	He will sustain His own.
As gold is tried in seven times' heated furnace,
	So must the Saints be tried;
Then will our God work out a great deliverance—
	Till then, stand side by side.

		E. A. BENNION.
Vernon, Dec. 14, 1886.


[Column 1]

A LITTLE PLAIN TALK.

	"Man's inhumanity to man
		Makes countless thousands mourn"

	The above quotation applies equally to
women. How surprising it is to an intelligent,
far-reaching mind to note the injustice done
to one another by our own sex.
	A number of speeches made in my hearing
by some of our sisters (I am sorry to say) in
my opinion go far to substantiate this fact.
	Some of these I will take the liberty to re-
peat in this little article, for the purpose of
showing what a habit some of us are uncon-
sciously falling into; instead of observing the
Mormon creed and helping to sustain the in-
telligent women that are among us. and that
have qualified themselves to occupy positions
that require education and intelligence, we
let jealousy creep in, and when this unruly
tyrant once takes possession of the citadel of
our hearts, we find he is very hard to conquer,
and we say and do that which we ought not.
If we will only try to possess ourselves of a
magnanimous disposition it will be of far more
worth than any of us can imagine; it will
strengthen and fortify us against such low,
petty views, that are constantly trying to take
possession of us, and we will possess a charitable
heart toward all our fellow sisters. The Bible
says, "Hatred stireth us strife, but love cover-
eth up all sins." Again: "A man shall be
commended according to his wisdom, but he
that is of a perverse heart shall bedespised."
Therefore, let us be willing to accord to each
one the mission they have to perform with all
magnaminity of heart.
	One sister says, in answer to an inquiry,
"No, I am not a subscriber for the EXPONENT;
I do not get much time to read, in fact I do
not believe much in woman's rights. I believe

[Column 2]

that a woman's mission is in attending to her
household duties."
	Oh, dear!  what a pity to have been born a
woman, if the name but serves to stay our pro-
gression intellectually. Our ideas of woman's
rights are a little different to those the world at
large entertain on the question, but we are not
the down-trodden, ignorant women we are re-
presented; we would have the world understand
that we are willing to and do stand by our
husbands, fathers and brothers in every princi-
ple, and especially the one grand principle of
our religion—celestial marriage—and this has
been accomplished most effectually through
the columns of the EXPONENT, edited by a
woman, and contributed to by such gifted
sisters as Sister Eliza R. S. Smith, Hannah T.
King, Helen Mar Whitney, and many others
I might mention, would time and space permit.
It is through the columns of this little paper
that the world in general have received much
enlightenment concerning the misrepresented
people of Utah, and I feel thankful to our
Heavenly Father that we have sisters capable
of assisting in this great work.
	Another sister said, "What! paid her as
much as you would a male physician?" in
speaking of a woman doctor, "the very idea of
paying a woman as much as a man. If I had
occasion to engage the services of either I
should certainly employ one of the opposite
sex."
	Just look at the absurdity of such a speech;
because one happens to be born a woman, she
should receive only half salary, or perhaps it
would better please jealousy and envy, who go
stalking abroad arm in arm, if she endeavored
to gain a livelihood by playing at rub-a-dub-dub
at the wash tub. This feeling should not exist
among the women of the Latter-day Saints.
	Let us strive to be up to every duty, and
hearken to the Scripture, "Get wisdom; and
with all thy getting, get understanding." If
we do this, each striving to help the other, we
will have a calm and contended mind, and be-
come noble, intelligent women. One author
says that the moment we acknowledge jealousy
in our hearts towards anyone that moment we
acknowledge them our superior.
	So let us be on the lookout, ever prayerful
and humble, willing to help sustain all that are
doing a good work, and whatever our station
in life, or the mission we are called upon to fill,
we will reap our reward in time to come.
		I. H.


A WORD TO THE SISTERS.

	I thought I would try and write a few words
to my sisters. My mind is crowded with sub-
jects, and yet I do not feel able to do any of
them justice, but in my weak way I may speak
upon several, and the first one that is in my 
mind is this: Whose duty is it to teach the
principles of the everlasting Gospel? Well, I
should say it was the duty of every Elder in
Israel; and should we stop here? No. Accord-
ing to my understanding it is the duty of 
every Latter-day Saint, both male and female,
to teach every pure and holy principle, and
not only teach it, but practice it, too. We are
the light of the world; we claim to be God's
people. Then it becomes our duty to let our
light shine to the world, and to do this we
must set an example worthy of imitation, by
our conversation, our actions, and our dealing
one with another. We should also set this ex-
ample before our family, our neighbors and
the world at large, and when we are doing this
we are preaching the Gospel.
	Some will say, "Well, I can do all this, but
to stand up before a congregation to speak, I
can't do that." I would ask you, my sisters,
why you cannot do this? Are you ashamed
to stand up before your sisters and bear your 
testimony to the work of God. Haven't you
had sufficient evidence that this work is true?


If so, then why can't you tell it to others? Let
me be plain with you, my sisters; it is no good
spirit that prompts you to feel that way, and I
would advise you, for your own good, to throw
off such feelings as that, and ask the Lord to
strengthen you, that you may be able to tell
the world of His goodness and mercy. If you
will do this, it will strengthen you and also
your sisters to hear you bear your testimony.
We must work for the promised reward; we
must strive for the crown we expect to wear,
and whenever we are called upon by our Lord,
we must work for him faithfully there.
	Now, there has been a great deal said to
mothers in regard to training their children,
which is a subject that too much cannot be
said upon; it cannot be spoken of too often,
but at this time I wish to speak to wives. I
would ask you, "Have you acted in a way to
make your husband love you and have full
confidence in you?" If so, you have it in your
power to control that man to a great extent,
to make him either good or bad, for the
woman, though she is considered the weaker
vessel; has more power and influence over man
than many may suppose.
	Then, knowing these facts, and considering
our position here on earth, that we are given
to man by God for helpmates—for what? To help
exalt him ourselves. Still we are dependent on
man to resurrect and exalt us; yet there is a
great deal depending on us whether we help
that man to be worthy of an exaltation. We all
want good and noble men, kind and affection-
ate husbands; then let us make them so by
kind words, noble deeds and good example.
When he feels despondent and low-spirited,
speak kind and comforting words to him; keep
your house and your person clean and tidy,
make everything look comfortable and pleasant,
and then your husbands will love home and
your society.
		M. O. BRADY.
	From the Advocate manuscript paper, Erastus.


TO MOTHERS.

	BY GRANDMOTHER.

	Now, I suppose you will think, what has
grandmother got to say now; I should think
she had said enough by this time; but that is
all a mistake; she has got a great deal to say
yet, for I love the little ones that God has
given to the daughters of Zion, for bright and
shining lights in the kingdom of God, and I
want to see every mother noble and true, and
teach the little ones noble principles--to be
virtuous, honest and industrious, for the more
the mind is kept employed, the easier children
can be governed. How the mother's heart
will rejoice to see her children grow up without
a spot or blemish on their pure and good name.
Oh! how different to see them take the other 
course, and bring their parents' grey hairs
down in sorrow to the grave; and when it is
too late they can look back and see where
they might have done better. The time to sow
the good seed is in their infancy.
	Some will say, "Oh! let them come to the 
years of understanding before you teach them
too much;" but you leave them that long to
themselves, and they think then that they
know too much to be taught, and they will
have no respect for their parents, nor for the
aged of either sex. But the mother who
teaches her children correct principles, and
rears them in the fear and admonition of the
Lord, and teaches them to respect and honor
old age, they will respect their parents and be
good children; they will be loved by everybody
and they will make good Latter-day Saints,
and be good citizens.
					CLARISSA WILHELM.
From the Advocate manuscript paper Erastus.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

		PUBLISHER WOMAN'S EXPONENT, 
			SALT LAKE CITY. UTAH.

SALT LAKE CITY,	JANUARY 15,1887.


[Column 2]			
	COMMENTS.

	The Edmunds Tucker Bill, of which so much
has been said of late, passed the House of Repre-
sentatives by a large majority with a "whoop and
a howl," on Wednesday, January 12th.  This out-
rageous bill derives its name from two leading
men in the Congress of the United States, and it
is greatly to their discredit that they should thus
degrade the talents given them, and subvert the
aims and intentions of all law-making and good
government by yielding to a popular clamor and
prejudice born of rumor and misrepresentation.
Senator Edmunds formulated the bill at first, and
it passed the Senate last session of Congress, and
was sent to the Lower House, where it was referred
to the Judiciary Committee, of which John Ran-
dolph Tucker is the Chairman, and in this way
this compound anti-Mormon, anti-American bill
received its double name.  Hon. John T. Caine,
Delegate from Utah, occupied an hour and a half
in opposing the passage of the bill, and exposing 
its inconsistent and iniquitious measures, and the
cruelties it would inflict upon an unoffending, and
already oppressed and persecuted people.
	His speech was able and eloquent, and he was
greeted with many congratulations at the close.
It was a strong protest against adverse legislation,
setting forth in a forcible manner facts and condi-
tions as they actually exist, as against false reports,
slanders and mis-statements.  Mr. Bennett, of 
North Carolina, spoke one hour upon the worst
features of the bill;  we remember this gentlemen
as the only one with whom we conversed, who 
said he would consider the bill prayerfully, and
we made a note of it at the time.  Reed, of Maine, 
who approved the bill, spoke in its favor for twenty
minutes, traducing and villifying the Latter-day 
Saints in the bitterest manner.  Reed might with
all propriety be termed a hot-headed Mormon-hater.
He was followed by John Randolph Tucker, the
god-father of the bill, who succeeded in arousing
sufficient antagonism to the Mormon people to
push the bill through under the excitement of the
hour, just when in the mist of clouds of dark mis-
representation and slanders it was rendered possi-
ble. 
	It seems incredible that such an outrageous bill
should pass without further protest;  there were a 
few negatives, but not enough to demand the
yeas and nays.  Doubtless Mr. Tucker is proud of 
that day's work, but, after all, if the bill should fail
of passing the Senate, or the President refuse his 
signature, he will not succeed so admirably in 
gaining popular favor at the close of his public
career by his attempt to crush out the Mormons.
	Mr. Tucker is a Presbyterian in faith, and
illiberal and bigoted in his religious views, as most
of that sect are.  He is strongly opposed to 
woman's suffrage, and after a hearing before his
Committee on that question, last winter, when
some of the ablest and most eloquent women in 
the land presented arguments and logical reasons
for the support of such a measure as the ballot in
the hands of women, Miss Susan B. Anthony,

[Column 2]
anxious to know what effect the speeches had made
upon him, inquired what he thought of the cause."
He replied, he was "too old to change his views."
"Then," said Miss Anthony, with the ready wit
which never forsakes her, "aren't you too old to
sit in the Congress of the United States?" Mr.
Tucker, who is very good natured, only laughed
at the joke, as he possibly considered it, though
Miss Anthony was in sober earnest.  Perhaps he
looks upon the Mormon question as only a possible
joke, but he will yet find that the Mormons are
most solemnly in earnest about adhering to their
religion.
	Senator Edmunds is, in every respect, a differ-
ent kind of man.  He is proud, rich, cold as an
icicle, aristocratic and arrogant.  He would never
have been half as famous as he is, and as widely
known, had it not been for the Mormons.  But
such popularity will not avail him much "in the
long run."  People who build themselves up on
the ruin of others are sure to have a terrible fall,
some day, and his will assuredly come.  There
are men here in Utah—"Liberals" they falsely 
style themselves—who are urging Congress to do
this great wrong against the people of Utah, and
deceiving the general public and members of Con-
gress, who are not themselves acquainted with
facts, by means of messages, telegrams and frac-
tional statements of things, elaborated and em-
bellished with falsehoods to suit their purpose.
Senator Edmunds and John Randolph Tucker are
not deceived, however; they know the situation
having heard the facts from those who are author-
ized to speak for the people, and therefore they
cannot plead ignorance, and are left without ex-
cuse.  On the question of woman's suffrage, Mr. 
Tucker holds the Southern view that women are
too good to dabble in politics or enter the field of
field of public matters in any such way.  But it is
very doubtful about his considering Mormon
women too good;  he likely thinks they are not
good enough, the same as he does Mormon men,
who are not in his bill thought good enough to
have the right to do anything to defend their rights
to the homes and inheritances, which their own in-
dustry and almost superhuman perseverance have 
made in a desert land, previously unknown, ex-
cept as uninhabitable.  But there are some things
neither Senator Edmunds nor the Hon. John Ran-
dolph Tucker, nor others of the same stamp can
do.  They cannot stamp out Mormonism from the
hearts and souls and consciences of those who
have embraced it honestly, and have received a
testimony of its sacred truths.  Persecution can-
not kill a principle, nor can imprisonment, or tor-
ture of any kind, and though laws are made to
root up and abolish it, the seed is sown and will
bear its fruit, notwithstanding all the influence of
the great, the noble and the wise of the kingdoms
and governments of this world combined together.
He who holds the nations and kingdoms and
kings and rulers in His hand has decreed, and
His words cannot fail.
	Even this extremely tyrannical bill, that makes
Mormons, one and all, little better than slaves,
unless they subscribe to an oath, which denounces 
the principles of their religion wholesale, is not
bad enough to suit such men as Baskin, he him-
self stated 'twas not what he wanted; he wants
something more like the Hailey Bill, and could, 
doubtless, see every Mormon hung or put to the
sword.  The spirit of hatred and vengeance, that
characterizes the men who oppose Mormonism in
the present day, is just as vindictive and quite as
cruel as the spirit that sought to destroy the
ancient Saints, when they cast the Prophet Daniel
into the lions' den, and the three Hebrew children
into the fiery furnace;  but even as they were
thwarted in their attempts to take the life of the
chosen of God then, so will they be in this day,
and all their cunningly concocted schemes will
fail, and the principles of truth stand firm as the
rock of ages, and those who abide in the principles

[Column 3]
revealed through the Prophets of God in this dis-
pensation will come off more than conquerors
There is no danger, so great as that of falling away
or becoming indifferent to the voice of the Holy 
Spirit, that leads into all truth, and which, under
all circumstances, is a sure guide and shield and
protection to the Latter-day Saint.


TO SISTER ELIZA R. S. SMITH.

	On the 23rd of December, having had a reunion
of the Nauvoo Saints and others, on the eighty-
first anniversary of the birthday of Joseph Smith,
the Prophet and Seer of the nineteenth century,
the committee most respectfully desire you to
accept the accompanying token of regard, which
will be presented by Elder Jacob Gates. The
article referred to is the artistic work of the
widow James Snow, and her daughter, widow
Sarah K. Young.
	PERCIA BUNNEL,		DIANA SMOOT,
	DEBORAH BILLINGS,	MARILLA DANIELS,
	FRANCES CLUFF,		LOUISA NEWEL,
	EMELINE MILLER,		HARRIET BEAN,
								Committee.
	Provo.
