	With many thanks, and gratitude that words can-
not express, I acknowledge the receipt of the
beautifal cake, politely presented by Elder Jacob
Gates, in behalf of the Committee.
		E. R. SNOW SMITH.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

	I would like to place a few of my thoughts on
paper, peradventure it should meet the thoughts
of some one else.
	In the year of 1852 I received the Gospel of
Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, knowing that it
was the power of God unto salvation to all those
that will believe and obey the same in all things,
as fast as it is made known to them. It was on
the 6th day of June that I went down into the
waters of baptism; I then met with many a conflict;
it was then that I upheld the principle of plural
marriage. I did not know that plural mar-
riage was in the Church at that time, but I said
that all good men in olden times had more wives
than one, and why not now? I thought it was a
good principle; it has always looked right to me,
and I have tried to live in accordance with the
same, and live according to the dictates of con-
science, trusting in the God of our salvation for
the outcome. Light and truth must be carried
unto all the world, that every ear may hear the
sound thereof, and the world left without an excuse,
and to clear the way before the consummation of
all things, which is fast hastening on.
	Every principle in its proper place, inasmuch as
the Gospel requires it—faith and repentance, and
obedience to every principle and commandment
that pertains to the salvation and exaltation of all
the human family that dwell on the earth, inas-
much as they will obey the same, and live accord-
ing to these requirements. I know that the
principle of plural marriage is a righteous principle.
I upheld it before I ever heard it was practiced in
this dispensation. It is upheld in the Bible so
plainly among the patriarchal order of families, as
they were acknowledged by the Father of the
Faithful in that day. The Scriptures speak of
faithful Abraham, and represent him as the
"Father of the Faithful." He did all things that
the Lord required at his hands, and it was ac-
counted to him for righteousness, and his children
followed after him. It was said that "I am the
God of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob." They were
called Abram and Sarai, (Genesis, xvi, 3). "And
Sarai, Abram's wife, took Hagar, her maid, the
Egyptian, after Abram had dwelt ten years in the


[Column 1]

land of Canaan, and gave her to her husband
Abram to be his wife." (xvii, 5). "Neither shall
thy name any more be called Abram, but thy
name shall be Abraham, for a father of many na-
tions  have I made thee; and God said unto
Abraham, "And as for thy wife, thou shalt not call
her name Sarai, but Sarah shall her name be."
Now, you see that the Lord visited them and
talked to them and ate with them and changed
their names. The Lord did not forsake Abraham
and Sarah because he (Abraham) had two wives,
but He blessed him, and told him that his posterity
should be as numerous as the sands on the sea
shore, or the stars in the firmament, that they
could not be numbered. Now Abraham took
Keturah for a wife to enlarge their families, that they
might increase according to the blessing that was
promised him. If we expect to have the blessings
of Abraham, we must do the works of Abraham,
for Abraham, in the Scriptures, is called the
"Father of the Faithful," of which I want to be
one.
	PLURAL MARRIAGE was an ancient order,
though men ignore it now. It was when the Lord
visited His servants the prophets. Men say that
we have no need for the Lord to talk with us, for
such things are done away now; but if He will
not speak to us there must be something wrong; it
must be that we are in the wrong, not the Lord
but I would like to be as good as the ancient peo-
ple of God, and be worthy to go where they
are, I would like to talk with them and enjoy my-
self with them, for I expect to know them and en-
joy their company, which we cannot do if they
will not speak to us, or if we are not worthy to be
spoken to; nor can we learn of their ways or
walk in their paths, that we may be like them. I
would rather be taught of the ways of the Lord
and walk in His paths, let them be ever so thorny,
for without trials what are we? Not worthy of
His notice. I could have no joy if I knew no
God and no hereafter. How could we advance in
the things of the kingdom?
	 I rejoice in knowing that there is a future state,
with all of its organizations, and I realize that I
am responsible for the knowledge that He has
given and blessed me with. Inasmuch as baptism
is necessary, so is the laying on of hands for the
gift of the Holy Ghost, and all the rest of the
ordinances of the Gospel. Plural marriage is as
necessary as any other principle of the Gospel.
If I did not believe in practising plural marriage,
I should not think that I was worthy of any, for
if one principle is wrong the others are wrong also.
How would we feel if we should pass behind the
vail and see those who were not allowed any
privilege to speak to us? I should not expect the
privilege of their society. We must do the work
that will claim the same blessings, then. Where
are all the faithful Prophets and Patriarchs of old,
as well as modern? I want to be worthy to meet
them and enjoy their company; I love them, and
the words that they have taught. We can go to
the Bible and read the words of Abraham, that
good man—he said that he married his sister.
(Gen. xxii, 12). "And yet, indeed, she is my sister.
She is the daughter of my father, but not the
daughter of my mother, and she became my wife."
Now this goes to prove that his father practised
plural marriage also. Abraham, whom the Lord
especially favored, had more wives, and his grand-
son is the same Jacob that, when traveling on his
journey, (Gen. xxviii, 11), "lighted on a certain
place, and tarried there all night, because the sun
was set; and he took of the stones of that place
and put them for his pillow, and lay down in that
place to sleep." (12). "And he dreamed: Behold,
a ladder set upon the earth, and the top of it
reached to heaven, and behold the angels of God
ascending and descending on it." (13). "And, be-
hold, the Lord stood above it, and said, 'I am the
Lord God of Abraham, thy father, and the God of
Isaac; the land whereon thou liest, to thee will I

[Column 2]

give it, and to thy seed." (15). "And thy seed
shall be as the dust of the earth, and thou shalt
spread abroad, to the west, and to the east, and to
the north, and to the south, and in thee and in thy
seed shall all the families of the earth be
blessed."
	Do we belong to the families of the earth?
Mark, He said that all the families of the earth
should be blessed. Now, where is one that it does
not reach? Have we not all sprung from the
patriarchal families, and how can we expect to get
along without obedience to the same law? There
is no blessing promised without obedience. Jacob
worked seven years for Rachel, and was deceived
and got Leah, and then he worked another seven
years for Rachel, and Leah and Rachel each had a
handmaid, and in time they gave each of them to
their husband for wives. This shows to me that
they loved their husband. Jacob, whom the Lord
named Israel, was the father of twelve sons, one
of them was called a dreamer, and his brethren
sold him. He was taken to Egypt, and he became
a savior to all his father's household, and had do-
minion over all things, and all Canaan, where the
famine reached them; and how many of us are of
the seed of Joseph, through the loins of Ephraim,
on whom greater blessings were conferred under
he hands of his grandfather, whom the Lord
talked with, and said his name should be called
Israel from this time forth.
	Moses was conversant with the Lord, and he
was the great law-giver of Israel. In his laws
especial provisions were made for polygamists; it
is not mentioned as one of the crimes for which
penalties were provided.
	Elkanah was a polygamist, yet his son Samuel
was a great prophet and judge in Israel. He was
born and lived under the special favor of God.
	David, king of Israel, was chosen of the Lord.
(1 Samuel, xvi, 12-13). "He took Abigail and
Ahinoam, and they were also both of them his
wives." 1 Samuel, xxv, 72-73. "He took him
more concubines and wives out of Jerusalem."
(2 Samuel, v, 13), we are further informed that,
"David did that which was right in the eyes of the
Lord, and turned not aside from anything
that he commanded him all the days of his
life, save only in the matter of Uriah, the Hittite."
(1 Kings, xv, 5). In this passage we have an as-
surance that David did right in taking all his wives
and concubines except in one instance, for which
he was severely chastised. When Nathan, the
prophet, reproved him for this sin, he said to him,
"In the name of the Lord I gave thee thy master's
house, and thy master's wives unto they bosom."
(2 Samuel, xii, 8).
	I might quote the passages from the beginning
of the Bible to the end thereof, and all would prove
these things, the purposes of the Lord, which are
one eternal round. Search the Scriptures; in
them ye think ye have eternal life, but they are
they that testify of these things. It has not for-
bidden these things for which many have to endure
the penalty of a broken law, and imprisonment
without a just cause, and with a conscience void
of offense toward the laws of man. God has com-
manded and we must obey. In this life only can
we prepare for the next existence, and because
we try to keep the commandments of God, our
fathers, husbands, brothers and sons are placed
before the tribunal and unjustly condemned, and
doomed to stay in prison for months or years, and
they will and do submit for conscience' sake. We
have endured many things, and hope to endure all
things. We feel sorry for our persecutors; they
know not what they do; they are measuring arms
with the Supreme Being, who has said He would
not be mocked nor hold them guiltless that oppress
His Saints. He has said that offenses must come,
but woe! unto them by whom they come. Jesus
said, "Judge not, lest ye be judged, condemn not,
lest ye be condemned." He said, "Let him that

[Column 3]

s without sin cast the first stone at her, and they
all fled."
	We have no hardness in our hearts toward our
oppressors; their lot is hard; they will condemn
themselves in that day when we all shall meet at
the bar of God; where none can say that God is
not just. We pity them, and say, "Oh Lord! have
mercy on them, for they know not what they do."
We fear not, for the Lord is at the helm of the
ship Zion, and He can steer it through the storm
that rages wild and fierce; we have nothing to fear
if we are faithful to our God. We must be tried
and proven until we are like gold seven times
tried in the furnace, and the dross is all con-
sumed.
		SARAH SUDWEEKS.
	Junction, Piute Co., Dec. 29, 1886.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


Y. L. M. I. A. CIRCULAR.

To the officers and members of the Young Ladies'
	Mutual Improvement Associations of the
	Oneida Stake of Zion:

DEAR SISTERS:
	As we are unable to meet with each Association
as often as we desire, we take this opportunity of
letting you know our thoughts and wishes con-
cerning the young ladies of this Stake. We are
engaged in the same great cause, and it should be
our greatest object to improve in all good things.
	The Presidency of the Church, in their wisdom,
have seen fit to organize these Associations for
the benefit of the young people of Zion, and it is
our duty to live up to the privileges we have. We
should learn the will of the Lord and then do it.
The Lord loves those who diligently serve Him,
and will help those who help themselves. The
Savior said, "The pure in heart shall see God."
	We would like to ask you, as daughters of Zion,
"Are you living pure and honest lives, so as to be
able to claim all the blessings in store for the
faithful?" We realize that a non-performance of
duty deprives us of the Spirit of God; therefore,
we would exhort you not to give way to evil in-
fluences, but heed the good instructions given you
from time to time.
	Are we, as young ladies, seeking to improve 
our minds as much as we are striving to adorn our
bodies with all the fashions of the day? If we
are not, now is the time to commence. Study the
best books you can get, especially those treating
upon our holy religion; also the laws of life and
health. Seek good society; use your influence
with the young men you may be associated with to
refrain from habits that are not in accordance with
the mind and will of God. Young ladies, you
have more influence than perhaps you are aware
of. President Young stated on one occasion that
young ladies had more influence than he, "For,"
said he, "you can put a stop to round dancing and
I cannot." If we have such an influence as this,
let us use it for good, and in the direction suggested
by him.
	Beware of those who are not of our faith, or
who speak lightly of sacred things. Be polite and
courteous to all, and respect old age. Avoid
speaking evil of others. Do not criticise the
authorities, but pray for them, and for all who are
in trouble or distress. Pay your tithing and fast
offerings, that you may be blessed. Keep the
Sabbath day holy. Be industrious, prayerful and
humble, for we are living in a most eventful age,
when much depends upon the youth of Zion, and
the young ladies are now called upon to pre-
pare themselves for the different positions and
labors they will have to perform, in helping to re-
deem Zion. Let us set our mark high, and faith-
fully and zealously work to it. Be patient, modest
and faithful, and study closely the spirit of the
times, and the sentiments of the living prophet,
that light and truth may be your guide through
this important epoch of our history. Hold your


meetings regularly and at the appointed time.
Have, if possible, one or more representatives
present at our conferences and officer's meetings.
The programme committee will find suitable
lessons to bring before the members from the
Bible, Book of Mormon and Doctrine and Coven-
ants. Use a portion of your money made at en-
tertainments, and so forth, to purchase books for
your library. Send the money with list of books
you have, and those you want, to Esther C.
Parkinson, and she will complete the list and
order the books for you. By ordering a number
they can be purchased cheaper.
	May the Lord bless you in your desires to do
right, is the prayer of,
		  Your sisters in the Gospel,
				ESTHER C. PARKINSON,
				RUTH H. HATCH.
	Of the Presidency of the Oneida Stake of Zion.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS.

UTAH STAKE.
	Minutes of quarterly conference of the Re-
lief Society of Utah Stake, held Nov. 26th,
1886, Prest. Mary John presiding, with her
Counselors. No visitors present. Conference
opened with the usual exercises of singing and
prayer. As there were no written reports to
be read, the presidents of the various branches
of the Relief Society throughout the county
were called upon to give verbal reports.
	Prest. John said she was pleased to meet
with us again, and "although, owing to bad
weather, we are few in numbers, the Lord has
promised to meet with us, and hence we will
claim a blessing. This is a trying time for us.
Sometimes there seems to be a dark cloud hang-
ing over us, but then we can see a rift in the
clouds and the sun appears, shining. Our
enemies are on the alert to overthrow us; let us
be humble and prayerful. I pray the Lord to
bless us, that we may be worthy of His smiles;"
etc.
	The presidents then gave in their reports,
after which Coun. Marilla M. Daniels gave a
great deal of good instruction to the sisters.
Said, "Straight is the gate and narrow the way
that leads to eternal life, but all that work for
it will gain it. We do not wish to falter and
faint now, but press on to the end," etc.
	Sister Moody, from Arizona, made a few re-
marks, and bore her testimony to the truth of
this work.
	She was followed by Bishop Myron Tanner,
who said he was pleased to hear the sisters
bear their testimonies to the truth of this 
work. "There is joy in these times; the hand
of God is over us for good. We cannot see
 the cause of these things, but the hand of op-
pression laid on the parents is doing more to
convince our children of the truth of Mormon-
ism than anything else could have done. They
are riveting the truths of Mormonism on
them." The speaker here recounted some of
the persecutions of Missouri, and told how he
witnessed the mob strike his father on the head,
and the effect it had on him. The speaker
was affected to tears in relating the occurrence,
but said, "we are called upon to pass through
these ordeals to purify us. Do not let us give
way to anger in regard to these things, but
bear the cross with humble hearts.
	Conference adjourned until 2 p.m. Singing.
Benediction.
	Afternoon session opened with singing and
prayer. Sister Emily G. Cluff, the first speaker,
spoke of the duties of mothers in regard to
their children. Thought the mother's influence
over them was more than the father's. Ex-
horted the older sisters to speak in Society
meetings; as it encouraged the younger sisters,
and gave them confidence in themselves to
speak.

[Column 2]

	Prest. A. O. Smoot said, "I cordially respond
to the call of Sister John. I am pleased to
meet with you again in a conference capacity.
The adversary is abroad in the land, and it is
time the line of demarcation was being drawn
between good and evil. Evil communications
will corrupt us and our families." Spoke very
strongly against round dancing, as being ex-
pressly forbidden by the First Presidency of
the Church, and asked the co-operation of
mothers and heads of families in discountenanc-
ing it and putting it down; also spoke of the
threatened evil of having saloons opened in
our midst. Exhorted mothers to watch over
the youth committed to their care. "We
should watch in the days of our temptation.
God will purify His people. He whips us in-
to line. I trust the inspiration of the Gospel
will lead us aright, and may the peace of God
be with us. Amen.
	Prest. David John endorsed the remarks of
Prest. Smoot. Said, "The subjects mentioned
are of great importance." Reviewed the
liquor question and other subjects. Said, "Let
us care for our poor and feed and clothe them;
comfort those who mourn, and if any are sick
and not able to attend meetings, it would be
right and proper that the sacrament be taken
to them." Spoke of the class distinction that
was rearing its head among the people. "The
Lord will not permit it. We must be humble.
We are baptized by one baptism; we must love
and esteem our brethren as ourselves; not by
words alone, but by works also, and in minis-
tering to their necessities. It seems to me in
the face of all our persecutions we have no
time to spare in dances. Let us meet often in
houses of prayer, and ask God to preserve us
from the power of our enemies. Let us put
away our vanity and turn to the Lord, for un-
less we do there are worse days in store for us
yet. Everything that can be shaken will be
shaken. We will all be tested. We are pass-
ing through a crisis that will prove us. No
power can crush us but our own weakness. All
these clouds will result in blessings. My
prayer is, that God will bless us and give us
His Holy Spirit.
	Anna K. Smoot made a few remarks in re-
gard to the Primary Association.
	Prest. Mary John felt that the Spirit of the
Lord had been with us. Said, "We will now
adjourn for three months." Singing. "The
time is far spent," etc. Benediction by Prest.
Smoot.
		CAROLINE DANIELS, Sec.

BOX ELDER STAKE.
	The Thirty-third Quarterly Conference of
the Relief Society of Box Elder Stake con-
vened on the 14th of December, 1886, in the
tabernacle at Brigham City, Sister Harriet
Snow presiding. After the opening exercises,
Prest. Harriet Snow arose and said, "I feel
thankful we have the privilege to meet once
more in a conference capacity. I trust we
may have the Spirit of the Lord to guide us in
transacting business pertaining to our duties."
	The Secretary being absent, Sister Annie
Wixom was appointed Secretary pro. tem.
Minutes of previous conference were read and
accepted.
	Susannah P. Boothe, Prest. of 1st Ward,
said, "I realize we have to make an effort in
meeting together, as well as in other respects.
We are living in a time that we need all the
teachings we can gather. Let us remember
our brethren and sisters who cannot be with us.
We can do a great deal of good in cheering
those who are down-hearted, besides doing
good to the poor. It behooves each and every
one of us to lift up our prayers early and late
in behalf of the youth of Zion; try to lead
them along, but not drive them. I hope we
will not be weary in well doing, that when we

[Column 3]

leave this existence we may leave a good re-
cord behind us."
	Mary Anderson, Prest. of 3rd Ward: "I feel
almost proud of our sisters who take an active
part in our ward, although we should not feel
proud, still it is a great encouragement. I
think the families in exile have just as hard
times as the brethren in prison. We don't
need to fear our enemies; they can do us no
more harm than the Lord will permit."
	Sister Bigler represented the 2nd Ward.
	Sister Keller, Prest. of Mantua: "I think
we are doing a little good, but we need to do
still better. This is a time when we need to
live near our God."
	Sister Watkins, Prest. of 4th Ward: "The
Lord is overruling all things for our good.
When we do good and show kindness to the
poor, we always reap blessings in return that
are of more worth than money." Bore a
strong testimony to the truth of celestial mar-
riage; felt thankful to have passed through
former persecutions, and hoped to faithfully
pass through the present.
	Coun. Olivia Widerborg: "I am not a very
proud woman, but I am proud of my religion."
Spoke warmly on the principle of celestial
marriage. Said, "I feel to praise my Heavenly
Father for my experience in this order; it has
learned me to strive to root out selfishness. We
would not be perfected if we had no trials."
Reported the Scandinavian Society.
	Bishop Hansen, of the 4th Ward: "Those
present are enjoying the influence of the Holy
Spirit, and their hearts are uplifted. There
are but few of the human family who have
this privilege. The life beyond is of far more
worth than the pleasures of this world. The
principle of celestial marriage is intended as
the greatest exaltation, but we cannot under-
stand all things in one day. There is nothing
that gives more comfort than to do good to
others in need. If we have done good here on
earth, that will follow us; anything else we
must leave behind.
	Sister Perry, Prest. of Three Mile Creek:
"My sisters, I am pleased to meet with you;
my health is poor; I came to get a blessing."
Compared the life of the Latter-day Saints to a
high school, "where they may gain intelligence.
What good we have learned will follow us into
eternity. There is nothing that looks more
beautiful to me than to see two wives of one
husband coming into this house together. The
young and the middle-aged have to prepare
themselves to be able to teach the women of
the world. Let us be kind and helpful to
those in trouble. Let us do all in our power
to share their trials and comfort them.
	After singing and benediction, adjourned
till 2 p.m.
	Afternoon session: After the usual opening
exercises, Sister Hubbard, Prest. of Willard,
addressed the meeting: "I feel to ask the help
of the Lord, for of myself I am nothing. How
could we change one principle of our holy re-
ligion. Our Father has blessed us with so
many things. No evil will befall the faithful;
all will be for our good. We who have em-
braced the principle of plural marriage have
reason to be humble, as families. Let us do
to others as we would that they should do unto
us. The Gospel never looked more beautiful
to me than it does now."
	Sister Harper, of Calls Fort: "We all have
our weaknesses to combat with." Spoke of
the principle of plural marriage. Said, "Let
us be united with each other, that we may be
strong; disunited we are weak. This is a day
for both men and women to prove themselves.
We should be very careful to pray for our sis-
ters as well as our brethren. I want to live so
as to meet the smiles of my Heavenly Father.
	Sister H. Hunsaker, Prest. of Honeyville:
"This kingdom will roll on, whether we follow
or not. Shall we enter into plural marriage?


Yes, let us not be afraid to do so, nor speak
against the principle; the Lord has given it for
the good of the human family.  We are co-
workers with our brethren in the kingdom of
God.”
	Emilia D. Madsen felt thankful to be
counted one with the Latter-day Saints.  “What
a beautiful spirit is here to-day; such a heavenly, 
warm feeling swells our bosoms and fills the 
atmosphere.  When we look at these sisters 
who have testified be strongly to the truth and
beauty of celestial marriage, how can we help
but believe them?  They are growing old,
but there is a light in their eyes, and a
sound in their voices, when they speak as 
they have done, that we are compelled to look
up to them with reverence and respect.”
	Bro. George Cook said, “I have felt the
good spirit in your midst.  We are called upon
to keep ourselves unspotted from the world.”
	Bro. Daniel Zundle: “There is a good spirit
present here.  We may labor trying to do our
very best, but when we lay down these bodies,
and we can look back, we will realize -that it is
but very little we have done to inherit the
great blessings that are before us.” 
	Bro. Wixom said, “I have heard some fine
instructions here; God is with you.  If the
world could realize the prospects that are be-
fore this people, they would rush in here by
thousands, by millions.  I believe we cannot
feel exactly the same as a woman.  She is the
helpmate and co-laborer with man in this great
work.  The man is not without the woman, nor
the woman without the man.”
	Sister Minnie J. Snow: “This is a time of
trying scenes, when we look back and realize
what has transpired within the last three years.
I feel an interest in the progress of this work.
I am led to believe, that more trying times are
to come yet.” 
	Sister Hardy: “These older sisters who have
addressed us are faded; their faces are wrinkled,
but there is something beautiful about them;
they are worthy examples, and we ought to
follow them.  The testimony of the older sis-
ters who have lived in plural marriage seems
to have more effect upon the young people than
a sermon from the brethren.”    
	Prest. Harriet Snow showed some beautiful
green homemade silk, sold at the same price as
the imported, and recommended the Relief
Society throughout to sustain this home indus-
try. “We have had a good conference, and I
feel thankful for the interest manifested.  We
need temporal food often, so do we also need
spiritual.  I like to hear the sisters bear their
testimonies to the principle of plural marriage.
I have lived in it for forty years. 	     *		*
I don’t know what our enemies will do with us.
We are the daughters of God; let us live, then,
as becomes His daughters.  May God bless all
our sisters, in all their efforts, and may we
meet again in three months.  If we are humble
we shall receive knowledge and wisdom to
guide us.”  Singing.  Benediction by Father
Box. 
			ANNIE WIXOM, Sec. pro tem.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

SYNOPSIS OF MINUTES.

	Semi-monthly meeting held Oct. 16th 1886,
Coun. D. H. Young presiding. After the
reading of minutes of previous meeting,
minutes of the P. A. of the 21st, 20th and 15th
wards, and Y. L. A. of the 21st, 2nd, and 3rd
wards were read; and Sister Zina then addressed
the meeting. Said, "This is the day that the
day the prophets foretold," and thought we
would have grace to stand if we lived near to
the Lord. Referred tenderly to the decease of
Sister Hannah T. King, and said, "Our older
sisters are gradually passing away, one by one,


but we should not  mourn  for  them, for,
'Blessed are the dead that die in the Lord.'
Sister King has gone triumphant and victori-
ous, and has left behind her a tangible record
of her life and labors." After many interest-
ing remarks, the meeting was given into the
hands of the sisters.
	Sister Harriet Woodbury spoke in the gift
of tongues, and the interpretation was given
by Sister Zina.
	Sister Sinah Bishop bore testimony that the
interpretation given had been dictated by the
Spirit of the Lord, and made many excellent
remarks.
	Sister Dyer spoke a few words in tongues,
and the interpretation was given by Sister
Bishop.
	Sisters Emily Stevenson, Grix and Morten-
son bore faithful testimonies. Sisters Evans,
Watmough and Brown each spoke, bearing
testimony and exhorting to faithfulness. Sis-
ter Mary Silver made a few remarks about
Sister King, and recited one of her poems.
	Sister L. G. Richards, after a few timely re-
marks, asked that the sisters would unite their
faith and offer up a prayer for our afflicted sis-
ter, Sister Wilkins. Sister Zina asked Sister
Richards to be mouth in prayer, and the sisters
all joined in the amen.
	Sister Zina then spoke a few words, and the
meeting was adjourned for two weeks. Closed
with singing Doxology, and benediction was
pronounced by Sister M. Snow.


[Column 1]

LEAVES FROM MY JOURNAL.

M. J. TANNER.

DEAR EXPONENT:
	10:50. The country grows pleasanter. Fields
are green, and land is being plowed; trees are 
beginning to leaf out, and low mountains or
hills form a background. The sun shines for
the first time since I left home. Things are
not as forward as in Utah, and I miss the irri-
gation ditches.
	Strong City, 11:15. Another wooden town;
wooden houses, wooden fences, and a general
wooden look. There is considerable timber in
this section of the country, but we cannot de-
termine just what kind it is.
	Emporia, 11:55. Quite a large city; street
cars, omnibus and hacks, the first I have seen
since leaving home. Houses of frame and
brick. Depot house of white sandstone. Pleas-
ant streets and fine stores and dwelling houses.
Looks, as we pass through, as if it might com-
pare favorably with Salt Lake City, but it does
not cover so large an area. Inhabitants given
at 4;500. It lacks one of the elements that
constitutes the beauty of Salt Lake City, and
that is, orchards and shade trees. There are
some orchards in the suburbs, but not in bloom.
There are quite a number of churches which
would denote a Christian population.
	Passing through the country we see large
corn fields from last year, the stalks still stand-
ing. It looks strange to see so much level
country. One fancies it must be cloudy, and
the mountains will be there when the clouds
clear away.
	Osage City, 1:15 p.m. Another wooden
town.  It looks so strange to see all the houses
of frame.  No more adobies and very little
brick.  Mrs. H—— is asleep, but I rouse her
up to look at the negroes—a house full of
them.  Black faces of all sizes peering out of
the doors and windows, and standing on the
porch.
	Topeka, 2:45 p.m.  Twenty minutes for
dinner. Topeka is the capitol of Kansas It
is a city of thirty thousand inhabitants. All
is bustle and confusion, but very little of the
city can be seen from the depot.
	Lawrence is a pretty town on the Arkansas

[Column 2]

River. Nice houses, with lawns and yards
nicely laid out. We are passing through a
timber country. New clearings are being
made, and the farms are full of stumps. We
travel for some time through woods.
	Kansas City, 5 p.m. Here we change cars.
It is a lovely city. The houses are built on
the hillside in terraces, one row above another.
The effect is very fine, but I should not fancy
the climbing. We cannot see all the city, but
the depot is very large and filled with cars.
Here we meet Bro. John Morgan, and Bro.
Nelson leaves us to go with him south. They
go to Chattenooga, after visiting New Orleans.
Three of the other brethren go with them.
Here we cross the Missouri River.
	Muscatine, 7 a.m., Friday, April 17th, 188—.
It seems to be a great lumber emporium and a
large city. Here we strike the Mississippi
River, the father of waters. We passed a hard
night; the cars are not so comfortable as the
ones we left, and we got but little sleep. We
have re-arranged our seats, and made ourselves
as comfortable as possible, when the river meets
our view. I remembered the days when I
lived on its banks, and the happy hours I had
spent searching pebbles on its shore. The last
time I saw it was when we crossed it in our
exodus from Nauvoo. My heart thrills as I
watch it, and I forget that I am weary or hun-
gry. There is a steamboat passing.
	Davenport, 7:45.  Cross the river to Rock
Island, and soon lose sight of it. We pass
through Moline, and see the large factory
where the Moline plows are made. We are in
Illinois now, and the country is level and wet.
It stormed hard last night, and is cloudy now.
Very little land is plowed, and no wheat up
yet. The country is covered with water and
mud.  I think the country must be pleasant
when the weather is fair. We pass hills and
woods, farms and vineyards. At Peru we ran
over a cow and killed it. We are traveling
along the Illinois River, and nearing Chicago.
We go to the dressing room and wash and
comb preparatory to changing cars.
	Jolliet. Here the States' Prison is situated.
It is a fine looking city. We pass a beautiful
cemetery, and immense stone quarries and
groves of timber. The ground is green, but
the trees are bare yet. The whole country is
covered with rain and mud.
	Chicago, 3 p.m. We have been duly trans-
ferred from our landing place to another depot,
and have to wait there three hours for the
train. It is raining hard. Some of the
brethren go out to see a little of the city, but
the ladies remain inside. We have seen noth-
ing as yet, for we entered the city between two
rows of freight cars, and see nothing but the
tops of stone buildings and masts of boats
bristling above the cars. We enter a covered
depot, and are emerged in covered carriages,
and see very little but a mass of high stone
walls. Sister Harrison and I go into the
restaurant and get a warm dinner.
	We leave Chicago at 5:15 on the Chicago
and Atlantic. We have a special car. It is
still raining, and all we can see is mud and
water.
		To be Continued.


PRIMARY PARTY.

	The Third Ward, St. George, Dec. 29th,
1886, gave an interesting party. Having an
invitation, I attended the happy children's 
party, given them by the President, Sister
Margaret Mathie, who had provided everything
for their comfort and amusement. Over seven-
ty-five children, boys and girls, were present.
The party was called to order by the President.
The child choir sang, "Happy Children," ac-
companied on the organ by Sister Warren.
Prayer by Sister Mathie. The choir very 
beautifully rendered, "Love at Home."


Then came a time of feasting for the children
on the good things provided for them. A recita-
tion came next in order, by Louisa Cox, entitled,
"Goodbye, Little Bird." Martha Mills and
Ella Bird sang, "Poor Mary came o'er the
Wild Moor," very sweetly, which song was re-
peated. A funny little dialogue, by three little
girls—"What are we good for"showed that
little girls can be of use. "Running away from
home," a dialogue by three boys, pictured
the folly of leaving a good home for the uncer-
tain life of a great city. An original piece by
Sister Mathie was recited as follows:

	O God, the Eternal Father!
		May our faith increase in thee;
	Though our eyes grow dim and shaded,
		May our spirits always see.

	May we e'er be true and faithful,
		And walk in the narrow way,
	May our path to life eternal,
		Grow brighter every day.

	Be kind to the poor and needy,
		The lame, the halt and blind,
	Then in the restoration
		Our place we'll surely find.

	Speak a kind word to the erring,
		For they have the debt to pay,
	And thank God, our Heavenly Father,
		That we've chose the better way.

	Let us teach to Zion's daughters
		To shun the path of sin;
	With words of persuasion and kindness
		Do let us gather them in.

	Say to the gay and thoughtless,
		"It's no time for idle glee,
	For soon the judgments of heaven
		All over the nations will be."

	Next Joseph Mills told what he would do
when a man. "Curfew shall not ring to-night,"
was well spoken by Alma Clark. Two little
girls sang, "Merry Bells," with Sister Warren
accompanying on the organ.
	Then came a recess, and after the children 
came in William Higgins spoke on intoxicat-
ing drinks. Delia Nixon played on the organ,
while the choir sang, "Round the Throne of
God." Mary Cox spoke, very feelingly, "The
Letter." "We're Marching on to Glory," was
sung, Candy, nuts and popcorn were passed
around, and the smiling children dismissed by
Lizzie Cox.
		EMILY B. SPENCER.


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HEALTH AND HYGIENE.

	Muchardt, professor of hygiene at the Paris
Faculty of Medicine, declares that aged people
should have exercise for the benefit of all the
organs of nutrition and locomotion as well
as young people. He says that the tendency
to rest brings on a gradual diminution of
strength. Moderate exercise, particularly of
walking, is commended. It is well known that
the disuse of any organ impairs its powers.
Why, then, should not the aged lose the use of
their members the sooner from giving way to
an inclination for rest.


[Column 1]

OBITUARIES.

	DIED, at Pima, Graham Co., A. T., Nov. 23rd, 1886,
Caroline Adrianna, beloved daughter of Heber and Caroline
Masham, born May 8th, 1872, at Santaquin, Utah.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT,
	Adopted by the Y. L. M. I A, of Pima, to the memory
of Caroline Adrianna Masham:
	Whereas, by the decree of an alwise Providence, our
worthy young sister, Caroline Adrianna has been taken
from our midst.
	Resolved, That we, her associates and co-workers, of

[Column 2]

the Young Ladies' Association, do fondly cherish her
memory, and while we sadly miss her; we feel to bow in
humble submission to the divine will of heaven.
	Resolved, That we condole with the bereaved family in
the loss of their dear one, not, however, without the con-
soling reflection that she has gone in her purity, and is
worthy to come forth in the morning of the first resurrec-
tion.
	Resolved, That these Resolutions be entered in the
Young Ladies' record; also a copy be presented to the
parents of the deceased, and one be sent to the WOMAN'S
EXPONENT for publication.
		LAURA NUTTALL,
		LUCINDA GUSTAFSON, Committee.
		LIZZIE BIRDNOE.

	DIED, at Ephraim, Colorado, Dec. 26th, 1886, of In-
flamation of the lungs, Bodel Katrine Poulsen, wife of
Andrew Poulsen, born in Denmark, Oct. 3rd, 1820. Was
baptized in February, 1852; started for Utah, in connection
with her husband and family, in Dec., 1852, arriving in
Salt Lake City, Utah, September 29th, 1853. They made
their home in Manti, Sanpete County, where she patiently
bore the trials and hardships of those early days. While
there she gathered around her a host of friends, assisted in
making a comfortable home, which she enjoyed until 1880,
when her husband was called to this place, she arriving
here in 1881. She was ever ready to impart to the poor,
visit the sick and comfort those who mourned. At the
time of her death she held the office of presiding teacher in
the Relief Society, which office she filled with honor, per-
forming the duties with great credit. We have lost a true
helper, her aged husband a faithful wife, and her children
a loving mother. She leaves a husband, seven children,
twenty-five grandchildren, and one great-grandchild to
mourn her loss. Two children and eighteen grandchildren
have passed behind the vail before her. We sympathize
with the bereaved family in their loss, and hope that we
with them may meet her in the morning of the first resur-
rection, where there is no parting.
		ANNE MARTHA RASMUSSEN, Prest. R. S.

	DIED, at Nephi, Dec. 31st, 1886, of paralysis, Sister
Julia Bryan, wife of the late C. H. Bryan. Deceased was
born in France, January 2d, 1812. She embraced the Gos-
pel in 1851; was baptized by Bro. Bolton. Emigrated to
Utah in 1853, and came to Nephi the same year, where
she resided to the time of her demise. She joined the Re-
lief Society at its organization, and was chosen to hold the
office of teacher. She afterwards held the office of Assist-
ant Treasurer until June last, when she resigned that posi-
tion on account of declining health. "Aunt Julia," as she
was familiarly called, was a diligent and earnest worker in
the interest of the Society, faithful in the discharge of every
duty, diligent in taking care of everything committed to her
charge. Rejoiced in meeting with the sisters and bearing
testimony to the truth of the Gospel, as taught and prac-
tised by the Latter-day Saints. She left home and kin-
dred to cast her lot with the people of God. On Sunday,
her seventy-fifth birthday, her remains were followed from
the family residence to the meeting house; by her family
and intimate friends, the Stake and local officers, teachers,
and a number of the members of the Relief Society. After
singing by the choir, "Softly beams," etc., a very impres-
sive prayer was offered by Bishop Udall. The choir sang,
"Mourn not the dead," etc. The congregation was ad-
dressed by President C. Sperry, George Kendall, Andrew
Love and David Cazier. Her remains were followed to the
cemetery by a large number of citizens, conveyed in
carriages (about forty in number). Singing by the choir,
'Unveil thy boson," and "Wanted on the other side."
	At a meeting of the Relief Society, Dec. 6th, the follow-
ing Resolutions were adopted:
	Whereas, it has pleased our Heavenly Father to release
from her suffering our beloved sister, Julia Bryan,
	We, as officers and members of the Relief So-
ciety, feel to acknowledge the hand of God in taking
from our midst a good, noble, upright and devoted mem-
ber of our Society, feeling that she has accomplished a
noble mission, lived in the celestial order of marriage, and
with her sister wife labored for the interest of the family. In
blessing, she was indeed blessed, with the love and affection
of her husband's children and grandchildren.
	Resolved, That we deeply sympathize with the bereaved
family in the loss of a faithful member and devoted friend,
and that we, her co-laborers, strive to emulate her exam-
ple.
	Resolved, That a copy of these Resolutions be placed

[Column 3]

on the Relief Society records, printed in the WOMAN'S
EXPONENT, and a copy given to the bereaved family.
		MARY PITCHFORTH,
		AMY L. BIGLER, Committee.
		E. R. UDALL,

	Dearest sister, we have loved you,
	We have loved you fond and true,
	And it fills our hearts with sadness,
		That we have to part with you;
	But a crown of royal brightness,
		Waits to deck your honored brow,
	And a robe of purest whiteness
		Waits your body to endow.

	Where your friends await to meet you,
	And your husband waits to greet you,
		For you have been good and true;
	You have been so kind and patient,
		Ever laboring for the poor,
	And for this the Lord has loved you—
		May you rest in peace secure.

	Dearest sister, we shall miss you,
		We shall miss your presence here,
	But we hope again to meet you—
		Farewell, sister, sister dear.

			S. K.

	The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.
VOL. 15	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, FEBRUARY 1, 1887.	NO. 17.


[Column 1]		
	WHAT CAN I DO?

"What can I do," says the aged sire,
	As he stands near the bounds of life,
"What can I do other hearts to inspire,
	To help amid earth-care and strife?

"My time is far spent, for good or for ill,
	One moment I cannot recall;
My limbs have grown weary, though strong is the will
	And slowly the night shadows fall."

You can show to the youth where the pitfalls lie,
	And snares for unwary feet,
For we clearer see over days gone by,
	When the web of life is complete.

Those lessons in life are of priceless worth,
	If you've garnered the golden store;
Now bring them forth in the home and hearth,
	While you're nearing eternity's shore.

The mother dear, with silvered hair,
	Whose journey is well nigh done,
Dreams, while she sits in the old arm chair,
	With face toward the setting sun,

Of that other time, when life's sun arose,
	Pure and fresh and bright;
Thinks how the joys were oft mingled with woes,
	How tears oft quenched the light.

"What can I do, what can I do?"
	Is the prayer of her loving heart,
For those whose dreams of life are new,
	Those of her life a part.

She can strengthen the tender, untried feet,
	When the morning shadows fall;
She can teach the young hearts how to meet,
	The cares that must come to all.

She can tell of charity, faith and love,
	And the strength that prayer can give;
She can tell of that peace from heaven above
	Which helps the weary live.

The boy goes forth in the morning's beam,
	With hopes and ambition high;
The future to him is a glorious dream,
	He heeds not the dangers nigh.

Two paths lie before him, the false and the true;
	One is death, the other is life,
Which will he take, what will he do
	In this wonderful conflict of life?

What can he do, what will he do 
	To bless or to darken the way?
'Tis a question for you, boys, a question for you,
	Think well 'ere from honor you stray.

You can bring joy and strength to the home,
	Which God unto you has given,
Aye, strength and joy, wherever you roam—
	You can help to make earth a heaven,

Or you can bend its grey heads down
	So low that they cannot rise;
You can trample in dust life's brightest crown,
	That you might have raised to the skies.

You can break the hearts, most loving and true,
	Plant wormwood where roses might bloom;
A blight or a blessing will aye follow you
	In your pathway through life to the tomb.

And girlhood's dream, too, is bright and rare, 
	With no shadow of cloud or storm;
No thorn on the rose in her sunny hair,
	No cause for a fierce alarm.

But soon she will learn there's a part to bear 
	By each of God's children here,

[Column 2]
That she must have courage and strength to dare,
	To shrink not from danger near.

She can do much, in joy or pain,
	To cheer, to comfort and bless;
Can make sunshine brighter, bring hope again
	To the heart in its sore distress.

And the little ones in the household band,
	So helpless, so loving and dear,
They seem a gift from some fairy land,
	Sent unto mortals here.

Dark is the home with no baby face,
	No pattering feet, no clinging hand;
In every true heart their power we trace,
	As they lead to the Better Land.

Then, let no one think there is naught to do;
	Seek and ye shall find.
Cast in your lot with the good and true,
	And work to save mankind.
					Hope.
		[signature on document] Sarah E. Russell


[Column 1]
		MARGARET FULLER OSSOLI.
	In the present day educational advancement
among women is so common, and their oppor-
tunities for learning are so general and excel-
lent, that when some bright star like H. B.
Stowe, Julia Ward Howe, Miss Woolson,
Adelaide Proctor, or Helen Hunt Jackson,
beams forth and illumines the pages of our
literary life with her sparkling effulgence,
little wonder is shown; we read, we criticise,
and we admire; we behold another name added
to the immortal list in much the same manner
as the calm astronomer beholds the appearance
of a new planet. It is there in the firmament,
shining with the others; it pleases, but causes
neither wonder nor surprise.
	Things are now changed; only a few years
since they were very different. The highest
privilege accorded womankind was but a
meager education, with a few accomplishments,
such as dancing, music, and a little French
added, for the benefit of the social circle, and
the prejudice against further advancement for
women ran so high that few dared venture be-
yond the allotted line and cultivate with higher
learning, the rich and fertile intellect, and feed
with knowledge, the hungry, starving soul.
Now, when annexed to the finest colleges in
the world, are schools for women, when women
own and conduct such colleges as Vassar, for
the sole purpose of the higher education of
their sex, when they own and publish promin-
ent newspapers, and are gladly admitted as
contributors to the finest magazines of the age,
when every little town can boast its "Ladies'
Literary Club," do we stop to consider how
much is due those few brave souls, that pierced
the veil of prejudice and paved the way which
led to this glorious result? Foremost among
these daring women stands the subject of this
sketch, a rare and wonderful woman—Margaret
Fuller Ossoli. Often, as we observe some vain
woman seeking fame, we think what a mistake
her life is; how beautiful it might have been in
the sphere of home; there she would have
been a queen, while in seeking for public notice
in the world, she is lost. This idea must be re-
versed with Margaret Fuller, for had the
world to which she rightfully belonged seized
her from the narrow, binding home, which
sacrificed her very life to its little exacting self,
it would possess, among its literary gems, the 
purest and loveliest of Pearls.
	It is, indeed, sad to behold her struggles,

[Column 2]
ever reaching, ever aspiring for refreshing
draughts from the cup of knowledge, and as
constantly being drawn back and bound to the
rack of sacrifice; yet, with all her longings
for knowledge, and aspirations to make a bene-
ficial use of her talents, in no way did she ne-
glect those homelier duties her home required,
but with a truly noble grace bore all her bur-
dens. Those who knew her best describe her
as "equal to doing everything at once," and it
was known, that she could rock a cradle, read a
book, eat an apple and knit a stocking all at
the same time.
	Margaret Fuller was born May 23rd, 1810,
in Cambridge, Mass. Her father was a gradu-
ate of Harvard, highly cultivated, but stern,
proud and exacting; full of candor, independ-
ance and self-conceit. Her mother, beautiful
as a flower, but also as delicate and frail. The
daughter, resembling more the father, still
possessed the mother's sweet disposition.
	And perhaps it was due as much to her
training as her nature, that she possessed so
many of her father's traits, for from her early
childhood he conducted her education and took
full charge of her outward life. It was he
who decided what dresses she should wear, and
what society she should cultivate. Her educa-
tion was carried on by a "forcing process;" at
six she studied Latin, and from that time on
was forced through all the harder and most
difficult tasks of learning. Added to such ex-
treme mental training, an unconscious neglect
of her health, her constitution was undermined
and ruined, a fact which she in later years felt
deeply, for she wrote, "I cannot help mourning,
sometimes, that my bodily life should have
been so destroyed by the ignorance of both
my parents." Still, this hard student life
could not have been disagreeable to her, for
later, when she had the management of her
own time in her hands, we read of her as active
and busy as when under her father's tutelage.
At the age of fifteen, after two years diligent
study at a private school in Groton, she de-
voted herself more assiduously than ever. Her
time was systematically employed; she rose
at five in summer, walked an hour, practiced
an hour on the piano, breakfasted at seven,
read "Sismondi's European Literature" in
French till eight, then "Brown's Philosophy"
till half past nine, went to school for Greek at
twelve, and then practiced again till dinner.
After the early dinner, she read two hours in
Italian, and the remaining part of the day
devoted to the family until eleven, when she
retired to write in her diary. Such a busy life
is indeed wonderful in any one, much more
so in a girl at the age of fifteen and sixteen, a
time when most girls prefer amusement to
study.
	It would be the highest pleasure to trace her
life from this time on, and repeat the many
bright, touching and interesting anecdotes con-
nected with her history, as we read them in her
biography, but here we have only space to note
the main issues of her life, and must pass
carelessly by her interesting girlhood at Cam-
bridge, where she studied in the same classes
with Dr. Oliver Wendell Holmes and Richard
Henry Dana, assisted her father in the enter-
tainment of President John Quincy Adams,
and reigned a queen in the society of the class
of 1829, said to be the most eminent class that
ever left Harvard, including, as well as the
two before mentioned, William Henry Chan-
ning, James Freeman Clarke, and others of


[Column 1]
equal note; also only glance at her life at
Groten, where the family moved in 1833. It
was there her father died, and there, also, be-
gan that never ending series of gentle sacrifices,
which so enrich, yet sadden her life’s history.
Cold and stern as this father had been, she felt
the fullest appreciation for his devotion to her
education; and though she missed, as a child,
that innate tenderness a father usually be-
stows on his children. in later years she realized
his true worth, and attributed his austere and
restrained manner more to the nature inherited
from a race of proud and opinionated men,
than to a want of an affection for herself. She
maintained herself and educated her brothers
and sisters principally by school teaching in
Providence and Boston. Whatever her task,
she worked laboriously and well, and left 
nothing unfinished. 	
	When men and women grown, these brothers
and sisters needed her aid no longer, and she
was free to select her own course, to begin to
live her true and perfect life, we behold her no
longer young, vivacious and able, in all respects,
to launch forth in her true sphere, the critical,
exacting, but loved literary world, but in
health, completely broken down, and impover-
ished in wealth, still rich in the posses-
sions of a well stored mind, and of
firm friends and admirers, such as
Ralph Waldo, Emerson, Elizabeth Hoar
Horace Greeley, Dr. Holmes, Nathaniel Haw-
thorne, Theodore Parker, and many, many
others of the leading minds of the age. It
must have been a woman of great ability who
could found the first literary club in America,
and conduct with ease and grace conversations
on such subjects as only interest philosophers
and the deepest thinkers. Yet Margaret
Fuller did this, and more. She was a reformer;
she wasted no time in "small talk," but inter-
ested in the leading subjects of the world the
leading minds. To think well, and to speak
understandingly leads to a desire to advance
thoughts to the universe, and out of the literary
club grew The Dial, the first really literary
magazine on this side of the Atlantic, and
Miss Fuller was given the position of Editor.
The magazine was a quarterly, and though it
claimed as contributors names which now hold
the highest rank in American literature, it
lived only four short years. Its early death
was certainly due more to an uneducated, un-
appreciative public than to a want of genuine
worth.
	After the beginning of The Dial Margaret
Fuller’s literary career was fully assured, and
from that time many rich and perfect gems of
thought have delighted the literary world.
She was a great lover of German literature,
and we are indebted to her for some of the
finest translations of Goethe and Eckermann.
She assisted Horace Greeley in 1844 in editing
the New York Tribune, and won great praise
for her literary criticisms and philanthropic
discussions. After nearly two year’s labor on
this newspaper, Miss Fuller realized the fulfil-
ment of one of her early dreams—a journey
to Europe. She was charmed with her visit to
England, where she was most hospitably re-
ceived, and had the pleasure of meeting so
many prominent people, among these, Words-
worth, Dr. Chalmers, De Quincy, Johanna
Baillie and Carlyle.
	After a pleasant and most interesting sojourn
in Paris, she hastened to Italy, the country
which had always held for her, beyond all
others, the greatest charm, and, though she
knew it not, her destiny as well. Here all the
fullness of her feminine soul found a sweet,
abiding place, and we behold Margaret Fuller
in the sweet aspect of a wife and mother. A
strange love story ’hers, but simple and beauti-
ful in the extreme. Her life in Italy was full
of adventure and saddening phases, but it was
crowned with such a perfect love, that her days
there were certainly the happiest of her life.

[Column Two]
She met the Marquis Ossoli at St. Peters, while
searching for some missing friends; he,observing
her discomfort, assisted her, and, failing to find
her friends, accompanied he home. After this
first chance meeting, the Marquis was fre-
quently seen to pass up and down before the
house, and slowly a more familiar acquaintance
began, which finally ended in avowal of
his love, and an offer of his hand in marriage,
which she at first declined; not because she
lacked in affection for him, but she scarcely
dared think him in earnest; and many have
wondered at so strange a union. He a young,
handsome, Italian soldier, of high rank, and
she an American lady of thirty-seven years,
plain, literary and poor; but one would never
doubt his sincerity after a perusal of the letters
exchanged between them, the only records
saved of her Italian life save a few reminisences
of personal friends, who describe him as his
wife’s adorer, and her married life as complete.
The exact date of her marriage is not known,
but her babe was born September 5th, 1848.
She was in Italy during the exciting time of
the Revolution, and her husband bore arms
on the side of the Republic, against family
and friends. She performed a most noble part
in influencing her friends on the people’s side,
and devoted herself, during these trying times,
to nursing in the hospitals. Always a leader,
—in mind and the literary field a George Eliot;
in the hospitals and battlefield, a Florence
Nightingale; in her home and heart, a wife
and mother—such as her own sweet mother had
been before her. Just at the sublime moment
when her future looked so bright, how sad that
the end of all should come.
	She had strange forebodings concerning her
homeward voyage, and greatly dreaded the un-
dertaking, but they finally sailed May 17th,
1850, from Leghorn, and after a long, periious
and unpleasant journey (the captain having
died of small pox, and her own [----] child
only saved from the same fate by the most ten-
der watchfulness) they came in sight of the
shores of her "own America," only to lose
sight of them forever more. On July 19th,
at four o’clock, the vessel was wrecked off the
coast of New Jersey, and the Ossolis found in
each others arms an ocean grave. With true
love for her little one’s life, she wrung herself
from its tiny clasp and consigned him to the
steward, with the hope that his life, at least,
might be saved from the wreck, and borne to
her friends at home, but, alas! when the shore
was reached, his little life also had gone to
that other, higher home, and all the friends re-
ceived of their dear Margaret was the lifeless
body of her boy, and the touching letters
written by herself and husband.
	It was a most tragic end, but her life, though
short, was well spent, and we can truly say,
"She did not live in vain."
					A. W. C.
				[signature on document] Annie W. Cannon


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


LETTER FROM OHIO.

DEAR MRS. WELLS:
	I have been mad, and sulking all this time,
because you did not come to see me when you
went to Washington; but, as the children say,
" 'Taint no fun to be mad at a person. if the
person don’t know you're made," so I now
write to tell you; and to tell you, too, how
glad my heart was to-day when the EXPONENT
came. I had been thinking of you all, and
wondering if the Edmunds-Tucker bill would
really affect your daily lives or not, feeling
sorry at the turn affairs had taken, and wishing
to know just how you felt, when here comes
the little messenger from you, containing:
"My Christmas Gift," "A New Year's Visit,"
"Candies and Creams," Views on Celestial 
Marriage," "Singing Lessons," "A New Hall
Opened," "Fragrant Leaves," "A Tribute of


Love,” “Various Qualifications,” “The Sequel,”
etc. How well acquainted I feel with dear
Helen Mar Whitney, Augusta Joyce Crocheron,
Sister Roxie and many others. Hannah T.
King—I never in life saw her face, never
heard the sound of her voice, yet when her
life came to an end, I felt the deep loss of a
friend.
	It seems to me that this agitation will adver-
tise your people widely to the nations,
		“Who'll learn the truth and real worth
		Of Saints who’ve borne oppression."
One of the G. A. R. ladies, who went with
the excursionists to California, said to me the
other day, “Come and spend the day with me,
and I’ll tell you all about my visit to Salt Lake
City.” “I will; and then I shall write to the
Mormons and tell them what you say about
them,” I answered. “Well, I cannot say any-
thing bad about them,” she said. She passed
by the Exponent office, and now regrets that
she did not pay you a visit.
	I, and many others, watch every phase of
the great Mormon issue with the greatest inter-
est. Whether Mormonism is to be, or is not
to be, is a question of vital importance.
	Wishing you well, and God speed in all
that is right, and asking God to preserve and
protect the good and innocent among you, I
remain,
	Your friend,
		Emily Scott.
Jan. 18, 1887.


PROTESTS AGAINST THE DISFRAN-
	CHISEMENT OF WOMEN.
	
	Hon John D. Long deserves the special
thanks of Massachusetts women for his effort
to have a separate veto taken upon that clause
of the anti-Mormon bill which disfranchises
the non-polygamous and Gentile women of
Utah.

	Woman Suffragists everywhere, both indi-
vidually and as organizations, should write at
once to the President, asking him to veto the 
Utah bill which disfranchises the non-polyga-
mous women of Utah. To punish the inno-
cent for the guilty, and to deprive thousands
of American citizens not convicted of crime
of the right of self-government, is the acme of
political injustice.

	One clause of the anti-Mormon bill which
passed the U. S. House of Representatives this
week, and is still pending, will, if passed, dis-
franchise the women of Utah. As has often
been pointed out in the Woman's Journal, this
measure is objectionable, for two reasons.
First, it is unjust; second it is useless. It is
useless, because the male population of the
Territory is overwhelmingly Mormon, and after
 the women are disfranchised the Mormons will
still have a political majority of nearly three
to one. If taking away the votes of the women
would give the Gentiles a majority,there would
be some inducement to do it; but no one claims
that it can make any practical difference in
the result of elections Hence it is a purely
gratuitous wrong. The measure is unjust, be-
cause it decrees an unequal penalty for an
equal crime; or, rather, it inflicts a gratuitous
penalty for no crime at all.	*	*	*
*	*	*	Some years ago, an ignorant
English peasant was convicted of selling his
wife to another ignorant man for a few shill-
ings and a pot of beer. The judge before
whom the parties were brought sent the
woman to prison, in order, as he said, to show
the men that the majesty of the law must be
respected. This is not the first time that
women have been punished to teach a lesson to
men; but it ought to be the last.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


RETRIBUTION.

	The olden homes, of peace and pride,
		Are left without protection now,
	And grief is felt on every side,
		While sorrow clouds the mother's brow;
	The precious one she loved so well
		Is banished from her fond embrace,
	And weeping children often tell
		How much they miss a father's face.

	One meek Apostle of the Lamb,
		In bondage with the murderer cast,
	Will soon surmount the tyrant's sham,
		And gaze with pleasure on the past.
	Upon his head there shall be set
		A crown of glory, bright and fair,
	For Christ will mark His jewels yet,
		And with His hand shall place them there

	How God-like is the free born will,
		Which purest liberty doth show
	And shackles forged by human skill
		Can never bind or bring it low.
	The noble souls, so tried and true,
		Eternal liberty shall own,
	And with the firm and faithful few,
		Shall kneel before the Father's throne.
	
	Our lovely, peaceful mountain home,
		Is blighted by the despot's sway,
	While fiends incarnate, fearless roam,
		To make her noble sons their prey.
	The sickly babe's or mother's tear
		Falls heedless on their hardened Hearts;
	They know no pity, love nor fear-
		Theirs yet shall be a bitter part.
	
	A day of retribution yet
		Upon their hardened hearts will fall;
	For them a judgment will be set,
		And soon upon the rocks they'll call
	To hide  them from the face of Him
		Who comes in glory, power and might,
	To smite the wicked in their sin,
		And put His enemies to flight.
						Jeanette Paton.


DON'T WAIT.

		L.M. HEWLINGS.

Don't wait until I'm gone, dearest
	Before you tribute pay,
Till the spirit has departed, 
	And left but lifeless clay;
Do not wait till all is over,
	And then, with mournful lay,
In a voice of trembling sorrow,
	Regret the long delay.

Do not tarry, but remember
	That time is on the wing;
That long since it brought me over
	The budding days of Spring,
Through blossoming of Summer,
	With Autumn's fruit and sheaves
Where the naked branches quiver,
	And sigh o'er withered leaves.

Where the heavy frosts of Winter
	Turn the green and gold to gray,
And crown the heads once radiant
	With wreathes of silver spray,
Scattering snowflakes thickly
	Round temples that were fair,
And broad furrows deeply planting,
	On freshly tablets rare.

O'er stateliest form recrossing,
	Till it must bend and bow,
Weaving lines of finest tracing
	O'er once fair cheek and brow.
Ah! vainly we pause to question,
	Ask swifter speed, or stay;
Time will ne'er heed love's entreating
	To hasten or delay.

Silently bearing us onward,
	Nearing the bound'ry line-
A world is too poor to purchase
	E'en one moment of time.


Don't wait, the end is approaching,
	The years will soon be told; 
The silver cord will be loosened,
	Broken the golden bowl.

Don't wait till the lightning message
	Speeds through the startled air,
To wing forth the cry of anguish,
	Strike with a dumb despair,
While you strain your eyes with weeping
	Over the lifeless clay,
In vain for one word of greeting
	From lips now closed for aye.


A HUMBLE TESTIMONY.

	It seems that the time has fully come when
the daughters of Zion are called upon to raise
their voices and declare unto the nation and
the world the testimonies which God has given
them of His work in these last days. I believe
that a sense of duty, prompted by the Holy
Spirit, is resting upon many of our faithful
sisters, to not only speak in their meetings, and
bear testimony to the truth of the Gospel, but
to write and send it abroad in the world, that
all mankind may fully understand that they
are united with their husbands in bringing
about the glorious plan of salvation.
	When I read in the EXPONENT the humble
testimonies of my dear sisters, I know what
they say is true, for the same spirit has revealed
the same to me. I claim no praise or honor
for having submitted to the law of celestial or
plural marriage. I could not do otherwise, for
the simple reason that, through faith in God,
and prayer, I received a testimony that it was
true; and this was the testimony: “That it will
exalt the human family to a joy and glory that
they cannot attain to in any other way.”
	Soon after receiving this testimony I was
shown in plainness that I was called upon to
enter into that order. I informed my husband
of this fact; he said he had no idea who it
would be that he would ask to come into our
family; he was afraid of hurting my feelings,
and was also fearful that he could not do right.
He was afraid of his own weakness.
	One month had not passed since this time
until we found ourselves living in this order of
marriage with a young woman that neither of
us had seen since she was about seven years of
age. Surprised as I was, I can truly testify
that it was the happiest announcement that
ever came to my ears. Yet it was not I—
poor, weak, frail human being—that could as-
sure happiness upon such an occasion; but that
same spirit which had given me the testimony
also gave me joy and happiness, which the
world knows not of. Did I think less of my
husband? I can say, no! but, on the contrary,
I loved him more, for I felt that he had a true
heart, that he was willing to obey God’s laws,
and I said, “By the help of my Heavenly
Father I will help you to live according to the
law of God.” The woman he married, no one
can say aught against her; she is a true Latter-
day Saint, and many times have I felt to thank
my Heavenly Father for such a worthy, good
woman, and I have tried to make her happy,
and hope that I may always be found worthy
of the constant companionship of the Spirit of
God, for I know that He lives, and that His
hand has been over me from my earliest recol-
lections, and that He will help all those who
put their trust in Him, and I feel to say to all
those who are living in this holy order of mar-
riage, “Lift up your heads and rejoice, for you
are serving a good Master, who will repay in
His own due time, and you will rejoice and
your happiness will be sufficient for you, and
exceed all you ever anticipated.”
	Let women, who belong to the same family,
pray for one another; sustain your husbands; 
teach your children to respect each senior 

[Column Two]
member of the family; do not allow your
daughters to speak reproachfully of the sister-
wives, and God will bless you, and His Spirit
will be with you always, which will cause your
burden to seem light unto you, for God has
said that He would have a tried people; for
how will he know that they love Him above
all other things? We should acknowledge
His hand in our trials as well as in our joys.
We must learn to overcome all things that try
us here; if we do not, we will have them to
overcome when we find ourselves in another
sphere.
	I was once sorely tried, and I said to myself,
“I feel as though I could not go one step further
in this world, and I don’t care if I die!” Some-
thing instantly whispered, “If you cannot
overcome in this world, how do you expect to
in the next?” which caused me to stop and
think that I had better learn more patience
and try again. But how merciful is our Hea-
venly Father; if we could only be so merciful
to each other, and always be willing to forgive
—we must learn to forgive even our worst
enemies, to say nothing about those with whom
we expect to spend all the endless days of eter-
nity. The Lord has said, “Vengeance is mine,
and I will repay.” We should leave all
things in His hands; even all our troubles, if
we go to Him in the hour of need, He is able
to make us strong, and if we desire it, He will
give us wisdom, that we will be enabled by our
own actions to escape much trouble.
	Sisters, above all things, trust in God; have
faith, and cultivate and exercise patience, and
we will overcome, which is the desire of, 
			Your sister in the truth,
								PHENA.
	Jan. 18,1887.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	Miss Anthony has become the sole proprie-
tor of "The History of Woman Suffrage."

	Henry B.Stanton, the husband of Elizabeth
Cady Stanton, died this week at an advanced 
age.

	Miss Rose Elizabeth Cleveland has written a
narrative poem of 400 lines, which is called 
"The Dilemma of the Nineteenth Century,"
and treats on the woman question.  

	Dress reform is taken up with much earnest 
ness by the Scandinavian progressive women.
In Christiana, it is already taken up in prac- 
tice by some independent individuals. Eye
witnesses have declared that the Norwegian
reformed dress has the great advantage of es-
caping the spirit of mockery, because its char-
acter is of the greatest simplicity. In Stock-
holm is a Dress-Reform Association, and it has
offered a premium for hygienic boots and shoes
for women. Lectures about women's dress, 
the need of reform, and how to reform, are
frequently given by intelligent women and by 
humanitarian men physicians in the Scandi- 
navian countries.—Ex.	
	A new Portia, who defended, however, not
her lover’s friend, but her own father, has ap- 
peared at Montpelier, France. A farmer
living near that town had been tried and con- 
demned for breach of trust. He took an ap-
peal, which was granted, and on the day ap-
pointed for his hearing he walked into court
accompanied by his daughter, a tall, good-
looking, and intelligent young woman, about
twenty-six years old. To the surprise of the
judges, the young woman opened her father’s
case in a masterly manner, argued the defence 
with the sill of a practical lawyer, and finally
gained for her client a verdict of acquittal. 
She then walked triumphantly out of the
court, hanging on her father’s arm.—Ex.
