SALT LAKE CITY,	-	-	March 15, 1887.


[Column 1]
			COMMENTS. 

	Since it has been understood that the Edmunds-
Tucker bill had become a law by default, the
President of the United States withholding his
signature, the situation in Utah thus brought
about has furnished a topic for conversation, dis-
cussion and  argument,  in  many  places and
among friends and foes.  A municipal election
following so soon after in one of the principal
cities of the Territory, gave a good opportunity
for expression of sentiment at the polls.  It
seemed to be the opinion of prominent men op-
posed in their views to the veritable citizens of the
Territory, that the Latter-day Saints could not
possibly take the test oath, which was formulated
for them by members of the Utah Commission.
The sequel shows that a large number of them
could, and therefore the real design of the clique,
who manipulate things here and make represen-
tations against the Latter-day Saints to the Con-
gress of the United States, are again thwarted in
their design to gain possession of all the local
offices in the Territory, at least.  It may be that
many changes will follow the enforcement of this
cruel law; but it has been so modified that those
who labored and clamored for its passage are
greatly disappointed.  It has certainly been
robbed of some of its worst and most objectionable
features.
	The municipal election in Brigham City on
Monday, the 7th inst., was very satisfactory to the
people who have settled and built up the country.
To have put their city into the hands of a minority,
who are at bitter enmity with the majority of its
citizens, would have been deplorable indeed.  It
is a thriving, industrial town, and the people have
exerted themselves to make it beautiful as well,
and they have kept down elements that are calcu-
lated to destroy and lead away the young, but
since the so-called liberal element has shown
itself there, liquor saloons have been started, and
the influence of these has already made itself
apparent, and it is in this and other such ways,
that the young people of Utah are to be civilized
by those would-be reformers who are determined,
so they have stated, to crush out and kill Mor-
monism.
	The women of Utah who have exercised the
privilege of the elective franchise for seventeen
years are, through the passage of this bill, excluded
from the polls, and without any excuse whatever,
simply because they are women, not for any real
reason for no complaint has ever been made
against the manner in which they have used this
right, bestowed upon them by the Legislature of
the Territory, except that the liberals have said
they voted as they were told, and voted with their
husbands, and this they call a misuse of the ballot.
The ballot is understood to be a free expression of
one’s political opinion, and in Utah, where for
many years the ballot has been a secret one, there
could be no reason for any one voting in any
other way, except as the person’s own intelligence

[Column 2]
dictated, whether man or woman.  The writer is
well acquainted with the exercise of the elective
franchise in the hands of the women of Utah,
knowing, perhaps, as many women interested in
the suffrage for woman as any other person in the
Territory,having been for quite a number of years
vice-President for Utah of the National Woman’s
Suffrage Association, and practically interested
in affairs of that kind, and the advancement and
enfranchisement of women everywhere.  And
having been one of the three women named in a
petition presented to the courts here against
women voting, and trying “to compel the assessor
and register of voters to erase and strike from
the list of voters the names of Emmeline B. Wells,
Maria M. Blythe and A. G. Paddock, and also
the names of all women whose names thereon ap-
pear on the aforesaid list.” etc.  This case was
decided in favor of women and the names of
women were continued upon the voters’ lists and
women were allowed to vote at any and all elec-
tions in this Territory until the passage of the
Edmund-Tucker bill.  At the municipal election
in Brigham City,Monday, the 17th, two ladies pre-
sented votes and were rejected.  The case will
doubtless be carried through the courts, at any
rate it ought to be, and ascertain whether even
Congress can take the suffrage from any class of
citizens who have legally held it, and who have
not been accused of any misuse of the privilege.
To deprive women who have been voters of the
ballot is just as wrong as it would be to deprive
men, at least we see no reason why such is not the
case, and to submit to the indignity without pro-
testing and defending the position would not be
consistent with the courage and independence
manifested by the women of Utah in the past.  If
Congress can take the ballot from the women of
this Territory it can take it from the women of
Wyoming, and there is no telling how soon they
might attempt it.
	The women who assembled in convention in
Washington, during the month of February, made
a vigorous and forcible protest against the action
of Congress in the matter, and also presented a
memorial to the President of the United States,
urging him to veto that particular clause of the
Bill which disfranchised women voters, which we
publish in full in this issue.
	The restoration of the dower, about which so
many law-makers and pretended friends of all
women have said so much, is a positive injustice
to the women of Utah, and cannot be too severely
deprecated, as it prevents any provision being
made for plural wives and children, and would
place women, who have entered into the most
sacred covenants of marriage, from the strongest
religious convictions, on a level, so far as it is
possible for them to do it, with that unfortunate
class of women in the world whom men dishonor
and then cast off at their pleasure.
	It is true, beyond contradiction, whatever the
world may think, that “Mormon” women know
what they want  better than these regenerators,
either men or women, can tell them, and as for 
suffrage and rights, they know what they are en-
titled to, and will and can defend their own posi-
tion.  As for the principle of plural marriage, they
have steadily maintained their convictions in re-
gard to it, and all the persecution brought to bear
will not shake the confidence they have in its di-
vine authority, nor can it take away the belief from
any one, whatever may be done or legislated
against its practice.  Hindering women from vot-
ing will not help to abolish the principle, for they
will have no promise to make and no oath to take,
and consequently can talk all they like, and there
will be no restraint; thus it is that in everything
almost that the enemies of this people seek to ac-
complish, they thwart their own purposes, and by
and bye they will be caught in the meshes of the
net they have set to catch the Latter-day Saints.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	WE are pleased to be able to announce that the
picture of Lucy Smith, the mother of the Prophet
Joseph, has just been received (yesterday, the 14th
inst.), and copies will be sent as soon as practica-
ble. Persons entitled to them, living near, had
better call for them, as it will save rolling and
perhaps creasing them.

	WE have received, by courtesy of the respective
members of Congress who made them, several in-
teresting and excellent speeches. Senator H. W.
Blair of New Hampshire, on Woman's Suffrage,
December 8, 1886, presenting logical and, one 
would think, conclusive arguments in favor of the 
enfranchisement of women. Senator Joseph E.
Brown of Georgia, on the same subject, taking
the opposite view of the matter, and presenting
all those beautiful and flowery reasons that a re-
fined and thoroughly cultivated Southern gentle-
man is accustomed to use in favor of woman's 
delicacy and incapacity for public duties. If
every woman had a husband like Senator Brown,
perhaps this question would never have been
raised, but women as well as men have to fight
the battle of life, and therefore they need the pro-
tection of the ballot and courage to stand, and
such practical education as will prepare them to
defend their rights, politically, and stand equal
with man as well as educationally. The stirring
and timely speech of the Delegate from Utah, of
which mention has been made before in our
columns, and the minority report in favor of the 
resolution asking for a constitutional amendment
providing for woman's suffrage. This minority
report is signed by members of the Judiciary
Committee of the House, E. B. Taylor, W. P.
Hepburn, L. B. Caswell, and also A. A. Ranney,
who, although not one of the Committee who pre-
pared the report, adds his name below and says,
"I concur in the opinion of the minority, that the
resolution ought to be adopted."


HENRY WARD BEECHER PASSES
	AWAY.

	New York, March 8, 9:45 a.m.—Beecher is
dead.
		DEATH-BED DETAILS.
	New York, March 7, midnight.—A message
just received from Brooklyn says that a very de-
cided change for the worse has taken place in the
condition of Beecher, and that the members of 
his family are gathered about his bedside.
	Many telegrams were received during the day,
but none of them have been opened.
	10 o.clock p.m.—Mr. Beecher is steadily failing,
but we do not think he will die to-night.
					W. S. SEARLE, M. D.

	At 10:30 the house was closed for the night.
	A meeting of parishoners of Plymouth Church
was held in the lecture room commencing at 8 p.
m.  The room was crowded with male and female
members of  the  congregation and  not a few
friends from other churches, and there was scarce-
ly a dry eye to be seen from the beginning to the
end of the proceedings. Mr. Tilney presided and
Rev. Dr. Hallidie opened the meeting with a
moving prayer, in which he decided that their
beloved pastor might not even be allowed to
seem to suffer, but that he must speedily attain
unto that rest prepared for him. After singing a
hymn, Jackson offered prayer and the chairman
then read a portion of Scripture commencing:
“Finally, beloved, be strong in the Lord and in the
power of His might, which is able to keep you
from failing,” etc.
	It seemed as if those words were a message to
him who was leaving them, because it seemed as
though he were a born warrior. In all the past


[Column 1]﻿
years he had to fight and struggle; there had al-
ways been something for them to battle with and
he had fought the fight.  There has never been
any flinching, never any turning back.
	The following letter from Mrs. Beecher was
read:  "To the beloved members of Plymouth
Church:  I cannot speak my thanks to each and
every one of you for the sympathy and love and
devotion manifested in these last days, sad days
for your pastor.  He can no longer speak to you
for himself.  Then permit me to do it for him, for
myself, for my family.  Each word of love from 
the people so dear to your pastor’s heart has been
a comfort and balm to a heart wrung almost to
bursting.  How he loved his church you will only
learn in its fulness when you stand with him in
heaven.  To each and every one of you, my earn-
est thanks and most earnest blessiug.  Pray for
his wife and children soon to lose their truest
companion and tenderest father, and if we must
relinquish all possible hope of his full recovery,
oh,  beloved,  pray  that  his  departure may be
speedy, that we may not be long agonized by this
struggle  between  life  and death.  Pray, if he
must leave us, that before another day dawns he
may receive his crown and be forever with his
Lord.
		(Signed)		EUNICE N. BEECHER.
	New York, March 8.—Henry Ward Beecher
began  to  sink  slowly after midnight  and the
watchers at his bedside soon saw that the end was
near.  Beecher passed quietly away while asleep;
he never recovered consciousness after the paraly-
sis stupefied his mind.
			BEECEER'S WILL.
	In the name of God, amen!  I, Henry Ward
Beecher, of the City of Brooklyn and State of
New York, hereby revoking all other and former
wills by me heretofore made, do make, publish
and declare this to be my last will and tastament.
	First—I hereby authorize and direct my execu-
tors, or such of them as shall qualify at my death,
to collect and receive the amount of my life in-
surance, to invest the same and pay the proceeds
of such investment to my wife during her life in
equal quarterly yearly payments.
	Two—I hereby give, bequeath and devise unto
my executors, or such of them as shall qualify, the
net residue and remainder of my estate, both per-
sonal and real of every kind, in trust for the bene-
fit of my children, and I hereby direct that my
said executors distribute and apportion my said
estate among my said children in such manner
and form, and at such time or times as shall, in
their judgment, be  for  the best interests of my
said children, giving unto my said executors full
power to sell and mortgage such and so much of
my real and personal property as they shall deem
best, and to invest or distribute the proceeds of
such sale or sales as herein provided.
	Third—It is my will that if any of my said
children should die before the complete distribu-
tion of my estate, as above provided, their children
shall stand and take in place and stead of the said
parents, taking per stripes and not per capita.
	Fourth— I hereby nominate, constitute and ap-
point my sons, Henry B. Beecher, William C.
Beecher and Herbert A. Beecher, all of Brooklyn,
New York, and my son-in-law, Bev. Samuel Sco-
ville, of Norwich, New York, executors and
trustees of this my will, and it is my will that no
bond shall be required of them or either of them.
	July 11th, 1878.
		(Signed)		HENRY WARD BEECHER.
	The witnesses are A. S. Barnes and G. D.
Blake of Brooklyn.
				THE FUNERAL.
	New York, March 12.—The remains of the late
Rev. Henry Ward Beecher were taken from Ply-
mouth church at 8:30 this morning and conveyed

[Column 2]
to Greenwood cemetery, where they were deposited
in the large receiving vault, where they will re-
main until the family select a lot for final interment.
All night long the church was guarded by Ply-
mouth Company G, and at 7 this morning the re-
mains were turned over to the Plymouth church
committee, who had charge of the burial.  Only
the family and close personal friends of the de-
ceased went out to the cemetery.  The funeral 
cortege proceeded from the church to Greenwood
cemetery by the shortest route.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HONOR TO WOHM HONOR IS DUE.
	
	I wish to place in your valuable paper a few
lines on the death of William Anthol McMaster,
who departed this life January 22nd, 1887.
	I was first acquainted with him in England,
when he was on a mission. I prayed the Lord if
He had a servant of His who had the power to
cast out devils and heal the sick to send such an
one to our house, in the City of Exeter. Bro.
McMaster was sent in answer to my prayers, and
stayed with us six days. My husband had been
out of his mind for some time, and sick. When
Bro. McMaster came into our door, the evil spirits
went out and left my husband in his right mind.
Bro. McMaster baptized my husband, a young
lady that was living with me, and myself in
Exminster Sea, and from that time I have rejoiced
in the Gospel of God. The other two have been
cut off from the Church.
	I know Bro. McMaster to have been kind to
the poor and needy ones, and always on hand to
administer to the sick, both by night and by day if
required. He had the gift of healing. I have
been administered to by him many times, and al-
ways obtained immediate relief.
	God bless his remains and crown him with
eternal joys. My sincere prayer is for the Lord to
bless his family.
						MARIA B. FORD.


WOMAN’S VOICE.

EDITOR WOMEN'S EXPONENT:	
	I thought I would try to write a few lines for
our valuable little paper, which is ever welcome,
bringing with it so much encouragement to the
sad and weary mothers, sisters and daughters in
Israel.
	I feel that it is necessary at this very eventful
and trying day that we should stand by one
another, and give each other all the encourage-
ment possible, and that we each one seek earnestly
to our Heavenly Father for His Holy Spirit to be
our constant guide, or we may be led astray at
any time, for we do not understand the ways of
God only by the Spirit of God; His ways are
higher than man’s ways, as the heavens are above
the earth; but He has said, “Take my yoke upon
you and my burden, for it is light.” But, dear
sisters, His must be the whole burden or it may
be very heavy; more than we can bear.  Let us
be sincere and honest in all our thoughts, words
and works, that we may gain little by little, and
eventually overcome our follies, and prove our-
selves worthy of the Spirit of God to be our con-
stant guide, then we cannot go astray.
	It has been foretold that we should be tried and
tested like gold seven times in the furnace, if we
were worthy. Oh! let us try to be worthy and
willing for the dross to be consumed and the gold
to be refined. 
	Let us acknowledge the hand of the Lord in all
our trials, and learn some lessons of importance
from the same, is the prayer of your unworthy
sister,		
					E. J.
	Kane Co., Utah


OUR DUTY AS "DAUGHTERS OF
		ZION."

	Giving precedence to that which comes first by
nature and time, I would refer to the duty of a
daughter in her parents' house. God has sent us
all forth on a noble mission, and endowed us with
the requisite gifts whereby we can perform that
mission faithfully, and work out for ourselves an
exaltation, which will qualify us to dwell with His
holy ones in the eternal worlds.
	It is for us as women to see that the talents with
which we are endowed are not allowed to rust.
Filial love and respect are the qualities we should
first cultivate, to lighten a mother's burden, with-
out usurping her authority; to help as much as
possible, without displacing her; to secure for
her the ease, which years spent in toil and anxiety
for us render necessary, without making her feel
that she is no longer needed and her counsel un-
heeded; to obey our father as the head of the
household; to anticipate his wants, and be ever
ready to fulfil a daughter's duty.
	An excellent guide to a girl's character and dis-
position can be ascertained by her manner in the
family circle. If eyes brighten at her approach,
and voices welcome her coming, we may feel sure
that she is beloved; but if voices are hushed and
no interest is taken in her when she enters the
room, there is a feeling of suspicion that rises in
our minds as to whether she is doing her duty, or
acting in such a manner as to win love and confi-
dence in the sphere in which she is placed. A
woman's especial place is home. The very men-
tion of the word home calls up recollections of
our mothers or sisters, and the refined influences
that women exert there takes hold of the most
hardened and coarse natures, and makes us feel
truly there is "no place like home." Who is it
that has not felt at some time or other of his
life the sweet restraining power that the gentler
sex know so well how to use, and which will move
a strong will from the path of error, and by her
persuasion and gentleness, will be the good angel
of men's lives. In order to exert this influence it
is requisite for a woman to know all things that
pertain to the comfort of those around her. There
is an idea prevalent among the young ladies of
to-day, that housework is a far too degrading
thing to do, and to betray even a slight knowledge
of it is "exceedingly bad form;" but what an igno-
rant idea. What harm can it do a girl to learn how 
to cook, clean, sew, darn, etc.?	*	*	That
which constitutes our degradation is not when w
fill our proper place, but when we seek to fill some
other place than that which God and nature has
designed and appointed for us. And does not
that book, which we all profess to revere, give
greatest honor to the industrious? The women
that are held up as patterns are not the idle and
useless, but those who worked with both hand
and head. Our Savior's praise of our sex
was, "She hath done what she could."
	If some are fortunate enough to be able to em-
ploy servants to do the menial work for them, a
knowledge of the way it should be done will al-
ways win them the respect of those they employ,
and if, in this way, we are ever called to fulfil our
mission as a wife, we shall be able to materially
look after our husband's interests in that dearest
of all places, "home."
	Though we may not all be under the necessity
of engaging in such duties now, we ought, as a
matter of choice, to learn how to do them, for the
purpose of fitting ourselves for what we may re-
quire. Let no foolish notion, that work is degrad-
ing, hinder us from doing our duty. There are
more of the truly noble among those who work
for their daily bread, than among the highest
of the nobility of the land.
	There are many duties we miss performing be-
cause we want to do so much more than there is
any necessity for; we are so apt to look to the top of


the ladder before we put our foot on the first step,
and to aspire to be so great before we learn to
live in a humbler way.	*	*	*	*	*
	Let us, then, be determined not to listen to 
those who would turn us aside from a work which 
even angels might covet, and from a noble fulfil-
ment of a mission that alone constitutes our true
glory.
				MARION SCOLES.
	London, England.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS.

BEAVER STAKE.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The Relief Society of this Stake met in 
conference March 3rd, pursuant to adjourn-
ment, in the Latter-day Saints meeting house
of this place, Prest. Lucinda Howd presiding.
There was a good representation of officers and
members from the different wards; Prest. Mur-
dock and others of the brethren were also
present. The reports showed a healthy and
prosperous condition of the various branches.
	An excellent spirit prevailed, and the re-
marks of the sisters and those of the brethren
who were present, were replete with good coun-
sel and instruction. The proper training of
the young, the storing of grain and the inter-
ests of the EXPONENT were some of the sub-
jects dwelt upon. All those who spoke mani-
fested a firm reliance in our Heavenly
Father at the present crisis in our affairs, and
a determination to uphold all the principles 
which God has revealed, even unto death, if
necessary.
	The conference was adjourned for six months,
to meet at Adamsville. Benediction by Bro.
Daniel Tyler, this closing what was universally
felt to be the best conference of the sisters ever
held in Beaver Stake.
		Respectfully,
		LOUISA JONES, Sec. pro tem.

MOAB WARD, SAN JUAN STAKE.

	Report of the annual meeting of the Relief
Society of Moab Ward, San Juan Stake of
Zion, Emery County, Utah, held Feb. 19th,
1887, Prest. Sarah Burnham presiding.
Opened by singing, and prayer by Bishop R.
H. Stewart. After the reading of the minutes
of last meeting, the Secretary read the annual
report, which was satisfactory.
	Prest Burnham called upon Bishop R. H,
Stewart to address the meeting. He felt
pleased to meet with the sisters, and to con-
gratulate them upon the improvement that
was manifest among them; also felt to rejoice
because of the good spirit prevailing. Prayed
God to bless the Relief Society of Moab, and
gave some valuable instructions relative to the
various duties devolving upon the members as
mothers in Israel, and exhorted them to faith-
fulness in the discharge of the many require-
ments required of them. They should be hum-
ble and prayerful, and be united among them-
selves, etc.
	Bro. Edward Bolton said he was glad to 
meet with the Relief Society, and felt to en-
courage the sisters to go forward in their great
and good work. Was pleased to see the spirit
of unity apparent among them, and had no
doubts as to the improvements manifest. Com-
mented upon the importance of the Relief So-
ciety, as an able adjunct in building up the 
kingdom of God. Upon the mothers devolved
an enormous responsibility—the training of
the young was mostly in their hands; therefore,
they should seek for wisdom to enable them to
discharge their duties faithfully. They should
be united and sustain their President and all
placed in authority over them. Prayed God
to abundantly bless the Relief Society through-
out the various Stakes of Zion.

[Column 2]
	Prest. Burnham was glad to see so many of
the sisters together, and felt to endorse the 
many good counsels and instructions given by
the brethren. Spoke upon the necessity of
union combined with humility, and a) prayer-
ful spirit, etc., etc.
	Singing. Benediction by Brother Edward
Bolton.
				CLARA BURNHAM, Sec.


[Column 1]		
	ITEMS FROM ARIZONA.

MRS. EMMELINE B. WELLS:

	DEAR SISTER:—It gives me pleasure to send
you a few lines from Arizona.
	We have been very greatly blessed, for
which we are very thankful. We have had
a very beautiful winter in the "sunny south,"
with general good health, but we are in a very
scattered condition, which is very inconvenient.
	The EXPONENT is a very welcome visitor in
our midst. May it ever prosper in its noble
efforts of doing good.
	May the blessings of God be with you, my
sister, and all who labor with you in the cause
of truth.
						A SUBSCRIBER.
	St. Joseph Stake, Arizona, Feb. 21, 1887.

		ITEMS FROM SNOWFLAKE.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Thinking that a few notes from this little
settlement might be worthy of room in our
valuable paper, we take pleasure in sending
them. It is seldom we are heard from, but we
are trying to bravely fight the battles of life,
and prove ourselves valiant to the cause of
Christ on the earth.
	As a stake, we are having a rest now from
persecution, but we know not how long peace
may prevail, and we deeply sympathize with
those of our brethren and sisters who are not
so favored, but we know all will be well with
those who are not faint-hearted nor weary in
well-doing.
	Our Relief Society in this ward is doing very
well; we try not to let any go needy or desti-
tute; we strive to visit and comfort the sick, and
soothe the grief of those who are called to
mourn.
	We are very thankful for the recent visit of
Dr. E. S. Barney to our land. She came as a
stranger to us, but when she went away we felt
she was our friend and sister. She spent her
whole time, while here, teaching us things for
our good, and we shall ever hold her in grate-
ful remembrance for the good she has done us.
	We have had a pleasant, open winter, and
recently a rain and snow storm, which moist-
ened up the earth nicely for putting in crops.
Our brethren are busy now, fixing their water
ditches, and making a resevoir to store water
in for the dry part of the seassn. With the
blessings of God upon us, we hope to raise our
own bread, and not have to import as much as
we have had to do in past years.
	We prize our little paper very highly, as it
comes to us laden with good, and we pray that
it may always be a comfort in Zion.
	Praying for the welfare of Zion and the
spread of truth in the earth, we remain,
		Your sisters in the Gospel covenant,
						MARY J. WEST,
						LUCY H. FLAKE,
						MARY H. LARSON.

			ITEMS FROM PRICE.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	As we have not seen anything lataly in our
valuable paper about our little town. Price, we
thought we would write a few lines to let our
sisters know that we are laboring for the king-

[Column 2]
dom with the rest. We are trying to do as
near right as we can. We feel to sustain and
bless everybody who is interested in the great
work of Christ.
	We had a very enjoyable time here the 17th
of Feb. ult., in the gathering at the meeting
house to surprise Sister Jane Eldredge, Presi-
dent of the Primary Association of Price, on
her thirty-sixth anniversary. Singing by the
little Sunday School choir, as Sister Eldredge
was accompanied into the house by her hus-
band, together with several sisters, where a
number of tables were spread with all the
blessings that the earth could produce in the
shape of food, and surrounded by about 175
persons. A blessing was offered, and all par-
took with grateful hearts.
	After dinner, the following token was read
in respect to Sister Eldredge's energetic labors
with the Primary children:

	"A token of respect to our beloved sister,
Jane Eldredge, President of the Primary As-
sociation of Price, for her diligence and patient
perseverance in well-doing with the children
of this place, and feeling that she had more
than performed the duty assigned her, never
letting wind nor weather stop her in the per-
formance of the same.
	Therefore, dear sister, we say unto you, ac-
cept these small tokens of our respect and
gratitude, and may the blessings of our Father
rest down upon you with health and strength
to still go on for many years to come in the
good cause you have entered into with the
young. May every year be brighter than the
last.						SARAH COX.
	She was then presented with some presents
made by the Primary children, and also by
the Sunday School choir.
	Sister Eldredge was a little overcome with
joy and surprise, not looking for anything of
the kind. She thanked them for the honor
and respect shown to her, and also for the
presents they had bestowed upon her.
	There was dancing in the evening, and
toasts, songs and sentiments were freely given.
All had an enjoyable time.
						E. S. H.
						F. A MEAD,
						F. ALRED.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

DEAR EDITOR:

	The kingdom of heaven is for those who
seek for it; not many rich or noble, but the
humble and faithful and pure in heart. We
as a people are of the tribe of Ephraim, prone
to wander from the fold of God, but, oh! let us
return; now is the trying day, and we should
be willing to show our colors. Jesus said, “If
ye will not forsake all and life itself for the
Gospel and my name’s sake, ye are not worthy
of me.”
	Let us turn from our follies. Israel often
worshiped idols and went astray; are we not of
the same lineage? Let us not worship our
houses, lands, clothing or any property, for our
God is a “jealous God, visiting the sins of the
fathers on the children,” but if they walk not
in the path of their fathers he will turn away
His anger, reads the 18th chapter of Ezekiel.
Oh, what comfort to the erring ones! Let us
not be discouraged, but try more earnestly to
live our religion,keeping all the commandments,
observing the Sabbath day and fast days, for
there are great promises to those who do so.
Read the 57th chapter of Isaiah, and you
may understand the promises of the Lord
to the faithful; also the 3rd chapter of Malachi,
you will see the promises of the Lord to those
who pay their tithes and offerings, and those
who assemble to worship their God shall be re-
membered as a man will remember and reward


his own son that serveth him. “He will be
mine;” saith the Lord, “when I will make up
my jewels.”
	Let us read and study our Bible, Book of
Mormon and other Church works, that we may
be prepared when the Bridegroom shall come,
is the prayer of
			Your sister,
						E. JENSEN.
	Orderville.


WOMAN’S  EXPONENT.


MEMORIAL.

TO THE PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES.

	The National Woman’s Suffrage Association,
through this committee, respectfully present to
you a protest against that clause of the anti-
polygamy measure passed by Congress, which,
whether in the Edmunds bill of the Senate or
the Tucker substitute of the House, disfran-
chises the-non-polygamous women of Utah.
	The clause relating to the disfranchisement
of women has no bearing on the general merits
of the end sought to be attained by the measure,
since Mormon men are the majority of the
voters of the territory.
	The non-polygamous women of Utah have
committed no crime. Disfranchisement is re-
served by the United States government for
arch traitors.  Justice forbids that such a 
penalty should be inflicted on innocent
women.
	Non-polygamous Mormon women and the
Christian women of Utah being thus disfran-
chised--the former for their opinions and the lat-
ter for the opinions of the former—a precedent
is established subversive of the fundamental
principles of our government, and threatening
the security of all citizens.
	If Congress deems it necessary to disfran-
chise citizens because of injurious beliefs, dis-
crimination between sexes is manifestly unjust.
	It has been held by the foremost statesmen
of the nation that the right of suffrage once
exercised, becomes a vested right which cannot
be taken away. Gratz Brown once said, in
the Senate of the United States, that if the idea
that suffrage could be taken away at pleasure
once crystallized in the minds of the peo-
ple, it would “ring the death knell of American
liberty.” Mr. Vest, of Missouri, on the 25th
day of this month, said, on the floor of the
Senate: “Suffrage once given can never be
taken away. Legislatures and conventions
may do everything else; they never can do
that. When any particular class or faction of
the community is once invested with this
privilege, it is fixed, accomplished and eternal.
	Thus every argument for justice, equal legis-
lation and the safety of our republican form of
government calls for the defeat of this clause.
	We, therefore, respectfully urge you, as
guardian of the rights of all American citizens,
to veto any measure coming before you which
disfranchises the women of Utah.
		LILLIE DEVEREAUX BLAKE,
		MATILDA JOSLYN GAGE,
		CAROLINE GILKEY ROGERS,
		MARY SEYMOUR HOWELL,
		CLARA B. COLBY,
		SARAH MILLER,
		ELIZABETH BOYNTON HARBERT,
		HARRIETTE R. SHATTUCK,
		LOUISA SOUTHWORTH,
								Committee.


IN MEMORIAM.

	Mrs. Abby Kelley Foster died in Boston,
Mass., at the home of her sister, Mrs. M. L. K.
Barton, with whom she resided, on Friday;
January 14. The next day was her birthday.
On it she would have completed seventy-six
years: Mrs. Foster was as well as usual until


Wednesday of last week, when she seemed very
tired and feeble. Thursday morning she was
too weak to get up.  On Friday afternoon she
died.  She suffered none, and seemed to sleep
away her life.  Her daughter was telegraphed
for, and arrived just before her mother’s
death.	
	Thus has passed from us the most remarkable
woman, in some respects, the world has ever
known. She came upon the stage of active
life when the anti-slavery movement began. 
She was a Quaker woman about twenty-six
years of age, teaching school in Lynn. Her
conscience summoned her to leave everything
for the slave’s sake. It is impossible to des-
cribe the situation. Slavery was entrenched
in the government and laws. It was sanctioned
by religion and upheld by all classes of society.
All the traditions of the world were opposed to
the speaking of women in public. Still stronger
were the forbidding, texts which imposed
“silence upon women.”  To undertake the
cause of the slave was to consent to such mighty
odds. It was to give up respectability, ease,
quiet, comfort, the hope of a competence. It
was to encounter mobs, to endure cold and
hunger, and fatigue, and to face death itself.
	Abby Kelley did not hesitate. She put her
tender shoulder to the work.  Never once, for
nearly thirty years was there space for pause
or rest. A weaker woman, or one with a fibre
less fine, would not have attempted, or would
have given up. To her the latter was impossible.
Think of the long years, the constant torture,
and the unflinching courage!  Verily she is
without a peer.  Over the highway she helped
to build, the slaves walked to freedom, and
over the highway whose foundation stones she
laid with bleeding hands, women are marching
to their equal rights.
	Those who remember Mrs. Foster fifty years
ago will recall the fair and comely young
Quaker woman, whose voice, sweet and clear
as a silver bell, penetrated to the farthest
listener. Those who have only seen her in
later years, will think of her as feeble, bent,
worn with the battle of life, and with a voice
hoarse from over use and much outdoor speak-
ing, “over-plied in liberty’s defense.” The
world is richer and better for Abby Kelley
Foster.—Lucy Stone, in Woman's Journal.


ANOTHER ART CRAZE.

	The latest art work among ladies is
known as the "French Craze" for decorating
china, glassware, etc. It is something entirely
new, and is both profitable and fascinating. It
is very popular in New York, Boston and other
Eastern cities. To ladies desiring to learn the
Art, we will send an elegant china placque (18
inches,) handsomely decorated, for a model, to-
gether with a box of material, 100 colored de-
signs assorted in flowers, animals, soldiers,
land scapes, etc., complete with full instruc-
tions, upon receipt of only $1.00. The placque
alone is worth more than the amount charged.
To every lady ordering this outfit who encloses
the address of five other ladies interested in
Art matters, to whom we can mail our new
catalogue of Art Goods, we will enclose extra
and without charge, a beautiful 30 inch, gold-
tinted placque.
	Address, The Empire News Co., Syracuse, 
N.Y.


QUEEN VICTORIA’S “DRAWING-
ROOMS.”

	A “drawing-room,” as Queen Victoria’s re-
ception to ladies is called, begins at 3 o’clock
in the afternoon. But—more particularly of
late years—the Queen has not always been able
to remain throughout the ceremony. Perhaps,
after hours sufferance on her part, her Majesty


retires and the Princess of Wales fills her
place. But the Princess is not the rose. She
only lives near it, and the triumph of the day
is darkened for those ladies who, arriving a
little late, courtesy to the Princess instead of
the Queen. Accordingly it seems to have oc-
curred to some enterprising lady that if she
had her carriage at the gates of the palace an
hour before they were thrown back, she would
be in pretty good time. At the next drawing-
room some one bettered this, being there an
hour and five minutes early. The process has
gone forward till now the clanging of noon
from the clock-tower of Westminister is heard
adown a long string of carriages containing
ladies in lowest evening dress, sitting stonily
waiting whilst a mob of sightseers peer at
them as mercilessly as if they were framed
pictures of their ancestresses.


CONSTANCY IN THE SEXES.  

	A writer in Lippincott’s Magazine calls at-
tention to the fact that, while many novelists
have treated of love as indicating divided
affections on the part of men, almost no one of
them has represented woman as finding room
in her heart for devotion of two men at the
same time, or even of doubt as to which was
entitled to this tribute. Thackeray, who inti-
mates that Laura Bell might have preferred
Warrington to Pendennis, had it not been for
the obstacle of the former’s marriage, is the
nearest to an exception on this point among the
more famous writers This is a tribute, of
course, to the superior positiveness of woman
in fixing her ideal, and adhering to it. That
is an altogether unjust satire, as far as it applies
to the female sex, in the Spanish writer who
composed as an epitaph:	

		She who lies beneath this stone
		Died of constancy alone.
		Fear not: approach, O passer-by!
		Of naught contagious did she die.

	Trollope's favorite hero is one who hardly
knows his own mind as between two charmers. 
Howells humorously hints at the cause of the
inconstancy of his sex, when he says in his
“Indian Summer” that “after eighteen hundred
years of Christianity man is only imperfectly
monogamous.” Had novelists existed in the
days of polygamy they would have found an
easy way out of this dilemma for their heroes,
though it would have much lessened the avail-
able stock for their stories.—Boston Herald.


W. N. C. T.  U.
	
	NEW YORK, March 9, 1887.

KIND FRIEND:

	Will you please call attention to the offer of
a Prize Banner to the New England State,
which reports the largest per cent of increase
in membership, including regular and honorary
members of the W. N. C. T. U., and the "Y's;"
also the middle, Southern and Western State,
which makes this report, with one for the state
beyond the Missouri, having the largest per
cent of increase in its membership, and one
for the territory that leads in this respect dur-
ing the present year- Thus, the weakest state
is placed on a level, at the start, with the
strongest. The banners will be presented at
Nashville, next autumn, at the annual meeting
of the W. N. C. T. U.
	Please print this note and plead for a more
thorough canvas for names. It means the
spread of the total abstinence propaganda, and
that means victory.
			Yours for prohibition,
				FRANCES E. WILLARD.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


NOTES ABOUT WOMEN

	Miss Mary Booth, the editor of Harper's
Bazar, is said to be going to Europe for rest.
	Ex-Empress Eugene has applied to the city
government of Naples for permission to enter 
the hospital as a nurse, and care for soldiers
wounded at Massowah.
   	Hannah More, when she was told a tale
against anyone in her village, usually answered,
"Come, we will go and ask if that be true."
The effect was something wonderful. The tale-
bearer, taken aback, would begin to say, "Well,
perhaps there may have been a mistake," and 
beg that no notice be taken of the matter.
But no, the good lady would go there and then,
taking the scandal-monger with her, to make
inquiry and compare accounts. If all tale-
bearers could be treated in this way, few tales
would be told. 
	Mrs. Henry Wood, the prolific English
novelist who lately died, aged seventy, had
edited the Argosy for twenty years. A sketch
of her in the Pall Mall Gazette says: "Her life
may fairly be said to have been prolonged by
her intellectual activity and power of concen- 
tration." Her most popular novel, "East
Lynne," has been translated into French, Par-
see, and Hindustanee, and has been drama-
tized by several adapters. She is described as
a good Englishwoman, of strong domestic
tastes, and rather shrinking from publicity.
All her thirty novels were popular. Her
kindness to youthful literary aspirants was
great, and her conscientiousness in replying to
her many correspondents made havoc with her
time. Her circle of correspondents was wide;
only a week or two before her death, she re-
ceived a letter about one of her books from a 
stoker in the Persian Gulf.


NOTES AND NEWS. 

	Municipal suffrage for women has been de-
feated in the Nebraska Legislature by a small
majority. 
	The women of Kansas are registering in
large numbers, to vote under the new municipal
suffrage law. A circular, urging them to do
so, has been issued jointly by the Kansas Equal
Suffrage Association and the Kansas W. C. T.
U. Public jubilee meetings have been held in
a number of [-]owns, and great enthusiasm pre-
vails.


Patents have been granted to women for the
week ending Feb. 22, 1887, as follows:
	Josephine Doriat, New York, N. Y., Means
for Serving Guests in Restaurants.
	Catharine C. Griswold, New York, Corset.
	Elizabeth Guion, New York, Medicine
Spoon. Dressmaker's Fitting Chair.
	Sarah A. Parke, Bay City, Mich., Sawtooth
Swaging Machine. 
	Elizabeth Wilson, Kokomo, Ind., Dust-de
flector.


MISCELLANEOUS.

	Thrift of time will repay you in after life
with a usury of profit beyond your most san-
guine dreams.—Gladstone.

	Let us help the fallen still, though they
never pay us, and let us lend, without exacting
the usury of gratitude.—Thackery.

	A true woman is to a true man's influence
what the rolling process is to the furnaced gold,
for she rolls it into multitudinous sheets.


When we are most filled with heavenly love,
and only then, are we best fitted to bear with
human infirmity, to live above it and forget its
burden.—Maria Hvre.

	A man who lives a virtuous life and in the
pursuit of knowledge may have great joy with
only coarse rice to eat, water to drink, and
his bended arm for a pillow —Confucius.


RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.

	Resolutions of Respect to the memory of Sister Annie
Sharp, who died suddenly at her homo in Preston, Oneida
Co., Idaho, Feb. 19, 1887, aged sixteen years, seven
months and nine days.
	
	Whereas, It has pleased our Heavenly Father to call
from us by the hand of death, our beloved and respected
sister in the Y. L. M. I. A.,
	Resolved, That we, the members of the Y. L. M. I. A.,
have lost a true friend and faithful laborer, one who was
ever patient and faithful to her duty, and her good qualities
will ever endear her to our memories,
	Resolved, That we deeply sympathize with the bereaved
family in the loss of an affectionate and dutiful daughter
and sister.
	Resolved, That a copy of these Resolutions be placed in
our record book, printed in the Young Ladies' Enterprise
and a copy given to the bereaved family.
					NELLIE PARKINSON,
					CASSIE WEST,		Committee
					LUCY M. SABIN,


OBITUARIES.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	It is with feelings of deep sorrow that we communicate
the death of our beloved young sister, Catherine E. Suther-
land, daughter of John and Ellen Sutherland, who de-
parted this life at the residence of her parents, Big Cotton-
wood, Salt Lake County, at 9 a.m., on the 22nd of Feb.,
1887, of fever and paralysis of the brain. She was aged
fifteen years, four months and twenty-eight days, and for
her age had great faith in the Gospel; was most amiable
and tender in disposition and manner. She was a regular
attendant at Sunday School, an assistant secretary, and led
the singing in the Primary Association; also a member of
the Y. L. M. I. Association. To know her was to love and
respect her. The funeral services were held at the ward
meeting house on the 24th of Feb., at 12:30. where a large
number met to pay their last respects.
									COM.

			RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.

	In memory of our aged sister, Anna Maria Jasperson,
who departed this life Dec. 17th, 1886. She was born
Sept. 12th, 1809.
	Whereas, The Lord in His infinite wisdom has seen fit
to call from our midst our worthy and beloved sister, Anna
Maria Jasperson, and,
	Whereas, She was a true and faithful Latter-day Saint,
and a faithful member of the Relief Society of Goshen
Ward, and for many years honorably filled the office of a
teacher in the same, it is fitting that we record the high es-
teem and appreciation of her services. Therefore be it
	Resolved, That her counsel and example will be held in
grateful remembrance by our Society.
	Resolved, That we who have seen the ever willing and
cheerful spirit with which she performed every duty will
endeavor to profit by the same.
	Resolved, That it is the sentiments of our Society that
we spmpathize with the relatives, and that a copy of these
Resolutions be furnished them, one sent to the EXPONENT, 
and also placed on the minutes of this Society.

Sleep sweetly, dearest sister, from sorrow, grief and
	pain,
Thy labor is all over, it was not done in vain;
Thy faithful course here on this earth is worthy of imita-
	tion;
The day draws nigh when we shall see the Lord and His
	salvation.
					PIRENA JAMESON,
					MARTHA JENSEN,		Committee.
					JULIA S. MACKEE,

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.

Vol. 15.		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, APRIL 1, 1887.			No. 21.


BEAUTIFUL TEMPLE OF GOD.

Put the cares of the world aside for a time,
	And lay down each earth-binding load,
To discharge for your kindred the duties sublime
	In the beautiful temple of God.

			CHORUS:
		Beautiful temple, Beautiful temple,
		Beautiful temple of God.

Where the life and the power, that Elijah inspires,
	Will bring forth the dust from the sod,
When the hearts of the children are turned to the sires,
	In the beautiful temple of God.
										Cho.

Where the feet of the proud and the steps of the meek
	Are directed in one narrow road,
That will bring forth the union and love we should seek
	In the beautiful temple of God.
										Cho.

Where the prayer of faith can arise from the soul,
	And ascend to Jehovah's abode,
And the humble and faithful are reaching the goal
	They aspire to in temples of God.
										Cho.

On the Ladder of which the Patriarch dreamed,
	The feet of the angels had trod,
The living are crowned and the dead are redeemed
	In the beautiful temple of God.
										Cho.

Where the echoes of heaven respond to the heart,
	To dispel all the cares that corrode,
And the curse from the earth has begun to depart,
	In the beautiful temple of God.
										Cho.

Where the bright Lamp of Life and the Tree in its bloom,
	With the oil of salvation's bestowed,
And the wise are preparing to meet the Bridegroom
	In the beautiful temple of God.
										Cho.

Where dressed in the bright robes of glory we seemed,
	With the sandals of righteousness shod;
And fanned by the zephyrs of spirits redeemed,
	In the beautiful temple of God.
										Cho.

Like the odor of Aaron's anointing so sweet,
	Was the comfort and blessing that flowed
From the crown of the head, to the soles of the feet,
	In the beautiful temple of God.
										Cho.

Where the pure balm of Gilead is soothing the breast,
	And wafting its fragrance abroad.
Bespeaking the glorious coming of Christ
	To the beautiful temple of God.
										Cho.

Where the weak and infirm are restored to their health,
	When with pain and affliction they're bowed,
And they offer their thanks for such priceless wealth
	In the beautiful temple of God.
										Cho.

Where the jewels of worth can be laid up in heaven,
	When we walk by the bright iron rod;
Where the keys and the powers of the priesthood are given,
	In the beautiful temple of God.
										Cho.

Where the triumphs of Jesus will bring forth the dead,
	From the precious seed that is sewed,
And with glory and song the redeemed will be led
	Through the beautiful temples of God.
										Cho.
							JANETTE PATON.


[Column 1]
		BIBLE PROPHECY.
	Isaiah, chapter x: “Woe! unto them that
decree unrighteous decrees, and that write
grieviousness which they have prescribed to
turn away the needy from judgment, and to
take away the rights from the poor of my
people, that widows may be their prey and
that they may rob the fatherless!”
	“And what will ye do in the day of visita-
tion and in the desolation which will come
from afar?  To whom will ye flee for help,
and where will ye leave your glory?”
	The above to the Latter-day Saints needs no
comments, for they fully know that through
the unrighteous decrees known as the Edmunds
and Edmunds-Tucker bills, that the needy
have been turned away from judgment, for how
many times have our brethren and sisters
gone to the rulers of this nation and plead
their cause, for they were in need of justice,
and does not this unrighteous decree take away
the rights from the poor of this people, and are
not children left without their fathers, and
robbed of the necessary support that their
fathers have earned for them?  Surely they do
rob the fatherless, when kind fathers are
snatched from their wives and children and
thrust into prison, and their property sold to
pay an unlawful fine.  And were not the
women the wives of those men, true to their
religion and their God they would surely fall
a prey to those robbers, who are trying to rob
them of the loving care and protection of their
husbands, thinking that they might thereby
drag them into the mire that thousands of
women are degraded into who live much nearer
to Senator Edmunds and other great men of
the world, than do the Mormon women, but
their object will fail here, for the Mormon
women are virtuous and God-fearing; they
have entered into their marriage relations in
obedience to the commandment of God, and
they are willing to leave the outcome in His
hands, for they know that the hand of the
Lord is in this present persecution, that He is
only using His enemies as a tool wherewith to
purify His people, for He says in the same
chapter, verse 12, “Wherefore, it shall come
to pass, that when the Lord hath performed
His whole work upon Mount Zion and on
Jerusalem, I will punish the fruit of the stout
heart of the king of Assyria, and the glory of
his high looks.”
	“For he saith, By the strength of my hand
have I done it, and by my wisdom, for I am
prudent, and I have removed the bounds of the
people, and I have robbed their treasures, and
I have put down the inhabitants like a valiant
man, and my hand hath found as a nest the
riches of the people; and as one gathered eggs
that are left, have gathered all the earth (all
they have) and there was none that moved
the wing, or opened the mouth or peeped.”
	The present Edmunds-Tucker bill having
become a law, we will see the above prophecy
fulfilled, for who is so blind that they cannot
see that it is a plan to rob the treasures of the
people; but still in this we will acknowledge
the hand of God, as in another prophecy of
Isaiah He plainly warns the church of what
shall befall them because of pride; and the
Lord requires His people to be humble, and a
proud heart is not pleasing to Him, and yet
He says in the same chapter, 15th verse: “Shall

[Column Two]
the axe boast itself against him that heweth
therewith? or shall the saw magnify itself
against him that shaketh it?” etc.  He is doing
this work, although we sometimes think that
some very rough tools are being made use of;
the Lord holds them with a rein, and they will
go just as far as He will let them and no
farther.  In the same chapter, 24th verse:
“Therefore, thus saith the Lord of hosts, my
people that dwell in Zion be not afraid of the
Assyrian; he shall smite thee with a rod; and
shall lift up his staff against thee after the
manner of Egypt, for yet a very little while,
and the indignation shall cease, and mine anger
in their destruction, and the Lord of Hosts
shall stir up a scourge for him according to
the slaughter of Midian at the rock of Oreb,
and as his rod was upon the sea, so shall he
lift it up after the manner of Egypt, and it
shall come to pass in that day that his burden
shall be taken from off thy shoulder, and his
yoke from off thy neck, and the yoke shall be
destroyed because of the anointing.”
	There are many things to which the Saints
can look forward to for comfort in this trying
time, and let us pray that God will hasten the
day when the yoke will be destroyed, and let
us all strive to stand faithful until that time
comes, when the Lord’s anointed will come out
of their hiding places, and from within prison
walls, and will rejoice with their wives and
children, and again teach this glorious plan of
life and salvation to the children of men, and
we will see the Canaanites, that they will no
longer ask for help from their enemies, but, “It
shall come to pass in that day that the remnant
of Israel and such as are escaped of the house
of Jacob will no more stay upon him that 
smote them but will stay upon the Holy One
of Israel in truth.” And the kingdom shall
grow, and the little stone which Daniel saw
will roll until it fills the whole earth, for God
will make room for it, and it will break  in
pieces all other kingdoms.
	The Constitution of the United States has
already been broken upon it, and the senate
and representative men of this nation are di-
vided in their opinions and judgment, and
their sense of justice is broken and divided be-
cause of it, and they will be confused and their
minds darkened and troubled, and men’s hearts
shall fail them, and the inhabitants of the
earth will be shaken and destroyed.  The Lord
will open up a passage for His people, and they
will be delivered from bondage, even as he
opened up a passage through the Red Sea for
His people in ancient days.  A deliverer will
the Lord prepare for His people, “And the
Spirit of the Lord will rest upon him, and the
spirit of wisdom and of understanding, the
spirit of counsel and might, the spirit of
knowledge and the fear of the Lord; and shall
make him quick of understanding in the fear
of the Lord, and he shall not judge after the
sight of his eyes, neither reprove after the
hearing of his ears; but with righteousness
shall he judge the poor, and reprove with equity
for the meek of the earth, and he shall smite
the earth with the rod of his mouth, and with
the breath of his lips shall he slay the wicked.”
	With joy the Saints look forth to that day,
which is nigh at hand, for the Lord has said,
“Though the heavens and the earth pass away,
my words will not pass away.”
				SOMANTHIA T. BOISE.
Manassa, Colorado,


WOMAN’S	EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
LEAVES FROM MY JOURNAL.

M. J. TANNER.

Continued.

	When we got back to our room, we found
our trunks there. We dressed and went for a
walk up Broadway. It being Sunday morning
the streets were quiet and business houses
closed; still it was very pleasant, and many
people were stirring. We had been traveling
so long we had lost our reckoning, and could
hardly realize that it was Sunday.
	New York is much as we expected to see it.
but it gives one very much the idea of the old
song:
	"We cannot see the city for the houses."
All houses; houses everywhere; not a spot of
ground to put your foot on. Such high houses;
from five to seven stories high; no chance to
see out but to look up to the sky, and even that
is being appropriated, seemingly, for when peo-
ple can no longer build out they build up.
	On our return we concluded to visit Sister
Harrison. We went to the office and inquired
for her room. The clerk turned to the direc-
tory, and informed us that Mrs. Harrison and
Mrs. Sharp had No. 828. We stepped on the
elevator and asked for room 828. We were
taken up seven stairs, and directed which way
to go. We followed the numbers on the doors
until we found 828. Here we found our
friends in a very pleasant room, overlooking
the street, as also many other houses. They
take their meals at the restaurant, and we
might have lived in the same house for weeks
and never met. It is like a small city laid off
and divided by halls and corridors, with stairs
leading in every direction. We could go up
and down by stairs or elevator. Our friends
had been to church, and we were sorry we did
not go with them. We all met in Bro. Hart's
room and spent the evening. Quite a number
of our old friends were there, and it seemed
pleasant to meet so many among strangers.
	N. Y.,  Monday, April 20. Rested well,
but got up this morning with a headache.
Went down to breakfast; found ourselves late.
Were a little surprised, but were shown to a 
smaller dining room, where we were nicely
waited on. On comparing time, I found my
watch an hour slow. I had forgotten that New
York time was three hours ahead of us. After
breakfast we went for a walk on Broadway.
We went a long way, and got back very tired,
to find a lady wating for me in the Parlor. On
going in I found it was my cousin, Mrs. Kim-
ball. I packed my trunk and she took me
home with her.
	The method of traveling was quite new to
me. We went in a buss, I think they are
called, to the ferry. We crossed the river on
a steam ferry-boat, which I enjoyed very much;
then we took a street car to her home. It is
about eight miles from the hotel. It is on
Long Island, and is called Williamsburg,
though it seems to me a continuation of the
same city. It is now incorporated in Brooklyn
and is called "Eastern District."
	The streets are paved with rock, and packed
with vehicles of all kinds, and the noise is so
great that we can scarcely hear each other
speak. Everything is so strange, I should be
frightened without a guide. I am warmly
welcomed by my cousin’s family, four hand-
some, black-eyed boys, from thirteen to twenty-
four years of age. Her husband is away, but
will be home in a few days. We are nice and
quiet, but I am so tired, so tired; the house
seems turning about, and the grinding noise
seems running through me from my fingers to
my toes. I wonder if I shall ever feel calm
again.
	Williamsburg, Tuesday, April 21. Rested
some last night, but cannot get over being tired;

[Column 2]
every nerve seems frizzling and grinding. Mrs.
Kimball is a great talker, as well as myself,
and we have so much to say. In the evening
we walk out to look at the stores by gaslight;
the streets are so light that everything looks
lovely. We take a street car and ride home,
and spend the evening in conversation until
late.
	Wednesday, April 22nd. Feel somewhat
rested, and go for a visit to the Brooklyn
Bridge. We ride over on a steam car, fare
three cents; walk back, fare one cent. It is a 
long walk, a mile and a quarter, but it is very
pleasant, and we have such a fine view of the
country, and river, which is dotted with boats
of all kinds, from the tiny row boat, tossing
about, to the ship. The bridge not only crosses
the river, but spans over the city for a long
distance, passing over houses, six and seven
stories high.
	Williamsburg, Sunday, April.25th. Raining
this morning. We had proposed to hear the
Rev. Henry Ward Beecher to-day, but it has
rained all day and we could not go, but spent
our time talking and reading Scripture. My
friends have great curiosity about our faith,
and are a little surprised to hear it defended
on a scriptural basis. We have some very in-
teresting arguments.
	I have visited several points of interest in
the last few days, among which is the water-
works, or reservoir, which supplies water for
the city. It is a large pond or basin, nicely
walled in with an iron fence around it; at a
little distance is another fence, making an en-
closure, inside of which the keeper lives, and
no living thing is allowed inside the enclosure.
No person can go inside the enclosure without
permission. There seems little danger of dead
animals or anything of the kind getting into
the water, which is conducted through pipes to
all parts of the city, and each house is fur-
nished with water in every story; also pipes to
carry off sewerage, which are laid below the
water mains.  Everything is so complete. The
streets are swept, and all the dirt carried off
every few days. The dust and ashes of the
houses are collected in barrels, which are
emptied and carried away by men whose busi-
ness it is, and no garbage is allowed in the ash
barrels, as it is used in filling up low places,
and vegetable decay would breed miasma. It
looked strange to see cold victuals and scraps
from the table thrown into the fire and burned,
but when it was explained to me that such
things would make a litter if thrown in the
back yard, and perhaps a disagreeable odor, as
well as attract cats and dogs, to the discomfort
of the people, I could readily see that circum-
stances altered cases.
	A large proportion of the people live in
rented houses. Some of the houses are built
with two or three stories and a basement, with
a small back yard for clothes line etc., and a
small yard in front. The sidewalks are paved
with flagstones, and each one is required to
keep his own sidewalk clean.
	Some houses are built in "flats," on the
French plan, five or six stories high, a complete
suit of rooms in each story, rent ranging ac-
cording to the height. Three families live one
above another. As a consequence, people find
it unpleasant to have many children in a house.
The first question from the landlord is, "Have
you children?" "Are they boys or girls?" If
girls, "have they beaux?" "Do you keep ser-
vants?" If these questions are answered satis-
factorily, and you can pay the price demanded,
you may rent the house if it suits you. If the
family is too large, or the children too small, if
you have a baby, or play the piano, or run a sew-
ing machine, it is difficult to procure a house.
	A young couple commence housekeeping
and when a baby comes they are liable to be
requested to change their quarters. Children,
then,are a constant source of anxiety, and every

[Column 3]
advent is looked upon with a species of con-
sternation. Such a condition naturally makes
children unwelcome, and it must take great
efforts of patience and goodwill to live so
closely packed together. Every effort is made
to secure cleanliness and good order. Houses
are not so high in Brooklyn and Williamsburg
as in New York.


THREE SCORE AND TEN.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The 19th of March, 1887, being the 70th
birthday of the President of the Relief Society
of Santaquin, Sister Elizabeth J. Stickney,
the members of the Society assembled at the
school house at 2 o'clock p.m., in honor of the
occasion, together with the Bishop and Coun-
selors and some of the most prominent brethren
of the place.
	Our President, with the older ladies, seated
in a large circle round the stove, chatting gaily,
looked very beautiful, while the younger por-
tion participated in the dance for a short time,
when the following address was read by Bro.
Eli Openshaw:
	To Mrs. Elizabeth Stickney, President of the
		Relief Society of Santaquin:

	DEAR SISTER:—By the counsel of our
Bishop and Counselors, we, the Relief Society
of Santaquin, have met with you to-day for
the purpose of celebrating this your seventieth
birthday.  It is a good old age, but yet we
hope you may live to see many happy returns
of the day
	You have now been President of the Relief
Society of Santaquin eighteen years and six
months.  Your labors in that capacity have
been arduous and unceasing.  Your charitable
disposition has always made you a friend to
the poor, the sick and the aged; many there
are who can testify of your care for them, and
will always pray for your welfare in this life
and that which is to come.
	As a financier, you connot be surpassed.
That you have handled the funds entrusted to
your care with interest for the benefit of the
Society, has been fully realized and duly ap-
preciated by us all.
	We therefore wish to return unto you our
heartfelt thanks for the honorable discharge of
your duties, and ever pray that long life may
be given you, and that you may retain your
powers of mind, so that you may continue to
be our elect lady for many years to come.
	Signed in behalf of the Relief Society of
Santaquin,			ELIZABETH WALL,	
					SARAH J. HALLADAY.
					BETTIE BOSS,
					ANN J. STICKNEY,
					ELIZABETH OPENSHAW.
					RENDENA OLSON,
			Committee of arrangements.

	Prest. Stickney responded to it, and ad-
dressed the assembly for a short time, thanking
the sisters for their kindness and appreciation
of her labors, and bearing a strong testimony
to the work of God.	
	Then there was dancing again until half
past four o'clock, when a bountiful repast was
partaken of, and an intermission taken for one
hour, when the company again returned to en-
joy a very pleasant and agreeable evening to-
gether, consisting of dancing, interspersed with
several pieces of poetry, which had been com-
posed for the occasion.  Our Prest. entertained
the company by dancing two hornpipes, which
she executed very gracefully.  She was pre-
sented with a sum of money, and the names of
the donors were read.  Refreshments were
served again during the evening, and the party
was dismissed at eleven o'clock.  All seemed
highly pleased, as it had been a day long to
be remembered in Santiquin.
				ANN J. STICKNEY, Sec.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


LIGHT AND SHADE.

After the long, weary months of the winter,
	When the blue of the sky is o'ercast
With the clouds of a dreary December,
	And we've long heard the wind's chilling blast,
How grateiul is springtime so gorgeous,
	With sweet flowers, with sunshine and song;
The music of birds, so enchanting,
	We enjoy all the summer day long.
And we know that this world with its changes
	Is a gift from our Father above,
And to Him, through all of its seasons,
	We will give adoration and love.

After painful and lingering illness--
	Days of agony, mis'ry and pain,
When all through the night's dismal stillness
	We so longed for the morning again,
How we welcome the day's cheerful dawning,
	And we hail the bright sun's genial rays,
We feel that our strength is returning
	With the brightness of spring's sunny days.
How blessed indeed is the absence
	Of pain and misery's keen darts,
And our gratitude rises to heaven
	From our truly appreciative hearts.

And when by affliction o'ertaken,
	Our heartstrings are bleeding and torn,
By adversities hand we are shaken,
	And we feel so forsaken and 'lorn!
But we know where to look for a solace,
	A recompense pure, holy and sweet--
We go to our Father in meekness,
	And find satisfaction complete;
"For whom He loveth, He chastiseth,"
	That we may the greater joys know,
And patiently bear all our sorrows,
	That mortal hearts find here below.
							E. R. SHIPP.
Salt Lake City, March 25, 1887.


THE LILY.

TO LILY FREEZE.

	Lovely lily, in the springtime,
		Daintily its leaves unfold;
	How prophetic is its coming
		After winter's frost and cold;
	Gently, tenderly appealing
		To the stubborn human will;
	Giving promise of the summer
		The Creator will fulfill.

	Clothed in beauty by its Maker,
		Graceful lily blooming fair,
	Swaying in a jaunty fashion
		In the soft and balmy air;
	Wild upon the heath or mountain,
		Lilies blossom here and there
	Simply by the power of nature,
		Without aught of toil or care.

	Yet the rarest, purest lilies, 
		Those which please us most, I know,
	Are the ones we nurture kindly,
		And consider how they grow.
	We all know one charming Lily,
		That has need of constant care;
	Fragile, delicate and tender,
		Though her mind is rich and rare.

	Choicest lilies bloom at Easter,
		So I've heard wise-acres say;
	Emblems of the resurrection,
		And significant, alway,
	Of the pure, the chaste and holy,
		Yea, in every land and clime,
	And the sayings of the Savior
		Make the lily seem sublime.

	Just when Easter lilies blossom'd,
		And the fields were fair to view,
	Our sweet Lily came from heav'n
		With a welcome warm and true;
	And in truth she had a mission
		Corresponding to her name,
	And with purity of motive
		She will magnify the same.


Fold not up the talents given
		To improve while here on earth,
	Consecrate them to a purpose
		That shall be of priceless worth.
	Though you toil not, nor yet spin,
		There is other work to do,
	And a wise and loving Father
		Will reveal it unto you
							EMILE.
March 26, 1883.


A BRIEF SKETCH.

		ELIZA COLLINS HUNSAKER.

	Thinking that a short sketch of the life of
Eliza Collins Hunsaker would be of interest
to the readers of your noble paper, I
thought I would write you a little of what she
has had to pass through.
	(She was born March 5th, 1817, in Spencer
County, Kentucky, and was the daughter of
Allen Collins and Mary Braady.  She was
married at the early age of 16, January 4th,
1833, to Abraham Hunsaker.  She passed
through all of the persecutions of the Saints,
after she was baptized, which was not until
the 15th of Nov., 1840.  They were driven
with the Saints from that time until they
reached Winter Quarters, where they situated
on a small stream that was called Honey Creek,
about a day’s drive from the main batch of
the Saints.
	She had passed through trials up to the
present date, but now comes the trial of her
faith.  When the call was made for five hun-
dred men to go and fight with Mexico, her hus-
band was among the number.  He said that
when he left her with six small children, the
three oldest  girls, with no one to protect her
and the children but God, that he could not
have felt worse had he been called to lay her
in the cold earth.  While in this condition, to
make matters worse, her relatives, who were
opposed to her joining the Church, wrote to
her and told her that if she would forsake her
religion and return to them they would come
after her.  But like others, true to her faith
and to her God, she would not accept of the
offer.  By the help of God she was able to take
care of her six little children until the return
of her husband.
	One little incident, that happened while he
was on his journey, I will here relate:  He was
feeling bad about the condition he had left his
family in,when a dove alighted upon his head,
then flew by those that were placed by the
priesthood to lead. It was shown to him that
if he would obey counsel he would return
again to his family, and for him to trouble
himself no more about them.  This gave him
relief, and, strange to say, the same dove was
seen around the wagon where his wife was
camped, and one of the little girls said,
"Mother, there is a dove with A. H. on it;
it is from father.” They felt better after this;
it acted as a comforter to both parties.
	One year from that date the dove was seen
in a tree, and Bro. Hancock said, “Here is
Bro. Hunsaker.” At this remark the dove
flew over his head and fluttered, and then went
away.
	The following summer, after her husband’s
return, they came to the valley and settled
at Cottonwood.  He was afterwards called to
go to Carson Valley, where he stopped for a
while, and was then called back.  He finally
settled at Brigham City, where he lived until
about ten or twelve years ago, when he built a
mill on Salt Creek.  His wife afterward moved
there, where she now resides.
	This last summer she made a trip out to her
daughters at Alpine Co., Nevada, where she
bore a faithful testimony to the truth of Mor-
monism and the principle known as the patri-
archal order of marriage.  She came home in
good health, on March 5th, 1887.

[column 2]
	Her children and grandchildren surprised
her on her seventieth birthday.  We had a
very interesting time from 2 p.m. until about
8 in the evening.  She bore her testimony to
her children and grandchildren, who have be-
come very numerous.  She has been the mother
of twelve children, and has sixty-five grand-
children and twenty-six great-grandchildren,
making a total of 103 all told, most of whom
are living.  She said that she had never been
sorry that her lot had been cast with the de-
spised Mormons; she knew that this was the
kingdom of God, and that she had passed
through trials, for which she was thankful, and
that she was glad that her husband had gone
into the principle known as the patriarchal
order of marriage; she knew that it was from
God, and that the Lord would bless all those
who obeyed this order of marriage, and that
she would not be out of it for all the world, for
she knew that God had spoken from heaven,
and although earth and hell may combine, they
will never be able to destroy the people nor
the kingdom of God, and she exhorted her
children to remain faithful, that they might
get the blelsings in store for the faithful.
								REX.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	The large numbers in which women are
registering to vote under the new municipal
suffrage law in Kansas ought to set at rest the
fear that women would not use the suffrage if
they had it. The women voted at the primary
elections on March 6, and there was not the
slightest disturbance. One thousand of the
leading women of Leavenworth registered
within two days.
	The U. S. District Attorney for Washington
Territory recommends the women to go on vot-
ing as usual, and if refused, to take out a man-
damus to compel acceptance to their votes.
The validity of the woman suffrage law having
been repeatedly affirmed by several Supreme
Court decisions before the adverse decision by
only two of the judges. There is great doubt
whether the adverse decision will be sustained
by the full bench.
	Twenty-seven years ago, Henry Ward
Beecher, in a powerful lecture on woman
suffrage, predicted that one of the first good
results of the reform would be the removal of
the polling places to more respectable quarters,
and the increased order and civility of elec-
tions. The Republican Convention, which met
at Topeka, Kansas, on March  7, expressed a 
cordial welcome to the newly enfranchised
ladies, and adopted a series of resolutions, en-
titled, "For the new voters," favoring safety
and convenience of voters at the polls, and
recommending their removal to more respect-
able locations. They also declare that no
woman whose political duties call her on the
street on election day shall be subjected to any
inconvenience, or have any ticket thrust into
her hand unless she asks for it. The Boston
Traveler says: "This view of what is really
the practical effect of woman suffrage is inter-
esting, and the advocates of political enfran-
chisement for women will be more than mortal
if they can refrain from airs of triumph in
this fulfilment of their long prophesies as to
the better state of affairs when women go to
the polls."—Ex.


They talk about a woman’s sphere
	As though it had a limit:
There’s not a place in earth or heaven,
There’s not a task to mankind given,
There’s not a blessing or a woe,
There’s not a whispered yes or no,
There's not a life, or death or birth,
That has a feather’s weight of worth,
	Without a woman in it.
