Salt Lake City,	-	APRIL 1,1887.


[Column 1]
﻿RELIEF SOCIETY CONFERENCE.

	The Conference of the Relief Society of this
Stake was held in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly
Rooms, on Thursday, March 17th, Mrs. M. I.
Horne presiding. Present on the stand were Mrs.
B. W. Smith, Mrs. S. M. Kimball, Mrs. M. W.
Snow, and Mrs. Mary Pitchforth of Nephi, Prest.
of the Relief Society in Juab Stake. After sing-
ing, prayer was offered by Mrs. S. M. Kimball.
Continued with singing. Roll of presidents called;
23 presidents, 3 counselors and 2 secretaries re-
sponded.  Minutes and Stake report read and
accepted.
	Opening remarks by Sister Pitchforth; subjects,
care of the needy, the edification to be gained by
attending our meetings, guarding our speech,
special protection and guidance of our children,
the need of keeping all commandments given, and
other interesting topics.
	Prest. Horne urged the necessity of perfect re-
ports and promptness in forwarding them to the
Stake Secretary. Urged the-importance of home
industries, and advised the sisters to see to the
setting out and cultivating mulberry trees, that we
might make our own silk. “We will likely have
to depend on home made clothing. We are largely
advertised to the world through this persecution.”
Spoke of the storing of grain, for many would
come to Zion for bread. Thought from the re-
ports that the branches were improving tem-
porally.
	Mrs. S. M. Kimball thought the Relief Society
should foster education.  “Pick up some little girl
that has a taste for teaching, and let her gather
some of the little children from the street around
her, it will do good.” Spoke of the Kindergarten
and the Woman’s Home, and said, “We are able
to take care of our own people;” made many other
excellent remarks.
	Mrs. B. W. Smith spoke of the peace we enjoy;
thought we should pray for wisdom, and spoke of
her visit to the Tooele Conference with Sister
Lucy W. Kimball, where they had held seven
meetings and administered to the sick. She felt
the revelation given four years ago to President
Taylor was just as much binding upon us as any
other, “and we should help our husbands to set
our houses in order.”
	Mrs. Rachel R. Grant had been spending the
winter in St. George, and had visited around that
Stake considerably, and found the organizations
in good order. Thought we had nothing to fear
from our enemies. “Let us be firm and faithful,
and cultivate a spirit of love and charity one to
another.”
	Adjourned till 2 p.m. Singing. Benediction
pronounced by Mrs. Mary Alice Lambert.
	Afternoon session, 2 p.m.:  Opened with sing-
ing. Prayer by Mrs. M. W. Wilcox. Continued
with singing. Remarks by Mrs. M. W. Snow:
She loved to hear the work and kingdom of God
defended by the sisters.  Spoke upon prayer,
wisdom, kindness and faith, obedience and hu-
mility; thought that we should exercise our faith,

[Column 2]
that wisdom might rest down upon the Presidency
and Apostles of the Church in this important
time in our history. 		
	Mrs. E. S. Taylor gave an interesting account
of her visit to Box Elder Stake. Thought we
were too prone to watch each other. “We should
watch ourselves and try to be charitable, for we all
have faults; none are perfect. We should be
very careful not to try and stretch others to fit our
iron bedstead, and remember we are all differently
made; some are weak and some are strong; we
all vary in disposition; God does not want His
people to be cast down; let us be cheerful and
trusting, and make others happy wherever we go.
* * *	Let us practice all the teachings
in Christ’s Sermon on the Mount, that will give
us strength and power to be faithful unto the
end.”
	Mrs, Agnes Swartz spoke of the blessings we
enjoy; said the Lord would try us to see what we
would do; “we expect to be tried; it is good to be
tried.” Prayed the blessings of the Lord to rest
upon His Saints, and asked God to bless Sister
Horne and all that were trying to do some good.
	Mrs. Lucy W. Kimball spoke of her recent re-
turn from the Temple, and the beautiful influence
there; it was wonderful to see the interest taken
in the work for the dead. Related incidents con-
cerning the Prophet Joseph, when in prison and
in chains, and how he rebuked the wicked, and
they ceased their blasphemy, and he had peace
during the remainder of the night. Made other
edifying remarks, and added, “No matter what we
suffer, let us acknowledge the hand of God in all
things.”
	Mrs. M. W. Wilcox spoke of the performance
of duty with an eye single to the glory of God.
Urged us to seek for wisdom, that we might be
able to perform all our duties in righteousness.
	Mrs. C. Smith, Prest. of Draper R. S., Mrs.		
Mary Porter, of the Granger R. S. and Mrs.
Sarah Gibson, Prest. of the R. S. of Sugar
House, each spoke a few minutes, bearing testi-
mony to the truth, and also to the improvement
being made in their respective wards.
	Mrs. M. Pitchforth spoke of our persecutions,
and upon the principle of plural marriage. Said,
“We should never allow our feelings to cause us
to speak against this order. The Gospel helps
the people to improve in their manners, for our
religion teaches us true politeness. It is wrong
for our children to be sent to outside schools, for
the influence there is against the Gospel. Save
your children; keep them in the truth.”
	Mrs. Emma Terry rejoiced that-she had been
born and reared in the Church as she had not the
teachings and old traditions to contend with; she
was sure the Lord would come out of his hiding-
place and rescue us from our enemies. “Plural
marriage is a blessing to all who receive it.”
Spoke of setting good examples, and of many
good things, in an intelligent and edifying manner.
	Mrs. Ann Griffiths spoke feelingly of her ex-
periences in times of trial and of drivings from
our homes, and said many excellent things.
	The Secretary of the Stake, Mrs. E. Howard,
said she hoped all would assemble as often as 
they could to obtain spiritual food; referred to
other important subjects, and closed by repeating
the golden rule, and said, “do not let us esteem
ourselves above others.”
	Prest. Horne made closing remarks, referring to
the most important subjects in an impressive man-
ner, and thought each Relief Society should have
funds on hand if necessary to send one represen-
tative at least to attend the conference. Each
branch should contribute 50 cts. a year to the
contingent fund. Urged the sisters to care for the
sick and the needy, to cooperate with those who
have charge of the Primaries and Sabbath Sohools.
to teach by example as well as precept, to keep
the children out of the streets at night, to culti-
vate every virtue and try to have faith for the

[Column 3]
healing of the sick among us. Touched upon al-
most everything that would promote our happiness
peace and prosperity as a people.
	Conference adjourned for three months. Sing-
ing, “Praise to the man who communed with
Jehovah.” Benediction by Counselor E. S. Tay-
lor. 
	Condensed from the minutes of Mrs. Elizabeth
Howard, Relief Society Stake Secretary.
	Y. L. M. I. A.
	Condensed minutes of the Conference of the
Y. L. M. I: A. of this Stake, held March 18, in
the 14th Ward Assembly Rooms, Coun. M. E.
Dougall presiding. Present on the stand were
Mrs. E. S. Taylor, Prest. of the Central Board,
and her Counselors, Mrs. M. Y. Taylor and Mrs.
M. H. Tingey, also Mrs. S. M. Kimball, Prest.
B. W. Smith, Dr. M. C. Shipp, Mrs. Lillie
Freeze and others. Excellent singing by the 19th
Ward choir. Roll called a majority of the Asso-
ciation reported, minutes of previous conference
were read. The morning session was principally
occupied in reading the statistical reports of the
associations. Remarks were made by Mrs. J. C.
Howe, Mrs Z. G. Eardly, Mrs. E. C. Clawson,
Mrs C. H. Clayton, Mrs. M. Irvine, Mrs. B. W.
Smith, Mrs. L. Freeze, Miss G. Sansome and 
Mrs. Alice Kimball; each bore a faithful testi-
mony to the truth of the work in which we
are engaged, and exhorted the young ladies to
qualify themselves for the great future in store for
them; to study the scrtpture and be able to teach
them to their children; to read good books, and
thereby gain pure and noble thoughts; to attend
good meetings and obtain the Spirit of God,which
is always found there; to be prayerful and obedi-
ent to the commandments of God, and to His
revelations, which have been given expressly to
this people; in short, to so conduct themselves as
to be patterns of nobleness and virtue.
	During the morning session the revelation given 
four years ago to this people through the revela-
tor President Taylor, was read, and was listened
to with rapt attention.
	Conference adjourned until 2 p.m. Singing
prayer by Mrs. Erickson.	
	Afternoon Session: After singing prayer was
offered by Mrs Mary P. Young. Mrs. Dougall
gave the report of the Stake.
	Dr. Maggie Shipp then addressed the meeting;
spoke of charity, urged the young to avoid criti-
cising people’s actions, for such criticisms would
not be just, as we do not understand the motives;
also exhorted them to use their influence for the
good of society. Spoke of tithing and the bless-
ings promised to those who observe that law, and
alluded to other important subjects.
	Mrs. Kimball followed up the subjects intro-
duced by Dr. Shipp and referred to some of her
early experiences in the Church.
	President Elmina S. Taylor then addressed the
meeting in an impressive manner; among other
subjects, spoke upon the home; urged them to
cultivate love for the beautiful, “and even though
the home be a humble one,it can be a most charm-
ing place.” Advised the young ladies to cultivate
faith, and upon other matters of importance.
	Other speakers were Miss Sarah Eddington,
Mrs. Jane Freeze, Coun. M. Y. Dongall, Mrs.
Parkinson, Prest of the Y. L. of Oneida Stake.
Each of these sisters bore faithful testimonies,
and referred to the subjects already mentioned.
	A letter written to the Conference by the Stake
President of Y. L., Mrs. M. A. Freeze, was read,
after which the Conference adjourned for three
months,with singing and prayer.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	ON the 17th of March a small number of the
friends of Sister Elizabeth Goddard met at her
residence in the 13th Ward, in honor of her birth-


[Column 1]
day. On that day she reached the age of seventy
years, the allotted time of three score and ten.
Congratulations were extended, and many happy
returns of the day, from all those assembled, with
increased joy and happiness each year. Pleasant
conversation was indulged in during the afternoon,
and the time passed most agreeably, as it always
does when Latter-day Saints meet to do each
other honor.  A bountiful dinner was heartily
partaken of, and after dinner there was singing by
the head of the household, Elder George God-
dard, and his son, Heber, in which several of the
company present joined. Of those present we
remember Mrs. Catherine Horrocks, Mrs. M. Isa-
bella Horne, Mrs. Elizabeth Howard, Mrs. Rachel
R. Grant. Mrs. Lydia Ann Wells, Mrs. E. B.
Wells, Mrs. Ann Woolley, widow of the late
Edwin D. Woolley, Mr. and Mrs. F. D. Mitchell,
Mr. and Mrs. Will. Dougall, Mr. and Mrs. Joseph
Lyon, Mr. and Mrs. Orson Woolley, Mrs. Mary
S. Goddard and daughter Nellie. Mrs. Goddard
has occupied the position of Secretary of the
Relief Society in the 13th Ward ever since its
permanent organization, and has most efficiently
performed the arduous duties of that important
office to the satisfaction of the Society. Her life,
in some respects smooth, has been rich with events
of importance, and not wanting in the sombre
shading, that gives tone and interest to the lives of
individuals, and has a tendency to elevate and
strengthen human character. Aside from domes-
tic duties and the care of a large family, thirteen
children in all, and the active responsibility of
Secretary to the Relief Society in her own ward,
Sister Goddard has devoted much time to the
Sabbath School, in which she has been a teacher
many, many years. In fact her life has been
replete with usefulness and good works. As one
of her first acquaintances in the valley, we most
heartily and sincerely congratulate her on having
attained the age of seventy with all her faculties
unimpaired,and with such bright prospects before
her of enjoying the evening of her life with the
husband of her youth, after forty and more years
of wedded happiness.  May Bro. and Sister
Goddard live to celebrate their golden wedding,
and together enjoy all the blessings of the new
and everlasting covenant, is our earnest wish and
desire.
[Column 2]
We should rise up en masse and claim our rights
as guaranteed to us by law. But such a changable
and shaky, bogus law we could put no trust in;
but if we could reach the President, and he would
listen to reason and common sense, which I be-
lieve he would, we would tell him that we are
trying to lead a virtuous, honest, moral, upright
and Christian life, and we offer up our daily pray-
ers to our Heavenly Father, that He will bestow
upon us His Holy Spirit to lead and guide us into
all truth, and that the people of Utah, in the ma-
jority, are a law-abiding people; that they desire
peace, and seek to pursue a course that is honor-
able and upright in the sight of all good men and
women. We would prohibit the use of liquor, to
lead to drunkenness, also gambling places, houses
of ill fame, and many other vices that the law
sustains. Against our own judgment and moral
conduct, our would-be reformers and oppressors
tell us this is civilization, and they say our young
people ought to be free to walk in by and forbidden
paths.
	We agree with Henry Ward Beecher, when he
said, “Bring up your sons and daughters in purity
of life, and let them not mingle with the evil in-
fluence that blots the moral character and cannot
be erased.”  The Latter-day Saints are taking
this side of the question. We will set up our
guide-boards to our youth, and write thereon this
motto: “Keep yourselves pure and unspotted from
the evils  of the world, and walk by the light of
truth, that you may go down to your graves in
honor and peace.” The world has become very
wicked, and her sins have reached up unto the
ears of the God of Sabaoth, and these words are
written on the wall, “Mene, Mene, Tekel, Uphar-
sin.”  The glory of our grand old Republican
Constitution has departed, and the Edmunds-
Tucker bill has burst asunder the national bands,
whose links were made strong by that instrument,
life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness, which
granted independance of mind and religious free-
dom to all its subjects; and now this partial law
grants rights to other Territories and deprives
Utah of those same rights. Where, oh where can
the justice of these measures be found? No
where, and they will tumble to pieces with the
usage. Changing an impartial law to a partial one
leads to all its attendant evils—coercion, disrespect
for other’s rights, abuse of Federal power, con-
tortion, greed for other’s hard earned means, con-
fusion and disruption of tranquility and peace.
Those who live under these disruptive technicali-
ties have no safety in the pursuit of happiness;
and when a nation takes retrograde steps it is
soon to fall in its greatness.
	I will here prophecy that not many years hence
the present state of affairs will bring about a great
revolution. This nation has risen to its zenith.
The law of God is set aside, and the fundamental
principles of the holy Scriptures are disregarded
as idle tales. Where can there be an excuse
found with Bibles all over the land? How many
are there that read the word of God and act by its
wise counsels and its noble precepts? They can-
not be found in the halls of Congress, for surely
not much of its spirit is infused into their hearts
or they would not clamor so hard for injustice.
	But we have many friends abroad, who look at
things the same as we do, who see destruction in
the whirlpool. We thank them for the offset they
made against unconstitutional measures, and those
who favor Woman Suffrage, such as Judge Black,
Senator Vest, Call, McVeagh, Bennett and many
others, that I do not now call to mind. We also
thank the Memorial Committee of women who
went before the President and spoke in our behalf.
The majority of women of this Territory are
suffragists, and while we exercised the power to
vote we were always treated with the greatest re-
spect at the polls; men and women cast in their
votes and walked peaceably away to their several
homes, and attended to their various occupations

[Column 3]
as on any other day, and the many years that I
have voted I have not seen any brawls, conten-
tions, drunkenness or rabbles, or anything to dis-
turb the peace on election day. We have always
had the right to vote in Church affairs, and voting
has been no new thing to us, to have a voice in
appointments. We have all the rights accorded
us that we desire, and by our own appointment we
vote for those who are men of honor and integrity
of character; our vote is united for a good man,
and not for a mean one.
	The wholesale bill’s ruling as it passed the two
houses of Congress, is taken up by Judge C.
Zane and stretched out to condemn everybody. It
does not stop with the Latter-day Church, but
will condemn all members of churches for its es-
tablished belief in doctrine, if derived or estab-
lished from the Bible—for it upholds plural mar-
riage throughout all its pages, and God declares
Himself the friend of those who practiced it. The
Christian world will have to deny the Bible teach-
ings, or they will abet, counsel and uphold plural
marriage, although they do not practice it, but are
parties knowing that the Bible upholds and sus-
tains this practice. “The rich man lifted up his
eyes in hell and saw Lazarus in Abraham’s
bosom” in a state of happiness. Now, God and
Abraham agreed as touching this matter, and
God has commanded His people to practice it,
and He will be their friend, and His law, made by
Him, will stand forever.
	Latter-day Saints, stand by  it, be firm, and do
not falter. Mr. Zane, are you or any of your as-
sociates believers in the Bible? Did not those
who wrote in it practice, teach and uphold this
doctrine of plural wives? If you do, how can
you conscientiously take that test oath?  I would
advise all believers in the Bible, that do not be-
lieve in it, to be careful how they use the name of
Deity in taking an oath upon such sacred matters
as concern Ecclesiastical church members, that
the law has no legal claim upon. What a mixture
of State and Church, and what a mixed up state
of affairs to hasten the end.
	M. A. M. PRATT.
	Salt Lake City, March 25th, 1887.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


A WOMAN ON THE QUESTION OF
				RIGHT.
	The women of this community are going to
have a voice in the new law, or wholesale inquisi-
tion law, or whatever name it can be called by.
The widows of this Territory are lawful voters, as
I can prove, that when the right of franchise was
taken from us widows, in the fall of 1882, the
highest law in the land accorded us that right.
For threes years we were excluded from voting,
although we had had the right for fourteen years;
therefore, we were prohibited unlawfully; if not,
why did the law decide, in the year 1884, that the
widows were lawful voters, and they availed them-
selves of this privilege, and cast in their vote
without challenge, and to the aforesaid complaint
individually they sustained damage to the amount
of twelve hundred dallars and costs of suit. We
still remain widows, and have broken no law, and
are innocent of any offense whatever; there has
been nothing adjudged against us, and we are in-
nocent until the law proves us guilty.
    What confidence can we find in such supreme
laws, that will acknowledge and sustain its people
in certain rights, and in a short time after, before,
their words get cold, and they, take time to draw a
long breath, they condemn all they have done
formerly, and pounce upon the innocent persons
that their law set on their case and proved them
lawful voters.
    It appears to me that the law on these points is
open to the widows of this Territory for appeal.


APPRECIATION OF THE EXPONENT.

EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	DEAR SISTER: - The inmates of the Deseret 
Hospital, in which you have so long occupied the
position of Secretary and Treasurer, wish to re-
turn their sincere thanks to you and several
others for your valuable papers. I have often
heard the remark, during my two years' residence
in the hospital, "May God bless Sister Wells for
remembering us with her paper."
	Could you realize the good feeling of these true 
hearts who utter these words, it would lighten, I 
am sure, some of the many cares of your busy life.
If you could find time to write a few lines to our
suffering ones, who so often wish they could see
you in person (although I have explained to them
how fully your time is occupied) it would be 
another "crust of bread cast upon the water to re-
turn after many days."
	Thanks for the last papers just received, which
are at the present time in the hands of the patients,
brightening and relieving the tedium of weary
hours. I truly hope those whose names I so often
see in your good paper, will remember the sick
and suffering in their good wishes and prayers,
and also the working corps, although not needing, 
perhaps, the encouragement of the pen, yet solicit
a place in the faith and prayers of the faithful,
that we may ever be found trying in our humble 
way to do even as He would have us do. Though
I am alone, being far from every earthly tie, yet
I am not alone, for God has led me into the 
House of Truth, even into the midst of His own,
and the comfort of this gives to me and my co-
workers strength for our daily duties. We live


day by day hoping, if ever so little, that we are
helping in our Father's vinyard, and, like you,
dear sister, proving a comfort to many afflicted
ones.
	Our prayer is that you may long be spared to
continue your good work.
			Yours most sincerely,
				   MRS. JENNIE WHIPPLE,
				Matron of Deseret Hospital.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿TO THE Y. L. M. I. A.,
	RUSHVILLE DISTRICT, CLIFTON, IDAHO.

MY DEAR YOUNG FRIENES:
	How glad I am to meet you all once more
at our meeting. How blest we are, and how
happy we should be! We have kind parents,
who supply all our temporal wants, brothers
and sisters, friends and relatives, with whom
we associate daily, and here we are privileged
to meet once a week, to exchange our thoughts
and ideas, to be a mutual benefit. Each one
can gain some knowledge from the experience
all have had since our last meeting.
	There is no need to fear and tremble, when
we arise to speak, but rather cultivate confi-
dence in ourselves by speaking at every meet-
ing we attend, and by study and research dur-
ing the time we have between our meetings.
The practice of reading is a great help to
speaking. In your reading, be in earnest, al-
ways getting a perfect understanding of what
you read. Telling or writing in our own lan-
guage what we have read is an excellent way
to impress it upon our minds ready for use at
some future time.
	Besides these blessings, we should rejoice in
the knowledge that we have been privileged to
come upon the earth at this favored time, and
that we have been counted worthy to be num-
bered among the Saints of God; we should also
rejoice to know that we have come through the
royal lineage of the new and everlasting coven-
ant, heirs to all the blessings and privileges
belonging thereto.
	Now, we are all over the age of accounta-
bility; God holds each one of us accountable
for our actions. Each one has to form or
make her own character; be it good or bad,
that depends upon each one of us individually.
We are forming a character day by day. The
acts of each day form, as it were, a stone of the
main structure. How careful we should be to
have them good.
	As we pass through life, we wield an influ-
ence either good or bad, and this influence
bears upon our neighbor in a certain degree,
hence we are not only responsible for our ac-
tions, but the influence we may carry with us.
Now is the time we are building the founda-
tion of our character, and it is necessary for
us to know that we have a permanent one, one
that will stand through the trials and tempta-
tions of a life-time.
	Love should fill each heart; kindness should
dictate every action of our life; mercy should
be on our right hand and charity on our left.
The love of God and our fellow beings should
abound in our bosoms all the day long. We
should be slow to anger and quick to forgive.
We should never hold hatred against any one,
and above all, let virtue and chastity be our
crowning pride.
						D. E. DUDLEY.


[Column 1]
R. S., Y. L. M. I. A. & P. A. REPORTS

	EASTERN ARIZONA STAKE.
	The quarterly conference of the Relief So-
ciety of this Stake was held at Snowflake,
Friday, March 11th, 1887, commencing at 2
p.m., Mrs. Emma S. Smith presiding. After
singing, prayer was offered by Bro. Oscar

[Column 2]
Mann. Singing again. Verbal reports of ten
branches were given by their respective presi-
dents.
	Prest. Smith expressed herself much pleased
with the good attendance, and was thankful
that we were permitted to meet under such
favorable circumstances, and also pleased with
the good reports.
    Sister Sarah Driggs made a few encouraging
remarks, and made some suggestions in regard
to conducting the working meetings.
	The statistical and financial report was then
read.
    Coun. Frances White said her prayers were
continually offered for the unity of the Saints.
Spoke of the good the Society of St. Johns
had done in aiding the poor, and urged all to
be diligent in keeping the commandments of
God.
    Sister Mary J. Minnerly felt that although
we have many difficulties to labor under, we
are continually being blest and prospered. De-
sired the faith and prayers of the sisters in
her behalf, and spoke upon the necessity of
children attending the Primary.
	Prest. Emma S. Smith exhorted all to be
faithful in the discharge of their duties. Sug-
gested that each Society have one of their 
members appointed to take charge of the busi-
ness, in order to lighten the burden of the
president. “Mothers should teach their child-
ren the principles of truth and honesty, and
should seek to guard them against infidelity.”
	Conference adjourned for three; months.
Singing. Benediction by Bro. L. H. Hatch.
	DELLA FISH, Secretary.

	BOX ELDER STAKE.
	Condensed report of the quarterly conference
of the Relief Society of Box Elder Stake,
which convened at 10 o’clock on the 8th of
March, in the Tabernacle, Brigham City,
Sister Harriet A. Snow presiding. Editor
of the WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Mrs. Emmeline
B. Wells, was on the stand, and the Stake
Board and representatives from the various
branches of the R. S. of the Stake. After
singing, prayer was offered by Coun. Susannah
T. Booth.
    Prest. H. A. Snow made some opening re-
marks.
    Sister Annie Wixom was nominated Secre-
tary pro tem. Roll called. Minutes of pre-
vious conference were read and accepted; also
the reports from the different branches.
	Prest. H. A. Snow: “I feel thankful to be
here, and see so many of my sisters present;
we want to be very particular in our duties,
that our associations may be in perfect order.
We cannot vote any more, but one loss is one
little gain; we can advocate the principle of
celestial marriage. Knowledge will give us
confidence in ourselves; we can acquire this by
keeping the commandments of the Lord in
private and in public. The daughters of Zion
have a work to perform, and if we are faithful
to our privileges we will be a power in Zion, a
help to the brethren. That we may grow in
grace and knowledge is my prayer.
    Sister E. B. Wells: “My beloved sisters and
brethren, I am happy to be here with you. I
have enjoyed the reading of the minutes and
reports. Through these organizations, that
have been established in our midst, we become
acquainted with each other’s condition. No
matter where you are, if you are zealous in
good works, and energetic in the performance
of duties, you will receive wisdom and knowl-
edge.” Referred to the sentiments expressed
by senators on our question, and spoke in re-
gard to our present circumstances, and of hav-
ing confidence in each other, and not judging
each other hastily; said these days reminded
her of the days of Nauvoo. “We must not
say all we think; it is better to stop and con-

[Column 3]
sider before speaking and answering questions,
and we cannot be too careful; we must seek for
wisdom in all our ways. We who have been
interested in the Relief Society can testify that
it has been one of the greatest educational
privileges we have ever enjoyed; I am sure I 
can. I think if there ever was a people on
the face of the earth who are practical as well
as spiritual, they may be found in the Relief
Society. The time has come when women are
remembered, and I believe in the future they
and their good works will be remembered
more than in the past. Things are not so bad
as they might be; if we keep the Spirit of the
Gospel, the Spirit of the Lord, our trials will
not be so heavy as we anticipate. We shall
endure unto the end if we keep that Spirit,
and it is to those who endure that the reward
is given.”
Singing, “Zion stands with hills surrounded.”
Benediction. Adjourned until 2 p.m.
	Afternoon session: Sister E. S. Taylor had
arrived, and was with us.  After the opening
exercises, the Secretary read the condensed re-
port, also the report of the Stake treasury.
	Sister Harriet Hunsaker of Honeyville:
“We are living in a great day; many have de-
sired to live in this dispensation. I feel grate-
ful that we have passed through so many
scenes, and are still numbered among the Lat-
ter-day Saints. It is well for us to look to our-
selves, and see if we are standing on the right
foundation.”
Sister M. A. Hubbard: “We have great rea-
son to rejoice if we possess the Spirit of the
Lord while passing through every scene; I re-
joice in the principles of the Gospel. We
have everything to encourage us. Let us al-
ways feel subject to the Priesthood, cherish and
sustain them.
	Sister E. B. Wells said: “In regard to the
world, there never was more inquiring con-
cerning this people than at the present time."
Spoke eloquently in regard to having all the
laws tested. Said, “If those who feel weak
will bear their testimony, it will strengthen
them. We should uphold and sustain the
Priesthood. I believe it is every one's privil-
ege to have that Spirit within that will direct
them through all places and show them the
truth. Some say, ‘I have a strong testimony,
but cannot give it.’ ” She believed it necessary
to testify for the good of others. I can testify
that I have lived in the principle of plural
marriage many years, and enjoy the associa-
tion and blessing attending it. No condition is
more conducive to the cultivation of human
character, to the putting away of selfishness
and disciplining one’s self. To love a sister-
wife is indeed noble. We shall enjoy the sweet
Spirit of the Lord, and our trials will be sancti-
fied for our good if we go through them with-
out murmuring and complaining, but if we
always murmur, the Spirit of the Lord will
leave us.” Encouraged the sisters to bear
their testimony to the world, to write for the
EXPONENT. “I have always said from the be-
ginning of this persecution, that we will be
pretty well instructed in law and government
before we get through. I believe it is just as
necessary that women should understand princi-
ple, and be able to answer any question proper-
ly, as it is that our brethren should, who go out 
to preach the Gospel. Those who come here of
late, generally expect us to be nearly perfect;
if we are not, we certainly are striving to be.
My prayer is that we may gain the victory."
Prest. E. S. Taylor: "I think there is one
good thing accomplished by this persecution,
that of drawing the Saints together; no matter,
as long as we have the love of the Gospel we
are united; they may put our brethren in
prison, but as long as it tends to purge out the
hypocrite of Zion I think we do not need to
fear our enemies, but more false brethren and
false sisters. Some say we are given to pride


[Column 1]

each one can examine themselves. The great-
est gift is charity; it covereth a multitude of
sins; we must do to others as we would that
they should do to us. No one can do the work
for us; each one has to do her part, and stand
on her own individuality; I thank God for it.
Every good act the Lord will reward, for He
is just; as Sister Wells said, 'If we always had
justice we should not be tried at all.'" Spoke
of the beauty of wives putting themselves into
each other's place; "do to others as you would
like to be done by; the same with those who
have help, and those who work for others; we
are all selfish, but we should cultivate the
Spirit of Christ. Let us be thankful if we
understand principles better than others, and
teach and have charity for those who don't.
As Sister Wells said, 'Try to get the spirit of
every principle.' When we get so firmly estab-
lished, that we have a love of it, we shall see
the beauty of the principle of plural marriage;
we shall feel that Father is at the helm,
full of mercy and love; let us kiss the rod
whenever we are smitten."
	Emilia D. Madsen made a few remarks:
referred to the subject of doing good to others,
and quoted the Savior's words, "Some have en-
tertained angels unawares," ect.; also, "Inas-
much as ye have done it unto one of these
little ones, ye have done it unto me," and gave
excellent advice on these two subjects.
	Sister Peery of Three Mile Creek: "I think
we have had a feast; what are we without the
Spirit of the Lord? He is a Father and
friend in very deed; let us be humble and
prayerful; He knoweth the desire of our
heart."
	Sister Wixom and Sister Dewey each spoke
a short time.
	Prest. Harriet Snow: "I feel thankful for
this conference, and what has been said; I wish
we had time to hear from all the presidents."
	Singing, "How firm a foundation." Bene-
diction by Coun. Olivia Widerborg.
		EMILIA D. MADSEN, Cor. Sec.

Y. L. M. I. A., BOX ELDER.
	The Y. L. M. I. A. Conference of Box Elder
Stake, convened in the Tabernacle at Brigham 
City, March 9, 1887, a large assembly of young
ladies being present. On the stand were Terri-
torial Supt., Elmina S. Taylor, Stake Presi-
dency, branch presidents and representatives;
also Harriet A. Snow, Prest. of the Relief So-
ciety of Box Elder Stake.
	After the usual preliminary exercises, em-
bracing roll, minutes and reports from nine of
the various associations, read by the Secretary,
Lydia S. Pierce. introductory remarks were
offered by Supt. Minnie J. Snow.
	The first exercise on the programme was an
essay on health, creditably given by Susan
Hubbard of the Willard Association. Lecture
upon the "Personality of God," by L. S. Pierce,
giving proofs from the Bible, and Phena
Madsen proofs from Church works. The sub-
ject was efficiently treated upon, each showing
a thorough research and comprehension of the
subject. A vocal selection, "Ever Constant,"
was sweetly rendered by Miss Martha Wright
of the 1st Ward, Brigham City.
	Territorial Supt., E. S. Taylor, addressed
the assembly: In remarking upon young
ladies educating themselves to fill useful posi-
tions, advised the study of phonography.
Spoke in high praise of the able manner in
which the preceding lectures were given. Said,
"The fact of our Lord and Savior appearing
and revealing the plan of salvation to Joseph
Smith, an illiterate youth, should ever be an
invincible proof to us of the truth of our re-
ligion." Considered woman's franchise a
privilege given of God. Though now unjustly
denied our rights in this regard, believed they
would be restored in the near future. "It is
natural for women to be just and love morality;

[Column 2]

were they allowed to legislate and create laws
there would be no drinking saloons and other
institutions of immorality and vice in our
midst." Referred to present fulfilment of
prophecy; believed the Mormon girls the
brightest, as a majority, of any class with
whom she had met in the world. Encouraged
them, through prayer and watchfulness, to
fortify themselves against temptation. Ad-
vised the observance of the commandment,
"Honor thy parents," and claim the blessings
promised as a reward. Condemned tight lac-
ing and other practices abusing the laws of
health, and advised the cultivation of good
taste in dress.
	After singing and prayer, conference ad-
journed until 2 p.m.
	Afternoon session opened by singing, "I'll
serve the Lord while I am young." Prayer
was offered by Prest. Harriet Snow. Con-
densed report of the Stake read, after which
Alvira Rees was presented by Supt. E. S.
Taylor and sustained as Corresponding Secre-
tary for the Stake.
	Subject, "The higher culture of woman,"
was treated upon in a practical and impressive
manner by Coun. Hattie Jensen. "Will the
good old times come back again," was sung by
Misses Ettie and Louisa Madsen of the 3rd
Ward.
	Encouraging and instructive remarks were
made by Avilla Christensen of Bear River
City Ward, Eliza Davis and Ettie Madsen of
the Fourth Ward.
	Sister Taylor expressed her pleasure with
the exercises and manner of presenting the
programme. Offered instructions regarding
the order and laws necessary to be observed in
governing these associations. Dwelt upon the
duties of officers. "Where organizations are
effected, the Stake Supt. of Y. L. M. I. A.
should be present; also the Bishop of the ward.
When difficulties arise in these associations, go
for counsel to the Stake Supt., and should the
matter not be satisfactorily adjusted, appeal to
the Territorial Supt., before engaging the at-
tention of the Priesthood.
	Prest. M. J. Snow expressed a pride and
pleasure in laboring with the intelligent young
ladies comprising these associations, and noting
the rapid improvement and progress apparent
in our midst.
	"All is Well," was sung by the assembly 
with spirit and feeling. The benedictory
prayer was offered by Prest. Marion Peery.
Conference adjourned for three months.
	On the evening preceding our conference
about thirty of the officers of the Y. L. M. I.
A. of Brigham City, some from the neighbor-
ing towns, and a few invited guests, assembled
at the residence of Prest. Minnie J. Snow,
where the evening was pleasantly and profitably
spent. Mrs. Lydia S. Pierce, Misses Ettie
Madsen and Alvira Rees acted as programme
committee, Presidents Fannie Graehl, S. A.
Wrighton, C. Hanson and Freddie Widerborg
as committee on refreshments.
	The exercises commenced with singing. The
following address was given by the Prest:

"My Dear Young Sisters and Co-laborers in the
Cause of Improvement:

	"In obedience to the request of our pro-
gramme committee, I arise to offer a cordial
greeting and a hearty welcome to all present.
	We have gathered this evening to meet with
our beloved and esteemed Prest., Sister Elmina
S. Taylor, and in behalf of the young ladies
of the Mutual Improvement, offer our hospi-
tality and welcome her to our city, to listen to
words of advice, counsel and instruction, that
the Lord shall inspire her to utter; also to par-
take of the blessings of sociability and friend-
ship, increased and intensified by that love
known only to those having a knowledge of
the Gospel. How comforting, beautiful and

[Column 3]

grand are the principles of our holy religion,
and how blessed are we to be chosen from
among the many to labor in the Lord's vine-
yard for the establishment of these glorious
truths in the hearts of the fair daughters of
Israel.
	"We should indeed feel proud of such a
mission, and, sensing its importance, should
ever inspire our hearts, stimulate our actions,
elevate our ambitions and direct our energies,
to prove to our Heavenly Father, angels and
people, that we are worthy of that noble call-
ing.
	"When life's dream is ended, may we leave
behind unmistakable traces of an earnest, ac-
tive and faithful life; then, when meeting our
Savior at His throne of glory, merit the wel-
come plaudit, 'Well done, thou good and faith-
ful servant,, enter thou into the joys of thy
Lord." To realize the fulfillment of this
glorious hope it is necessary that the spirit of
obedience be cultivated, selfishness repressed,
union and confidence be preserved. May the
blessings of God be ours in rich abundance,
the spirit of intelligence and love flow from
heart to heart, making this a season of rejoic-
ing long to be remembered with pleasure and
satisfaction."
	The company now repaired to the dining
room, and surrounded the tastefully spread
tables, with their inviting supplies, generously 
provided by the young ladies.
	Grace was offered by President Harriet A.
Snow, who invoked the blessings of God upon
these His daughters. Our hearts ascended to
God in prayer for Apostle Lorenzo Snow, de-
prived of this, his former home, and the so-
ciety of his family, that peace, comfort and the
blessings of heaven descend upon him accord-
ing to his requirements.
	The ceremony of discussing the tempting
viands came next, and was disposed of most
satisfactorily, every one throwing off all re-
straint and vieing with each other to be agree-
able and contribute to the enjoyment of the
occasion. The bright young faces, the intelli-
gent countenances, beaming with smiles, and
the beautiful surroundings, presented, indeed,
a pretty picture. The supper over, all were
seated in the parlor, when Sister Taylor ad-
dressed the company as follows:
	"I am happy to be here; it does my heart
good to see your bright, happy faces, glowing
with intelligence and truth; also to witness
 your union. I thank you sincerely for the
kindness shown me to-night. It is truly grati-
fying to associate with young ladies on occa-
sions of this kind, who can deport themselves
politely, with propriety, and yet be filled with
joy and mirth. If proper, I would wish that
your lives always be pleasant and happy, and
your trials very light. May God watch over
you and keep you pure."
	The rest of the evening was devoted to
speeches, testimonies, reading, interspersed with
vocal and instrumental music, until a late hour,
when ended a very interesting occasion, par-
ticularly remarkable for the freedom and sweet
spirit of genuine good will that characterized
every feature of the entertainment.
	Respectfully,
		ALVIRA REES, Cor. Sec.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿ORGANIZATION.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	A special meeting of the Beaver Relief So-
ciety, having been called on the 16th of Dec.,
1886, to consider the advisability of dividing
the Society and organizing one in each ward, it
was unanimously agreed by the Stake Presi-
dency, Bishops, and Society, that it would be
proper and in keeping with the times to do so.
The former organization was dissolved, and
presidents chosen for the two wards. The


meeting then adjourned until the 31st of Dec.,
when the organizations were completed with
the following officers:
	For the First Ward, Ruth W. Tyler, Presi-
dent; Jane R. Baldwin and Charlotte Carlow,
Counselors; Alice M. T. Tanner, Secretary;
Mary A. White, Treasurer. For the Second
Ward, Harriet E. Fotheringham, President;
Mary Campbell and Orissa Harris, Counselors;
Hettie Gentry, Secretary; Mary E. Murdock,
Treasurer.		
	The sisters are taking hold with renewed
energy and diligence, to accomplish that for
which the Societies are organized, viz., to assist
the bishops in looking after the needy, etc. A
great deal has already been accomplished in
each ward, and all feel that the division of the
Society will be productive of much good. The
utmost union and harmony exists, and there
never was a time when there was more interest
taken than at present.
		LUCINDA HOWD, Prest.,
		ALICE M. T. TANNER, Sec. pro tem.
	Beaver City, U. T., March 10, 1887.


WOMAN’S  EXPONENT.


ANOTHER ART CRAZE.

	The latest art work among ladies is
known as the “French Craze” for decorating
china, glassware, etc. It is something entirely
new, and is both profitable and fascinating. It
is very popular in New York, Boston and other
Eastern cities. To ladies desiring to learn the
Art, we will send an elegant china placque (18
inches,) handsomely decorated, for a model, to-
gether with a box of material, 100 colored de-
signs assorted in flowers, animals, soldiers,
land scapes, etc., complete, with full instruc-
tions, upon receipt of only $1.00. The placque
alone is worth more than the amount charged.
To every lady ordering this outfit who encloses
the address of five other ladies interested in
Art matters, to whom we can mail our new
catalogue of Art Goods, we will enclose extra
and without charge, a beautiful 30 inch, gold-
tinted placque.
	Address, The Empire News Co., Syracuse,
N.Y.

containing thirty-two pieces of choice and popu-
lar music, full sheet music size, with complete

the Little feet; Oh, You Pretty Blue-eyed

A Year Ago; Bachelor's Hall; Ruth and I;
Good Night; One Happy Year Ago; Jennie in
the Orchard; The Old Barn Gate; Jack's Fare-
well; Polly; Whisper in the Twilight, This is
a very fine collection of real vocal gems, and
gotten up in very handsome style. Published
in the usual way and bought at a music store,
these 32 pieces would cost you $11.20. We 
bought a job lot of this music at a great sacri-
fice, and as the holidays are past, we desire to
close out our stock at once. Will send you the
entire collection, well wrapped and postpaid,
for only 40 cts. Send immediately.
	Address, The Empire News Co., Syracuse,
N. Y.


MISCELLANEOUS.

	He who shall introduce into public affairs
the principles of primitive Christianity will
revolutionize the world.—Benjamin Franklin.

		Up to labor! from thee shaking
			Off the bonds of sloth, be brave!
		Give thyself to prayer and waking,
			Toil, some fainting heart to save!
							—Miss Bremer.

	Happiness is a roadside flower, growing on
the highways of usefulness; plucked, it shall
wither in thy hand; passed by, it is fragrance
to thy spirit. Trample the thyme beneath the
feet; be useful, be happy.—Tupper.


OBITUARIES.

	Died, at Meadow, Millard Co., Feb. 11, 1887, Sarah L.
Stott Bond, in her forty-fifth year, after a short, but severe
illness. Sister Bond emigrated from London, England,
while yet a girl, singly and alone, and came to Fillmore,
where she became the wife of William Stott, to whom she
bore a family of children, three of whom are now alive.
She was left a widow, and was married to Wm. Bond but
a few months previous to her death. She died as she had
lived in full faith of the Gospel. She was much respected
as the President of the Primary Association of this place,
which position she filled with honor from the organization
of the Association. At her funeral consoling remarks were 
made by Bishop H. B. Bennett and Elders William
Stewart and John Nield.
				
						ELIZABETH BUSHNELL,
						HANNAH STEWART,
									Counselors.

	[The following obituary notice has been overlooked, for
which we are very sorry, yet feel, although late, should
find a place in the columns of our paper:—ED.]

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	With feelings of deep sadness I record the death of
another faithful, untiring and dearly beloved member of
the Relief  Society of Kamas. Sister Mary Lewis departed
this life May 29th, in her fifty-ninth year, leaving to mourn
her loss her beloved companion, Bro. Morgan Lewis, with 
whom she had gathered to Zion from their native country,
Wales, and with whom she had uncomplainingly passed
through such hardships as few, even of the early settlers of
these mountain vales, were called to endure. Together
they had nursed through thirteen years of suffering a dearly
loved daughter, who was in childhood stricken with that
terrible malady, rheumatism, and lingered in much suffer-
ing for years, till her pure spirit was freed by death. In a
little less than four years her mother has followed her, 
leaving, also, a daughter and four sons, the oldest of whom
is a helpless invalid, having for two years been afflicted with
the same disease that carried off his sister. For his sake
the fond mother desired to tarry, saying, "Joseph is getting
better now, and I long to see him once more on his feet;
and to His will I bow." In her the afflicted of our com-
munity have found a true and tender consoler. She was
ever patient, cheerful and charitable, and our Society has
lost one of the most faithful of its members. The deepest
sorrow and sympathy is felt for the bereaved family, and 
fervently we pray the blessings of God on them, that they
may acknowledge His hand, and that they may follow her
bright example.
							 LAURA C. PACK.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
		The Rights of the Women of Zion, and the Rights of the Women of all Nations.
VOL. 15.			SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, APRIL 15, 1887.			No. 22.


MY KATE.

She was not pretty as women I know,
And yet all your best made of sunshine and snow
Drop to shade, melt to nought in the long-trodden ways,
While she's still remembered on warm and cold days—
					My Kate.

Her air had a meaning, her movements a grace;
You turned from the fairest to gaze on her face:
And when you had once seen her forehead and mouth,
You saw as distinctly her soul and her truth—
					My Kate.

Such a blue inner light from her eyelids outbroke,
You looked at her silence and fancied she spoke;
When she did, so peculiar and soft was the tone,
The loudest spoke also, you heard her alone—
					My Kate.

I doubt if she said to you much that could act
As a thought or suggestion; she did not attract
In the sense of the brilliant or wise; I infer
'Twas her thinking of others, made you think of her—
					My Kate.

She never found fault with you, never implied
Your wrong by her right, and yet men at her side
Grew nobler, girls purer, as through the whole town
The children were gladder that pulled at her gown—
					My Kate.

None knelt at her feet confessed lovers in thrall;
They knelt more to God than they used, that was all;
If you praised her as charming, some asked what you
	meant,
But the charm of her presence was felt when she went—
					My Kate.

The weak and the gentle, the ribald and rude,
She took as she found them, and did them all good;
It always was so with her—see, what you have!
She has made the grass greener even here, with her grave—
					My Kate.

My dear one!—when thou wert alive with the rest,
I held thee the sweetest and loved thee the best:
And now thou art dead, shall I not take thy part
As thy smiles used to do for thyself; my sweet Heart—
					My Kate.
			ELIZABETH BARRETT BROWNING.


[Column 1]

﻿TO THE PEOPLE'S PARTY.

	When the members of the Territorial Cen-
tral Committee of the People's Party assembled
in the City Hall at 3 o'clock, Monday, April
4th, 1887, the sub-committee, composed of
Messrs. Junius F. Wells, John T. Caine, S. R.
Thurman, Thomas D. Dee and Alma Eldredge,
appointed to prepare an address to the voters,
reported the following, which, after some dis-
cussion, was unanimously adopted and signed
by the members present:	

ADDRESS TO THE PEOPLE'S PARTY OF THE
TERRITORY OF UTAH.

	In view of the recent congressional legisla-
tion, in relation to the right to vote, act as
jurors and hold office in the Territory, we feel
it incumbent upon us, as representatives of the
People's Party, to direct attention to some of
the salient points of the new law, point out
the restrictions which it imposes and awaken
your interest in the maintenance of the rights
and privileges of citizenship which are yet left
you. As to voters, the most cruel and sweep-
ing provision of the law is that which takes
the electoral franchise from the women of the
Territory. Under its provisions no woman will
be permitted to register or vote. As to male
voters of the People's Party, there is no pro-

[Column 2]

vision in the law which need necessarily re-
duce their numbers. The oath prescribed
therein, while in the nature of a test oath and
repugnant to the spirit of liberty and the
genius of free government, which every citizen
should cherish, is not regarded as a test of
faith or religious belief. The authors and
strongest advocates of the bill in Congress
maintained that it, in no sense, abridged the
right of conscience or curtailed freedom of
belief. Accept that interpretation of this
oath, and its whole intent and scope are to
prohibit actual violators of the law from vot-
ing, acting as jurors or holding office.
	The questions that intending voters need,
therefore, ask themselves are these: Are we
guilty of the crimes in said act? or,have we the
present intention of committing those crimes,
or of aiding, abetting, counseling or advising
any other person to commit them? Male per-
sons who can answer these questions in the
negative can qualify under the existing laws
as voters and office-holders: Some may not
understand fully the import of the words, "aid,
abet, counsel or advise." "Aid or abet" is a
phrase having a well-defined legal signification.
It means to knowingly assist the principal in
the commission of an unlawful act. As to
counseling and advising, no man in the com-
munity is expected to counsel or advise any
person to commit crime.
	All who can subscribe to the above, duty
calls to wakefulness and activity. The politi-
cal control of the Territory, of the counties
and the cities, still remains in the hands of the
People's Party, if you employ the power which
is left you. But there must be no backsliding
nor indifference among you. You should not
fail to exercise your rights and defend them.
It is not a time to indulge in "bogus" senti-
ment, but it is a time when every man, who
can do so, should feel it his first and most
sacred duty to exercise the right to preserve
himself and his people from political bondage.
Your ranks, depleted by an important element
—the women voters—can be closed up and
strengthened if every man who is eligible regis-
ters and votes.
	Your attention is called to the communica-
tion of the United States Commissioners di-
rected to the registrars of election (a copy of
which is enclosed) and to the interpretation of
the law contained therein. If their sugges-
tions shall be observed by those to whom they
are directed, fair treatment may be expected at
the registration and polls, and victory will
crown your efforts as heretofore. Should there
be a disposition on the part of any to curtail
you in the exercise of your rights and privi-
leges as herein defined, you should repel any
such effort. Do not let any one mislead or dis-
courage you in the performance of your duty
at the present time. You are the majority of
the people of this Territory. Your labors
have made it habitable. Your representatives
have governed and controled it, in your inter-
ests, faithfully since 1847. They have been
true to the trust imposed in them. They are
entitled to your confidence and support. Should
a change in the political control of this Terri-
tory occur, the disaster which it involves is be-
yond conception. In those districts where
such a fatality has overtaken the people, even
for a short time, they have been made to suffer
untold outrages at the hands of those who
usurped office and exercised power among
them. These things should be remembered by

[Column 3]

the voters of the People's Party, and they
should incite every man to the performance of
his duty.
	Let it not be said that any member of the
People's Party neglected his opportunity. But
let every one, from the commencement of the
registration to the day of election, keep care-
fully in mind the course that he should pursue,
attending to every formality to maintain his
political status and exercise his rights and duties
of citizenship.
	The following are the names of the members
of the committee in attendance: George
W. Thatcher, Cache County; Thomas F.
Rouche, Davis County; F. W. Chappell, Juab
County; James R. Stewart, Morgan County;
Wm. H. Lee, Rich County; John Sharp, John
T. Caine, John R. Winder, Angus M. Cannon,
Feramorz Little, Elias A. Smith and Junius
F. Wells, Salt Lake County; Alma Eldredge,
Summit County; H. S. Gowans, Tooele County;
W. N. Dusenberry, S. R. Thurman and A. O.
Smoot, Jr., Utah County; L. W. Shurtliff and
Thomas D Dee, Weber County; and E. G.
Woolley, Washington Couuty.
	The officers are: Chairman, Hon. John
Sharp; Vice-Chairman, Col. J. R. Winder;
Secretary, Judge E. A. Smith; Corresponding
Secretary, Junius F. Wells, Esq.; Executive
Committee; John Sharp, J. R. Winder, J. F.
Wells, E. A. Smith, F. Little, L. W. Shurtliff,
H. S. Gowans, W. F. Dusenberry and T. F.
Rouche.
	Messrs. E. G. Woolley, of Washington
County, and R. W. Heybourne, of Iron County,
were elected members of the committee, vice
J. M. McFarlane and Edward Dalton, who
have removed from the Territory.
	Before the adjournment of the Central Com-
mittee the executive committee were instructed
to prepare suggestions for the better organiza-
tion of the People's Party in the precincts, and
to formulate a circular for the instruction of
voters.


CORRESPONDENCE

		KIRTLAND, Ohio, April 3rd, 1887.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	If the intrusion upon the literary domain of
the opposite sex calls for an apology, I can
offer two reasons for the same. First, I am
engaged in the same great cause as your able
little journal—the dissemination of truth.
Second, I have very near relatives and friends
belonging to that sex, one of whom took a
prominent part in bringing me into the world,
therefore, I feel under obligations to them.  If
it be considered a compliment, it is certainly
merited, for truly, in my missionary travels I
have seen double the number of copies of the
EXPONENT for free distribution than of any
other sheet published in the interest of Zion,
and it is second to none in its efficacy as a pro-
mulgator of truth. I confess that until I came
on my mission, having never closely perused
the EXPONENT, I had far underestimated its
value in this regard.  But no doubt your sex
are accustomed to this treatment at the hands
of all who undertake to place a limit to their
importance as factors in the sum of human ex-
istence.  For instance, consider the obtuse-
ness of Pharoah's judgment, when, to impede
the dangerous increase of Israel, he ordered
every male child drowned. After eighty years of
this unavailing cruelty, we find a nation where-


in the females outnumbered the males about
seven to one, and the offspring of these much
married fathers were of a quality so superior
to their progenitors, that they witnessed the 
triumphant conquest of the promised land.
Their  marriage relations produced such happy
results, that the poet prophet (Isaiah)
broadly hints that the like condition will be
repeated in the latter days, by a marked scarcity
of the masculine gender.  If this foolish king,
whose name has become a synonym for tyranny,
had changed the drowning process to the other
sex he might have accomplished his purpose.
As it was, history records his miserable failure,
and traces its cause to a misconception of the 
true source of increase.
	The halo which surrounds the early scenes
of Church history so closely interwoven with
the little village from which I write, may cause 
a few lines to be read with interest.
	Appointed by the president of the Penn. Con-
ference to labor a few weeks in this vicinity, I 
arrived here the 10th ult.  Through the kind-
ness of the directors of the town hall I ob-
tained leave to hold three meetings therein,
which I did, bearing testimony with all the
strength God has given me to the divinity of
the Latter-day work as inaugurated by Joseph
Smith, and as carried on by his divinely ap-
 pointed successors, Brigham Young and John
Taylor.  The "Reorganized" have a small 
branch here, and their representatives chal-
lenged me to affirm the question, "Does the
Bible teach and endorse the practice of poly-
gamy?" to which I assented.
	The discussion lasted two evenings, each of
us having two speeches of thirty minutes each
for each evening.  They honored me with their
ablest representative for an opponent, whose
chief forte was as a debater, and who, though
well qualified for brow-beating, was fair and
reasonably courteous, and abstained from the
low, abusive, false accusations so often resorted
to by our enemies of this class.
	He denied that God had ever justified its
practice, or that the Mosaic law had ever sup-
posed or regulated it.  But when confronted 
with the numerous evidences of God's special
favors to Abraham, Jacob and others, and
such passages as Deut. xxi, 15, and xxv, 5,
Ex. xxi, 10, and xxii, 16, with many others of 
like import it was too much even for the 
nimble acrobatic feats of the Scripture twisting
"Reorganized," and he virtually acknowledged
the point.  He then referred to the New Testa-
ment, where, in speaking of the law as a whole
as a "schoolmaster" for the higher law, he 
said, "How could the law justify polygamy
when the book condemns the law?"  He 
claimed the words of the Savior, where he de-
nounces "putting away," and says of the mar-
ried couple, "they twain shall be one flesh,"
were condemnatory of the practice.  I showed 
from Romans, vii, 3, that while a woman is
forbidden to marry another while her husband
lives, the husband is not thus forbidden, and
that they twain may refer to a man and his
second wife, as well as the first, was shown
from 1 Cor., vi, 16.  I then referred to the last
chapters of Judges and the 25th of Numbers,
to show the high standard of morality required
by the Almighty of His people under the "im-
perfect" law.
	My friend now considered it his turn to
rush to the defense of modern methods, and
this valiant champion of morality, whose sensi-
tive olfactories took such Pecksniffian offense
at the loose morality of the ancient Patriarchs
who held converse with Deity, after he had
labored near one and a half hours to show that
Abraham, Jacob and David were bad, sinful
men, after he had lobbied in Washington for the
enactment of oppressive laws for the punish-
ment of the compeers of the maligned patri-
archs for practicing Biblical doctrine, rushed
to the defense of the assailed purity of nine-
teenth century "civilization," and spent much

[Column 2]
of his remaining time in explaining away and
palliating the slight ills that obtains under the
regime of apostate Christianity, and in telling
humorous manufactured stories of married life
in Utah, too foolish to be wicked.
	It surprised me to find the amount of evi-
dence existing to prove that Joseph the Seer
was the pioneer in the practice of plural mar-
riage right here under the very noses of those
who so stiffly deny the fact.  One intelligent 
lady said to me, "The 'Reorganized' Church
made a mistake in the beginning.  If they
had taken the stand simply that polygamy was
wrong, no matter who the author was, they
might appear consistent.  I have evidence 
enough in my possession to convince any one,
but the 'Reorganized' Church do not ask me
for it."   It does not suit them, probably.
Every one in this vicinity, familiar with
Church history, say it was well understood here
that he taught this in the last days of his
eventful life. One eccentric old lady, a devoted 
member of their church, said, "I remember, as
yesterday, when an Elder told me that Joseph
had received a new revelation, authorizing
him to seal more than one woman to a man for
eternity (this while Joseph was living, mind
you), not to live with them for this life.   It
was Brigham that added to that revelation
these words: 'Now they're ours we can do as
we like with them.'"
	Father Bond still lives, though quite feeble,
and is taken care of by his daughter.
	The village is substantially the same as
when described by an abler pen than my own,
and given to your readers in your issue of
March 15th, 1885.  The temple is being re-
paired with a view to hold conference therein,
beginning April 6.  Joseph is already in town,
and all their notables are expected.
	Wishing the EXPONENT deserved success, I
am,				Yours truly,
						    YOUNG ELDER.

			MINERSVILLE, April 12, 1887.
EDITOR EXPONENT.
	Saturday, the 8th of this month, was the 
sixty-ninth birthday of Sister Mary E. Light-
ner of this place.  A number of gentlemen
and ladies met at her residence and gave her
a dinner, which was highly appreciated by all.
	Before partaking of dinner, at her re-
quest we sang the hymn, "Oh, my Father." The
tables, laden with the good things of the earth,
were then surrounded with happy faces.  Sis-
ter Lightner with her brother, James H. Rol-
lins interested her group of friends with a 
review of her life.  A few lines composed for
the occasion were read, after which Elizabeth
Gilbert, Sister Lightner's aunt, who is in her
eighty-second year, made some remarks; the
closing exercises were short speeches by a num-
ber of the sisters who all wished Sister Light-
ner many happy returns of the day.  All 
seemed to enjoy themselves and I believe
we made the hearts of the widowed sisters for-
get their loneliness, at least for one day.
				Respectfully,
						ADA ROLLINS.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


PRIMARY ENTERTAINMEMT.

	The Sixth Ward Primary Association of
Logan had an entertainment on Friday even-
ing, the 11th of March, commencing at half
past seven o'clock. The object was to encourage
the children to speak correctly and cultivate
good manners, and to show the parents what
their children are capable of doing. The
programme was filled entirely by our Primary
children, and was as follows:
	Prayer by Fred Turner, Jr.; "The Welcome,"
by Maud Bowen and company; Ada Jones and
Alice Jacobson, a song, entitled, "Grandpa's


Spectacles," age eight and six years; Harry
and Mary Crowther, recitations from the
Juvenile Instructor, ages five and three years;
Hiram Thorp and John Wilson, a song, en-
titled, "Kitty Wells;" Frank Fulmer, a recita-
tion, entitled "Queer;" Eunice Jacobson, a
song, entitled, "Susan," aged three years; Bettie
Bowen, a recitation, "Rebecca Dobbs;" Bertha
Jones, a song, "Baby Darling;" "Little Wise
Heads," by Mable Barber, Ida Leishman,
Mamie Turner and Ida May Stewart, exceed-
ingly well done; "The Martyrdom of Joseph
and Hyrum," by Dorothy Hansen; Lillian
Parkinson, "Keeping His Word;" Rosella
Thorp, a song, "Little Bessie, the Drunkard's
Child;" Martha Bench, "Take Things as They
Come;" Ida Stewart, a song;  Julia Jacobson,
"The Chimney Sweep," in character; "Harry's
Bravery," by J. W. Pike, Isaac Stewart, Fred 
Turner, Nellie Bowen and Carrie Stewart;
Jane Gordon, a recitation, entitled, "Steenie,"
aged six years; Ida Leishman, a song, "The
Child's Message;" Rhoda Fulmer, a recitation,
"Mattie's Troubles;" Carrie Howard, a recita-
tion, "My Beau With the Corduroy Pants;"
Bangie Johnson, "The Wallflower;" Alice
Jacobson, a song, "Waiting for Pa;" Eunice
Jacobson, a song, "Who Will Wear my Stove-
pipe Hat?" in character, her sister, aged six
years, playing the accompaniment; Emma
Brown, "Nay, Speak no Ill;" Joseph Thorp
and company, a song, "Lady, Awake;" Georgie
Jensen, "The Washing Day;" Sophia Jensen, a 
song, "You've Been a Friend to Me;" "The
Jasper Sea" was sung by the Primary choir.
accompanied by Maud Bowen.  We were
greatly assisted by our Primary organist, Sister
Hannah Jacobson.  Ettie Smith, a song, "Ten
Thousand Miles Away;" Phoebe Downs, Cather-
ine Arnold and Ellie Ellis each a recitation;
Christine Fjeldsted, Jelina and Ada Ostlund
each a recitation, Maggie Johnson, a recitation,
"The Angels;" Adeline Jeppeson, "Thorns and 
Roses;" Sarah Larsen, a recitation; Dagmar
Christiansen, "The Prophet's Prayer;" Amelia
Christiansen, a song, "The Sailor's Song." The
evening's entertainment was concluded by
"The Sewing Bee," from one of the Primary
Books.
	Our Primary Stake President, Jane E.
Molen, and her husband were present, and we 
were sustained by our Bishop, Bro. Turner,
his Counselors, the ward generally, and quite
a number of visitors.
					EMMA PIKE, Prest.,
					MILLIE PIKE, Sec.


NOTES ABOUT WOMEN. 

	Mrs. Mary Howitt has just passed her eighty-
ninth birthday, and is in excellent health. 
	Mrs. Senator Sabin, of Minnesota, having no
children of her own, has adopted a family of
nine. 
	Mrs. Florence Howe Hall, a daughter of
Mrs. Julia Ward Howe, has just completed a
manual of American etiquette, entitled, "Social
Customs." 
	Mlle. Marie Deraismes is said to be the
only woman freemason in France. She is a
good speaker, a terse writer, and a strong advo-
cate of suffrage for women. 
	Miss N. B. Cummings, now the librarian of
the Department of Justice, at Washington, is
a daughter of a former justice of the Supreme
Court of Pennsylvania, and is regarded as one
of the best authorities on law books in the
country. She is literally an encyclopaedia of
reports, rendering invaluable aid to the lawyers
who use the extensive library of that depart-
ment.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


THE BURIED FLOWER. 

In the silence of my chamber, 
	When the night is still a deep, 
And the drowsy heave of ocean 
	Mutters in its charmed sleep; 

Oft I hear the angel voices
	That have thrilled me long ago, - 
Voices of my lost companions, 
	Lying deep beneath the snow. 

Where are now the flowers we tended? 
	Withered, broken, branch and stem; 
Where are now the hopes we cherished? 
	Scattered to the winds with them. 

For ye, too, were flowers, ye dear ones! 
	Nursed in hope and raised in love, 
Looking fondly ever upward
	To the clear blue sky above; 

Smiling on the sun that cheered us, 
	Rising lightly from the rain, 
Never folding up your freshness
	Save to give it forth again: 

Never shaken save by accents
	From a tongue that was not free, 
As the modest blossom trembles
	At the wooing of the bee. 

O! 'tis sad to lie and reckon 
	All the days of faded youth, 
All the vows that we believed in, 
	All the words we spoke in truth. 

Sever'd—were it sever'd only 
	By an idle thought of strife, 
Such as time may knit together;
	Not the broken cord of life! 

O my heart! that once so truly
	Kept another's time and tune,—
Heart, that kindled in the morning 
	Look around thee in the noon! 

Where are they who gave the impulse
	To thy earliest thought and flow? 
Look across the ruined garden—
	All are withered, dropped, or low!

O! I fling my spirit backward, 
	And I pass o'er years of pain;
All I loved is rising round me, 
	All the lost returns again.

Brighter, fairer far than living, 
	With no trace of woe or pain, 
Robed in everlasting beauty, 
	Shall I see thee once again, 

By the light that never fadeth, 
	Underneath eternal skies, 
When the dawn of resurrection
	Breaks o'er deathless Paradise? 
								SELECTED.


WELCOME TO PRISONERS FOR
		CONSCIENCE’ SAKE.

EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	Under the auspices of the Relief Society of
this place, a party was tendered to the brethren
lately arrived at their homes from the prisons
in Boise and Detroit.
	Large tables were spread, loaded with the
good things of this earth, and nearly all the
people of the ward were gathered, and partook
in the festivities as a token of love and respect
for the integrity shown by the persecuted
brethren, who would rather endure hardships
in prison than yield to any unjust measure or
interpretation of congressional enactments
binding conscience and demanding the abandon-
ment of a law and order revealed from heaven.
The brethren are enjoying their liberty in full
measure.


After dinner was over, the meeting house 
was cleared. A programme had been prepared
by and under the direction of the Y. M. M. I. 
A. and speeches by the late prisoners, inter-
spersed with music, were given. Good ad-
vice and cheering remarks were made by the
speakers. In the evening the people enjoyed
themselves in dancing, singing and music, until 
after 11 o'clock, when all went home well satis-
fied with their day. 
	Next day all the children of the ward were
similarly treated. It was a great sight to see
106 small children from four to twelve, and
some a little older, mingle in the dancing, sing-
ing, recitations and other amusements. 
	Bro. M. F. Cowley, President of the Y. M. 
M. I. A. in this Stake, was present on the occa-
sion, and with his cheerful voice and wise
counsel helped to highten the glee and merri-
ment, as well as to give substantial good coun-
sel and advice.
	The EXPONENT is ever welcome, and its con-
tents are carefully preserved. 
	Always praying for the welfare of Zion and
her inhabitants, and especially praying for the
exiled and imprisoned for conscience' sake, I 
am, as ever, 
			Yours obediently, 
					INGRID GRAHAM.


CIRCULAR.

To the Presidents of the Primary Associations
in the Oneida Stake of Zion:

DEAR SISTERS:
	Not having had the opportunity of visiting
all the Primary Associations in the Oneida
Stake through the past winter, we feel it our
duty to address a few lines to you on the sub-
ject of Primary advancement.
	The mission to which we, as officers of the
Associations, have been called is a most impor-
tant one, and should be accepted as a labor of
honor and love.
	The training and educating of the future
men and women of Zion has been committed to
our care, and this great responsibility,especially
in such an eventful day as this, demands our
most careful and considerate attention, for the
children who now compose the Primary Asso-
ciations will, in a few short years, be the repre-
sentatives of the greatest cause on earth, and
the reason of success or failure, in the battle of
life, will be followed back to the moulding of
the tender mind by the influence brought to
bear by those whose duty it was to teach and
train them in their youth.
	Presidents should, therefore, study the situa-
tion, and make their mission a reality and not
a mere name.
	Our duty does not consist in alone conducting
the meetings of the children, and then throw-
ing off all interest as to them elsewhere, but
we should seek to establish an influence among
them that will draw them to us in all our asso-
ciations together.
	Our example before them should be such as
we would be proud to have them follow, and
we should consider every act of ours a pattern
for them.
	Life and variety should be put into our
meetings, that they may be interesting and
profitable; and in order that a desire to read
and inform the mind may be created while
young, we suggest the advisability of collect-
ing a few good books, such as the "Faith Pro-
moting Series," "Juvenile Instructor" and
others of like character.
	We are very desirous that every Primary As-
sociation in the Stake should have a library of its
own, which may be obtained by a perpetual
offering of about five cents per month from
each member of the Association.
	Dear sisters, that we may know the condi-
tion of each Primary, and be enabled to make


out an accurate report for our quarterly con-
ferences, it is very necessary that we receive a
report from every ward, so please do not fail to
make out your reports as soon as possible after
receiving them, and send them in to Sister
Elizabeth Hale immediately.
	Our earnest endeavors to magnify our call-
ing will be acceptable unto the Lord, and will
most assuredly be fraught with great success.
	Praying the Lord to assist us in training the
youth of Zion, we remain,
	Your sisters in the Gospel of Christ,
		LUCY M. PARKINSON,
		ELVIRA PETTY,
	Of the Presidency of the Primary Associa-
tion of the Oneida Stake.


HONOR TO WHOM HONOR IS DUE.

	A pleasant and enjoyable surprise was given 
to the Secretary of the Relief Society of Loa,
Piute Co., Sister Sarah Jane Blackburn, on
her fifty-sixth birthday, Tuesday, March 22nd,
1887, by the Relief Society and Young Ladies'
Association of Loa. About fifty persons were
present—our worthy Bishop, E. H. Blackburn,
and sons and grandchildren and friends of
long standing, who have stood the trials and
hardships of this people, and are willing to
stand more for the Gospel's sake and their own.
	At 2 o'clock the tables were loaded with
the good things of this earth, which we humbly
thanked our Heavenly Father for. Sister
Sarah was the recipient of several handsome
presents from her husband and sisters of the
Relief Society. Sister Sarah thanked the
brethren and sisters, and said it was a complete
surprise to her. Expressed her appreciation
and gratitude for their honor to her.
	There was a good spirit, and all returned
home feeling well and benefited by meeting
together to honor one so worthy, noble and
faithful.
		JULIETTE BLACKBURN.
PRIMARY PICNIC.
	A Primary picnic was held at Loa on the 25th
of March, President and Counselors present.
Met at 10 a.m.; had recitations, songs and
dialogues. A good spirit dwelt in the hearts
of the little folks. Our worthy Bishop was
present, whom we were pleased to have in our
midst. The children came with their bright,
sunny faces. Their President is always pleased
to meet with them. We then adjourned our
meeting until 2 p.m, during which time the
children played games. We were called to-
gether again, and the little girls set the tables;
there were numbered sixty children. After
dinner we all had an enjoyable time in con-
versation. Dismissed with prayer.
	All went home feeling benefited by meeting
with the children and trying to entertain and
instruct the youth of Zion in the ways of the
Lord.
		JULIETTE BLACKBURN, Prest.


Mrs. Logan gives a striking instance of the
discrimination made in the pay of government
clerks on account of sex. She says: "No lady
is allowed the same salary in the higher grade
of clerkships, no  matter how well she fills the
higher salaried desks, and many of them are 
to-day in those positions, but do not receive
the pay. As an illustration, it has happened
that in cases of illness or disability of men
clerks, their wives performed the duty of their
husbands, drawing in their name the salary as
if they had performed the work. In one case
the wife kept up her husband's desk for three
years, during his illness, receiving the $1,800
per annum, and thereby supporting the family;
but from the day of his death, though she con-
tinued the same work, her salary was nine
hundred!"
